sh: The divine on the mountain that cannot be reached — the crown that is forever 'up there' and never 'in here'
ar: The Mountain God / The Supreme Creator of East Africa
cb: Waweru (the wind-being, the spirit that Ngai sends to communicate with humans — the intermediary) counterbalances Ngai (the supreme who is too high to approach directly). The accessible divine counterbalanced by the inaccessible supreme.

do: The dual lord, the self-created divine beyond all attributes, the thirteenth heaven
at: Ometecuhtli/Omecihuatl ('Two-Lord/Two-Lady') is the supreme divine pair of Aztec cosmology — the self-created androgynous divine who lives in the thirteenth heaven (Omeyocan). They are so beyond ordinary conception that they had no temple and received no sacrifice. The supreme beyond all devotion because it is beyond all concept.
of: None — they are beyond all devotion in the ordinary sense. The supreme acknowledgment of the beyond-conceptual divine.
an: None — they transcend all animal correspondence
co: The colour of the beyond — black and white together, which is all colours and no colour
dp: Sunday — the day beyond all days
cr: Clear quartz (transparent, beyond colour), obsidian (the mirror that reflects all things)
bp: The crown and beyond the crown
af: I acknowledge the divine that is beyond my understanding. I am a spark of something infinite and androgynous.
sh: The duality that becomes division — the supreme androgynous pair whose children created a world of conflict rather than the original harmony
ar: The Supreme Duality / The Beyond-All-Concept Divine
cb: The entire Aztec pantheon (all the named gods) counterbalances Ometecuhtli/Omecihuatl (the supreme beyond all names and forms). The manifest counterbalanced by the unmanifest.

do: The Hero Twins — the overcoming of death, the resurrection of the corn god, the sun and moon
at: The Hero Twins of the Popol Vuh — the greatest myth of the Maya. Born from the spittle of their father (the decapitated corn god), they descended to Xibalba (the Mayan underworld), defeated the Lords of Death through trickery and cunning, were killed and resurrected, and eventually became the sun and moon.
of: Corn offerings (they resurrected the corn god), rubber ball (the ball game was sacred to them), cacao, the Popol Vuh read as ceremony
an: Jaguar (his spots are the stars of the night sky), rabbit, deer
co: Black (Xbalanque — the night sun, the moon) and white/gold (Hunahpu — the day sun)
dp: Sunday and Monday — sun and moon together
cr: Obsidian (the night twin), clear quartz (the day twin), jade (the corn god their father)
el: Earth / Fire / Cosmic Consciousness — they span all levels
bp: The entire body — their bodies were sacrificed and resurrected
af: I descend into my underworld and I rise. Death does not defeat me. I play the game of life with joy and cunning.
sh: The overconfidence before the descent — the Hero Twins' first defeat came before their ultimate victory
ar: The Descent and Resurrection / The Eternal Game
cb: The Lords of Xibalba (the Lords of Death) counterbalance the Hero Twins (the triumph of life). The supreme Mayan polarity of life and death.

do: The light of God in the earth and mind, prophetic vision, the fire of divine understanding
at: Uriel means 'Light of God' — the archangel associated with the north, the earth element, and the fire of divine understanding. He is said to have given humanity the Kabbalah. He stands at the gates of Eden with a fiery sword that is also a torch of illumination. He is the divine intelligence within the earth itself.
of: Deep red and orange candles, offerings on the earth, prayers for wisdom and grounding, the study of sacred texts
an: Eagle (the far-seeing bird), bull (the earth's solidity beneath the divine light)
co: Deep indigo, the golden fire of divine understanding, earth reds
dp: Wednesday / Saturn
cr: Lapis lazuli, blue sapphire, indigo kyanite, amber
bp: Third eye, pineal gland, the root chakra (the light of God in the earth)
af: The light of God illumines my mind. I understand what was obscure. The fire of divine wisdom burns in my forehead.
sh: The light that blinds rather than illuminates — the divine understanding that becomes blinding certainty
ar: The Divine Understanding / The Third Eye Fire
cb: Archangel Gabriel (water, the moon, the receptive message-receiver) counterbalances Archangel Uriel (fire, the active light of divine understanding). The receptive moon counterbalanced by the projective fire.

do: The love of God, finding what is lost, compassionate love, the heart ray
at: Archangel Chamuel ('He who sees God') is the archangel of unconditional love, compassion and finding what has been lost — including lost love, lost people, and lost objects. He governs the heart chakra. His pink flame is the divine love that holds the universe together.
of: Pink candles, rose quartz offerings, prayers for healing of relationships and the heart, pink flowers
an: Dove, the pink flamingo (his colour)
co: Deep rose pink (the pink ray of divine love)
dp: Friday / Venus — the love ray
cr: Rose quartz, rhodonite, pink tourmaline, pink kunzite
el: Air / Fire (the pink flame of divine love)
bp: Heart, thymus gland, the lungs (breath of love), the arms (the embrace)
af: I am held in the unconditional love of the universe. My heart is a channel for divine love. I find what was lost through love.
sh: The searching that never rests — the seeker who cannot stop looking, who cannot receive what they are seeking
ar: The Love Seeker / The Finder of Lost Hearts
cb: Archangel Michael (the warrior, the sword, the protector through force) counterbalances Chamuel (the love angel, the finder through gentleness). The sword counterbalanced by the rose.

do: The thrice-greatest — Hermes, Thoth and Mercury merged, the source of Hermetic philosophy
at: Hermes Trismegistus ('Thrice-Greatest Hermes') is the legendary figure behind the entire Hermetic tradition — the synthesis of Greek Hermes, Egyptian Thoth and the universal principle of divine intelligence. 'As above, so below.' He is the principle of the word as cosmic law, the communication that creates reality. The Emerald Tablet is his teaching.
of: Mercury/quicksilver imagery, caduceus, the study of the Hermetic texts, the contemplation of 'as above so below'
an: Caduceus serpents, ibis, ram
co: Mercury-silver, gold, the colour of quicksilver
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the Hermetic planet, the day of Hermes-Thoth
cr: Labradorite (the trickster's rainbow), clear quartz (the Hermetic clarity)
bp: Throat (the word as law), third eye (the Hermetic vision), crown (the 'as above')
af: As above, so below. As within, so without. As the universe, so the soul. I am the meeting point of these correspondences.
sh: The Hermetic shadow is the over-complexity of esoteric systems — the labyrinth of correspondences that becomes more important than the living truth it maps
ar: The Thrice-Greatest / The Hermetic Intelligence
cb: Nature itself (the raw, living, unmediated reality) counterbalances Hermes Trismegistus (the system of correspondences that describes nature). The map is not the territory.

do: Foxes, rice, fertility, industry, worldly success, swords, tea, sake
at: One of the most widely worshipped Shinto kami — Inari is the divine principle of rice (and by extension, all abundance and prosperity). Deliberately androgynous — depicted as a young woman, an old man, or an androgynous bodhisattva. Inari's messengers are foxes (kitsune). Over 30,000 Inari shrines exist in Japan — more than any other deity.
of: Fried tofu (aburaage — foxes love it), rice, sake, tea, offerings at the shrine with red torii gates, white fox imagery
an: Fox (the kitsune are Inari's messengers and embodiments)
co: Red and white (the shrine colours), gold (the rice), red (the torii gates)
dp: Friday / Venus and Saturday / Jupiter
cr: Citrine (abundance), rice crystal, fox-orange carnelian
el: Earth / Water / Fire — all elements of the rice cycle
bp: Heart (the shared abundance), sacral (the fertility of rice), root (the earth from which rice grows)
af: The community is fed and the heart is open. My abundance is shared and multiplied. The fox brings what I need to my door.
sh: The fox's shadow — the kitsune trickster who appears as a beautiful woman and leads people astray. Abundance that becomes the lure.
ar: The Sacred Abundance / The Fox's Gift
cb: Raijin (the storm that can destroy the rice harvest) counterbalances Inari (the fertility that nurtures it).

do: The supreme creator, the one who retreated to the sky, the distant divine
at: The supreme deity of the Lozi people of Zambia — Nyambe created all things and originally lived among humans on earth, but humanity's disrespect (particularly a man named Kamunu who continually killed the animals Nyambe tried to protect) drove Nyambe to retreat to the sky, beyond human reach.
of: Prayers of acknowledgment, honourable behaviour toward all living things, ceremonies honoring the sky
an: Spider (Nyambe ascended to the sky on a spider web), eagle, any creature that Kamunu/humanity once hunted without respect
co: White (the sky, the divine distance), gold (the sun)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: Clear quartz, white calcite, meteorite (from the sky)
bp: Crown, the entire being, the breath as Nyambe's gift that can be withdrawn
af: I treat all living things with respect. The divine withdrew because we forgot. I remember. My reverence calls the divine closer.
sh: Kamunu — the human who drives the divine away through disrespect for creation
ar: The Retreated Creator / The Divine Driven to the Sky
cb: Kamunu (the disrespectful human — the figure of human transgression that drove Nyambe away) counterbalances Nyambe (the respectful divine). Reverence and disrespect as the axis of the cosmos.

do: Death, purity, the grave, chastity, the bones of the dead
at: The Orisha of the grave and purity — she governs death, the bones of the dead, and the most strict female purity. She lives inside the grave itself. Even Oya must ask her permission to work with the dead.
of: White and pink offerings, pristine white cloth, silence — she cannot bear noise
an: None — she is the silence of the grave
co: Pink and white (purity and death)
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the grave
cr: Pink rose quartz, white calcite
bp: Bones, the entire body returned to the earth
af: I rest in sacred purity. The grave is not the end — it is the deepest rest. I honour what lies beneath.
sh: The grave that refuses to release — the death that becomes imprisonment rather than transition
ar: The Grave's Purity / The Oldest Death
cb: Oshun (life, sweetness, the river) counterbalances Yewa (death, purity, the grave). The sweet living waters counterbalanced by the silent grave.

do: The oldest deity, the primordial swamp, the grandmother of all gods
at: Nana Buruku is the oldest deity in the Yoruba-Fon pantheon — older than Oduduwa, older than all Orishas. She is the primordial swamp, the mud and water from which life first emerged. She governs the oldest medicines (those made without iron — iron was not yet invented when her medicine was created).
of: Brooms (made without iron), white and purple offerings, the oldest herbs, ceremonies in swamps or still water
an: Crocodile (the swamp creature), the oldest serpent
co: White and deep purple (the oldest and most sacred)
dp: Saturday / Moon — the primordial night
cr: White quartz, dark amethyst, ancient clay
bp: The bones (oldest structures), the skin, the womb (the original swamp of life)
af: I am older than all the gods know. The primordial mud in me holds the first medicine. I am the grandmother of all.
sh: The ancient that cannot adapt — the oldest wisdom that becomes rigid because it was here before everything else
ar: The Primordial Grandmother / The First Swamp
cb: Oduduwa (the divine creator of earth and civilisation) counterbalances Nana Buruku (the primordial swamp before earth had form). The ordered creation counterbalanced by the formless primordial.

do: Smallpox, disease, healing, the earth's surface, transformation through illness
at: The Orisha of smallpox and infectious disease — the most feared of the Orishas. His body is covered in sores; he wears a straw mask (filá). He both sends and heals disease. No other Orisha's name should be spoken directly — he is called 'Omolu' or 'Obaluaiye.' He governs the skin, the earth's surface.
of: Popcorn (his sacred food — the popping corn represents the pustules of disease transformed), cowrie shells, straw, black and white offerings
an: Lizard, tortoise (his slow sacred animal)
co: Black and white, red and black — the colours of disease and the earth
dp: Monday — the day of his ceremonies
cr: Black tourmaline, cowrie shells, red and black jasper
bp: Skin (smallpox is the skin's disease), the immune system, the lymphatic system
af: I transform disease into knowledge. The earth's surface holds both sickness and medicine. I am healed through what afflicted me.
sh: The disease unleashed without mercy — the Orisha whose power becomes plague when disrespected
ar: The Disease That Heals / The Pox Deity
cb: Ogun (iron — Obaluaiye's medicines cannot use iron; iron is his opposite) counterbalances Obaluaiye (the oldest medicine made without iron). The forge counterbalanced by the swamp herb.

do: The rainbow serpent, cycles, wealth, movement, the connecting principle between heaven and earth
at: The rainbow serpent Orisha — the rainbow is the bridge between heaven and earth. Oshumare is androgynous, alternating between male and female according to the time of year. He/she governs movement, cycles, the serpent's wisdom and the rainbow's promise. He/she is the Yoruba cosmic serpent.
of: Gold and silver offerings, rainbow imagery, offerings of movement (dance offered to Oshumare), the serpent honoured
an: Serpent (the rainbow serpent), the python, all serpents
co: All the colours of the rainbow, silver and gold
dp: Monday and Thursday — the bridge between days
cr: Rainbow moonstone, labradorite, all iridescent stones
el: Water / Air — the rainbow is both
bp: The entire spine (the serpent path), the sacral (the cycle of energy), the crown (the rainbow's upper arc)
af: I am the bridge between worlds. My cycles are sacred. The rainbow of my being connects heaven and earth.
sh: The serpent that swallows its own tail — the cycle that becomes a closed loop rather than an ascending spiral
ar: The Rainbow Bridge / The Androgynous Cycle
cb: Ogun (iron, the straight line, the road cleared without curves) counterbalances Oshumare (the serpent's curve, the rainbow's arc). The straight line counterbalanced by the sacred curve.

do: Marriage, the Oba River, the first wife of Shango, the wound of love betrayed
at: The Orisha of the Oba River and the first legitimate wife of Shango. She was tricked by Oshun into cutting off her ear and putting it in Shango's food as a love potion. When Shango discovered the deception he banished her. She is the Orisha of the wound of love — the wife who loved absolutely and was betrayed.
of: Purple and brown offerings, offerings at the junction of two rivers, prayers for healing of love wounds
an: Turtle, the cow
co: Purple and brown (the colours of her grief)
dp: Saturday — the day of her ceremonies
cr: Amethyst (the wounded heart), brown jasper
bp: The heart (the wound of love), the ear (the ear she cut off), the womb
af: I am not diminished by what I lost. My love was real and it was sacred. I am healed of the wound of betrayal.
sh: The wound that never heals — Oba's shadow is the wife who remains forever in the moment of betrayal
ar: The Wounded Wife / The River of Grief
cb: Oshun (who tricked her — the rival wife) counterbalances Oba (the older, legitimate, wounded wife). The bitter polarity of two women who loved the same king.

do: Wealth, material abundance, the spiritual force of money
at: Aje is the Orisha of wealth — literally the spirit of money and material abundance. She attaches herself to individuals and groups. When Aje favours you, wealth follows. She is the Yoruba principle that abundance is a divine force with its own spiritual intelligence.
of: Money blessed and offered, white and gold candles, cowrie shells (the original Yoruba currency), prayers of gratitude for abundance
an: None — she is the force of abundance itself
co: White and gold
dp: Friday — the day of wealth and Venus
cr: Gold, pyrite, citrine, gold tiger's eye
bp: Hands (holding wealth), the solar plexus (personal power to attract abundance), root
af: Abundance is a divine force and it flows to me. My hands are full of Aje's blessing. Material wealth is sacred.
sh: The greed that drives Aje away — she leaves where money is hoarded and not shared
ar: The Spirit of Wealth / The Force of Abundance
cb: Ogun (iron, labour, the work that earns) counterbalances Aje (the spirit of wealth itself). The spiritual abundance counterbalanced by the physical labour.

do: Personal destiny, the head spirit, the most important spiritual relationship in Yoruba cosmology
at: Ori literally means 'head' — in Yoruba cosmology, Ori is the personal divine spirit that lives within the head of every person and determines their destiny. Ori is considered more important than any Orisha — it is Ori who chose your destiny before you were born, and no Orisha can help you if your Ori is not in alignment.
of: The head washed and anointed with sacred substances, the Ori propitiation ceremony (itori), prayers spoken into the palm of the hand
an: None — Ori is the personal spirit
co: White (the colour of the highest spiritual purity)
dp: Sunday — the day of the personal divine
cr: White quartz, clear quartz, selenite
bp: The head (crown of the head is the seat of Ori), the hands (Ori speaks through the palms)
af: My Ori is awake and aligned. I walk in my destiny with full consciousness. The spirit in my head leads me to my highest purpose.
sh: The Ori that is blocked — when the personal divine is out of alignment, no Orisha can help; the deepest spiritual emergency in Yoruba tradition
ar: The Personal Crown / The Head That Knows
cb: Olodumare (the supreme universal divine) counterbalances Ori (the supremely personal divine). The universal counterbalanced by the particular. The cosmic plan counterbalanced by the individual destiny chosen before birth.

do: The desert, the wilderness, volcanoes, the father of Shango (in some traditions)
at: The Orisha of the wilderness, the desert, and volcanic fire — the wild, uncultivated earth beyond human habitation. He governs the space between cities — the dangerous journey through the wilderness. Like Shango, he has tremendous power, but it is the power of the uncontrolled natural world.
of: Rum and tobacco, red and brown cloth, the wilderness itself as altar, offerings left at the edge of cultivated land
an: Volcanic rock, the lion
co: Red and yellow (volcanic fire), brown (desert earth)
dp: Tuesday / Mars and Saturn
cr: Lava stone, red jasper, fire opal
bp: Solar plexus (the volcanic fire), adrenal glands (wilderness survival), legs (for crossing the desert)
af: I cross my wilderness with the fire of Aganju within me. No desert can stop me. The wild earth is my ally.
sh: The wilderness that swallows — the desert that takes the traveller and gives nothing back
ar: The Wild Earth / The Volcanic Father
cb: Shango (his son — the civilised, royal expression of volcanic fire) counterbalances Aganju (the wild, uncultivated volcanic power). The king counterbalanced by the wilderness.

do: The divine androgyne, the forest and the river, youth and beauty, hunting and love
at: The son of Oshosi (the hunter) and Oshun (the river goddess) — he spends six months of the year as a hunter in the forest (masculine) and six months in the river with his mother (feminine). He is the Orisha of divine androgyny, the perfect balance of masculine and feminine. He is the most beautiful of all the Orishas.
of: Peacock feathers, fish and honey (combining his parents' sacred foods), turquoise and gold
an: Peacock (his sacred bird — the most beautiful), fish (from Oshun), hawk (from Oshosi)
co: Turquoise and gold (combining Oshun's gold and the forest's green-blue)
dp: Monday and Thursday — alternating between his parents' sacred days
cr: Turquoise, amber, blue topaz
el: Water / Earth — the river and the forest
bp: The sacral plexus (the meeting point of masculine and feminine), the entire body in its most beautiful expression
af: I contain both the forest and the river. My masculine and feminine are in perfect balance. I am the divine androgyne.
sh: The eternal adolescent — the one who can never fully commit to either the masculine or the feminine
ar: The Divine Androgyne / The Forest-River Child
cb: Oshun (his mother — the river, the feminine) and Oshosi (his father — the forest, the masculine) are his two counterbalancing forces. He is the living resolution of their polarity.

do: Personal achievement, strength, the right hand, masculine ambition, the force of individual will
at: Ikenga ('power of the right hand') is the Igbo deity of personal achievement and masculine ambition. Represented by a carved wooden figure with prominent horns. Every Igbo man traditionally had his own Ikenga — a personal sacred object representing his individual will and capacity for achievement.
of: Kola nut, palm wine, the blood of a cockerel (traditionally), chalk (nzu), prayers of dedication to one's work and ambition
an: Ram (his horns represent determination), cock
co: White and red (achievement and sacred power)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the warrior's ambition
cr: Red jasper (the power of achievement), carnelian (the will), hematite (iron will)
bp: Right hand (Ikenga = the power of the right hand), solar plexus (the personal will), arms
af: My right hand is blessed with the power of Ikenga. My will achieves what my vision sees. I am the force of my own becoming.
sh: The ambition without wisdom — Ikenga that accumulates achievement for its own sake, without service to the community
ar: The Personal Achievement / The Right Hand of Will
cb: Chi (the personal divine destiny which may conflict with Ikenga's ambition) counterbalances Ikenga (the personal ambition which must align with Chi to be truly powerful). The inner divine flame counterbalanced by the outward achieving force.

do: Trickery, bargaining, the marketplace, deception, the testing of human character
at: Ekwensu is the Igbo trickster spirit — a force of cunning, deception and bargaining. He is not pure evil but is associated with sharp dealing in the marketplace and with testing humans through adversity. Without Ekwensu's testing, human virtue cannot be proven. He was later misidentified as the devil by Christian missionaries.
of: Pepper (his sacred spice — sharp and testing), crossroads offerings, the acknowledgment of trickery in oneself and the world
an: The jackal (trickster animal), the chameleon (the one who changes)
co: Red and black (the colours of challenge and the marketplace)
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the trickster's planet
cr: Red jasper, black tourmaline, obsidian
bp: The tongue (organ of bargaining and trickery), solar plexus (tested through challenge), feet (the crossroads walker)
af: I navigate the marketplace of life with wisdom. I am not deceived and I do not deceive. I am tested and I pass.
sh: The pure trickster — the deception that has no limit, the bargain that is always unfair
ar: The Market Trickster / The Tester of Character
cb: Chi (the personal divine flame — which Ekwensu cannot extinguish) counterbalances Ekwensu (the trickster who tests). The sacred divine spark counterbalanced by the force that tests whether the spark is real.

do: Divination, madness, healing, the double-edged gift of spiritual sight
at: Agwu Nsi is the Igbo spirit of divination, medicine and the fine line between genius and madness. Diviners (dibia) are chosen by Agwu Nsi — often against their will. The dibia goes through illness and apparent madness before emerging as a healer. Agwu Nsi represents the dangerous gift of seeing what others cannot see.
of: The initiation of the dibia, sacred plants and medicines, the divination tools, prayers asking Agwu to grant insight without madness
an: The crow/raven (the madness bird), the spotted animal, the tortoise
co: Mixed colours — Agwu Nsi cannot be reduced to a single colour; he is contradiction
dp: Saturday / Mercury — the shaman's planet
cr: Labradorite (the iridescent dual-nature stone), obsidian (the dark sight), clear quartz (clarity from madness)
bp: Third eye (the vision), the brain (the line between genius and madness), the entire nervous system
af: I accept the gift of sight without being consumed by it. My clarity comes from the fire I have walked through. I see and I remain grounded.
sh: The madness without the healing — Agwu Nsi's shadow is the person who is given the sight but cannot find the way back to sanity
ar: The Shaman's Double Edge / The Gift of Sight
cb: Chi (the personal divine flame that grounds the seer) counterbalances Agwu Nsi (the overwhelming sight that can unground the seer). The personal anchor counterbalanced by the cosmic vision.

do: The sun, light, the all-seeing eye, the solar force of the Igbo
at: Anyanwu ('Eye of the Sun') is the Igbo sun deity — the solar force that brings light, warmth, and life. He is the divine eye that sees all things. One of the faces through which Chukwu (the supreme) manifests. He governs personal power, vision, and the capacity to see clearly.
of: Solar ceremonies at dawn, yellow and gold offerings, the morning greeting to the sun, corn offered at sunrise
an: Eagle (the sun's bird), the hawk
co: Gold, brilliant yellow, the white of midday sun
dp: Sunday / Sun
cr: Sunstone, citrine, yellow sapphire, tiger's eye
bp: Solar plexus, eyes (Anyanwu = Eye of the Sun), the entire vital force
af: I see clearly in the light of the sun. My inner sun is as bright as the one above. I am illuminated.
sh: The blinding midday sun — the clarity that becomes too intense, that scorches rather than illuminates
ar: The Solar Eye / The Igbo Sun Force
cb: Ala (the earth, the receiving of the sun's heat) counterbalances Anyanwu (the sky, the giving of heat). Also Chukwu (the supreme behind the sun) counterbalances Anyanwu (the manifest solar expression).

do: The forest, hunting, the wild places, masculine initiation
at: Ogbu-Ike is the Igbo forest spirit — the divine force governing the wilderness, the hunt, and the initiation of young men into adulthood. The Igbo initiation ceremonies that take young men into the forest are under his governance. He is the wild masculine principle, the counterpart of Ala.
of: Game meat, offerings left in the forest, the ceremonies of male initiation
an: Leopard (the forest king), python (the forest power)
co: Green, the brown of the forest floor
dp: Saturday / Earth
cr: Brown tourmaline, forest jasper, dark green serpentine
bp: Legs (for running in the forest), the body in its initiation trials, the root chakra
af: I walk with dignity in the wild places. My initiation has made me. The forest has taught me what no city can.
sh: The uninitiated masculine — the man who never went into the forest, never faced the testing
ar: The Forest Initiator / The Wild Masculine
cb: Ala (the cultivated earth, the feminine, the village) counterbalances Ogbu-Ike (the wild forest, the masculine, the wilderness beyond the village).

do: The sacred threshold between the living and dead, the divine ocean, the great boundary
at: Kalunga is one of the most profound concepts in Kongo and Palo Mayombe cosmology — it is simultaneously the line between the world of the living and the world of the dead, the sacred ocean at the bottom of the universe, and the name of God (death itself as divine force). Everything passes through Kalunga.
of: Water (especially ocean water), the cosmogram drawn with mpemba (white chalk) and pemba (red chalk), prayers at the threshold of death
an: Serpent (the line between worlds), the crocodile (the boundary keeper)
co: Black and white (the living and dead worlds), blue (the divine ocean)
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the boundary of death
cr: Black and white stones held together, dark lapis, white calcite
el: Water / Earth — the divine ocean at the base of the world
bp: The entire body between its two states, the root (the lower world connection), the crown (the upper world connection)
af: I live fully in the world of the living. I honour the world of the dead. The Kalunga line within me is clear and sacred.
sh: The blurred Kalunga — the person who lives too close to the dead world and cannot fully inhabit the living one
ar: The Sacred Threshold / The Divine Ocean
cb: Nzambi (the supreme above all thresholds) counterbalances Kalunga (the threshold itself). The source counterbalanced by the boundary.

do: Iron, war, the forge, clearing the path, machetes and swords, justice
at: Zarabanda is the Palo Mayombe equivalent of Ogun — the Congo force of iron, war, labour and the clearing of all obstacles. He governs all cutting tools: machetes, knives, surgical instruments, weapons. He demands absolute integrity and absolute commitment.
of: Iron objects, rum poured on iron, green and black cloth, cigars, railroad iron (his sacred object in Cuba where much Palo practice developed)
an: Dog (like Ogun), serpent
co: Green and black (his sacred colours)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the warrior's forge
cr: Iron meteorite, black tourmaline, hematite, magnetite
bp: Muscles, bones, the skeletal-muscular system, the hands that work iron
af: I clear the path with the force of sacred iron. My discipline is absolute. I work with the divine machete.
sh: The iron that turns against its maker — Zarabanda's shadow is the warrior who destroys without purpose
ar: The Congo Iron Warrior / The Sacred Machete
cb: Lucero (the trickster, the crossroads, the indirect path) counterbalances Zarabanda (iron, the direct force, the path cleared by machete).

do: The crossroads, communication, the first force in all ceremonies, the divine trickster
at: Lucero (also called Alúwansa) is the Palo Mayombe equivalent of Eshu/Elegua — the first force invoked in all ceremonies, the keeper of the crossroads, the divine trickster who governs all communication between the human and divine worlds. Nothing in Palo Mayombe can proceed without Lucero's permission.
of: Rum, cigars, candy, crossroads offerings on Monday mornings, red and black cloth, a small clay pot (his nganga) left at the crossroads
an: Rooster (his sacred bird), dog
co: Red and black (his sacred colours in Palo)
dp: Monday / Mercury — the gatekeeper of all paths
cr: Red jasper, black tourmaline, obsidian
bp: Feet (the crossroads walker), tongue (the trickster's speech), the root chakra base
af: I open the way with Lucero's blessing. Every path I take is cleared. The crossroads is my sacred space.
sh: The path that is blocked — Lucero's shadow is all roads closed, the trickster whose tests cannot be passed
ar: The First Gatekeeper / The Congo Crossroads
cb: Zarabanda (iron, the forge, the force of direct action) counterbalances Lucero (the crossroads, the trickster, the indirect path).

do: Sweet water, love, gold, the river, sensuality, abundance
at: Madre de Agua (Mother of Water) is the Congo/Palo name for the sweet water goddess — the equivalent of Oshun. She governs the sweet waters, love, gold and sensuality. She is the healing, nurturing face of the feminine divine in the Congo tradition.
of: Honey, orange blossom water, gold offerings, river ceremonies, sweet fruits, perfume
an: Peacock (her beauty), fish, bee
co: Yellow, gold, the colour of honey and rivers
dp: Friday / Venus — love, beauty, sweet water
cr: Amber, citrine, yellow topaz, gold
bp: Womb, kidneys, sacral plexus, the skin as beauty
af: The sweet waters of love flow through me. I am beautiful and I know it. Abundance finds me because I am grateful.
sh: The seduction that becomes the trap — the beauty that manipulates rather than nurtures
ar: The Congo Love Goddess / The Sweet Water Mother
cb: Zarabanda (iron, hardness, the masculine force) counterbalances Madre de Agua (water, softness, the feminine nurture). Iron and honey.

do: The creative force, the father of all, the supreme Mpungo, the earth that trembles
at: Tiembla Tierra ('Earth That Trembles') or Brazo Fuerte ('Strong Arm') is the Palo Mayombe equivalent of Obatala — the creator, the father, the white force of purity and cosmic power. He is the supreme Mpungo, the father of all Mpungos. He governs creation, purity and the cosmic order.
of: White cloth, white food (white rice, coconut, white yam), cool water, silver imagery — same as Obatala whom he mirrors
an: White dove, white snail
co: White (absolute white — the colour of the supreme in Palo)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, howlite, selenite
bp: Bones, the head (the seat of the divine crown), the entire body in its purest state
af: I create from the purest place within me. My white crown is clear and bright. I am the trembling of the sacred earth.
sh: The rigidity of the pure — the Mpungo whose whiteness becomes refusal of the necessary messiness of life
ar: The Congo Creator / The Father of All Mpungos
cb: Zarabanda (iron, the forge, the earthly masculine force) counterbalances Tiembla Tierra (white, the cosmic masculine principle). The earthly man of iron counterbalanced by the cosmic father of white light.

do: Lightning, storms, the cemetery, death, transformation, the wildest feminine force
at: Centella Ndoki is the Palo Mayombe equivalent of Oya — the Congo force of lightning, storms, cemeteries and the most dramatic transformation. She is considered the wildest and most dangerous of the female Mpungos. She governs the dead and the storm simultaneously.
of: Red wine, nine of everything, cemetery offerings, lightning water, burgundy and red cloth
an: Buffalo (like Oya), the storm bird
co: Red and burgundy (the colours of her lightning)
dp: Wednesday / Uranus — the lightning transformation
cr: Dark garnet, labradorite, obsidian
bp: Lungs (the storm breath), solar plexus (lightning power), adrenal glands
af: I am the lightning. I transform what I touch. The storm within me clears what must be cleared.
sh: The lightning without the rain — the transformation without the renewal, the storm that burns but brings no water
ar: The Congo Lightning / The Cemetery Storm
cb: Tiembla Tierra (the white, calm, creative father) counterbalances Centella Ndoki (the red, wild, transforming storm). The stillness counterbalanced by the lightning.

do: Thunder, lightning, royal power, justice, the seven rays of divine fire
at: Siete Rayos ('Seven Rays') is the Palo Mayombe equivalent of Shango — the Congo thunder deity of seven rays of divine lightning, justice, and royal power. He shares Shango's essence — the thunder king who commands the storm with absolute authority and whose justice cannot be bought.
of: Bata drumming, red and white cloth, apples, red wine, lightning water, the seven candles of the seven rays
an: Ram (like Shango), the horse
co: Red and white (his sacred colours, like Shango)
dp: Saturday / Jupiter — royal thunder
cr: Red garnet, meteorite (lightning stone), fire opal
bp: Solar plexus, adrenal glands, the spine of sovereignty
af: Seven rays of divine fire descend through me. My power is as just as thunder. I am the royal fire.
sh: The seven rays that scatter — the power that cannot focus, that strikes in seven directions without precision
ar: The Congo Thunder King / The Seven Divine Rays
cb: Tiembla Tierra (the white, still, creative father) counterbalances Siete Rayos (the red, dynamic, destructive thunder). Creation counterbalanced by transformation through fire.

do: Disease and healing, the earth's surface, the transformation through illness
at: Kobayende (also Tata Pansimba) is the Palo Mayombe equivalent of Obaluaiye — the Congo force of disease, healing, and the earth's surface. Like Obaluaiye, he is approached with great care and reverence, and his name is not spoken lightly.
of: Popcorn, straw, cowrie shells, ceremonies of healing and cleansing
an: Lizard (like Obaluaiye), the spotted animal
co: Black and white, red and black
dp: Monday — the day of cleansing
cr: Cowrie shells, black tourmaline, healing obsidian
bp: Skin, the immune system, the body's healing capacity
af: Disease carries its own medicine. I am healed through what afflicted me. The force within the sickness is the same force that cures it.
sh: The disease unleashed — the healing force that becomes its own opposite when disrespected
ar: The Congo Disease Healer / The Pox Force
cb: Zarabanda (iron, the force that cuts) counterbalances Kobayende (the healing herb, the force without iron). The machete and the medicine.

do: The moon, death, the feminine force of the dead, the cemetery's queen
at: Mariwanga is the Palo Mayombe deity of the moon and death — the feminine force that governs the cemetery and the dead. She is the Congo equivalent of Yewa — the most ancient, most respectful, most solemn of the female Mpungos. She works in the silence between the words of ceremony.
of: Moonwater, white and purple offerings, silent ceremony at the cemetery, the full moon ceremony
an: Owl (the moon's night bird), the bat
co: White and purple (the moon and death)
dp: Monday / Moon — the lunar death goddess
cr: Moonstone, white selenite, purple amethyst
bp: The bones (the dead's body), the entire body at the moment of death, the eyes closed in final rest
af: I honour the moon and the dead. The cemetery is a sacred place. I am at peace with what ends.
sh: The forever-mourning — the lunar death goddess whose shadow is the grief that cannot move into acceptance
ar: The Moon Queen of Death / The Congo Cemetery
cb: Siete Rayos (fire, thunder, the masculine solar force) counterbalances Mariwanga (moon, water, the feminine death force).

do: The Great Spirit, the supreme creative force, the most high
at: Gitche Manitou ('Great Spirit') is the supreme divine force of the Algonquian peoples — the Ojibwe, Anishinaabe, Cree, and related nations. Henry Wadsworth Longfellow made him famous in 'The Song of Hiawatha.' Unlike Wakan Tanka (the Lakota Great Mystery), Gitche Manitou tends to be understood as a more personal supreme creator.
of: The sacred pipe, sage, sweetgrass, tobacco, prayers spoken outdoors facing the sky
an: Eagle (the messenger), bear (the wisdom keeper), wolf
co: White (the colour of the supreme), the blue of the sky
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: Clear quartz, turquoise, white shell
bp: Crown, the entire being, the breath
af: I am held in the Great Spirit. My prayers rise to the most high. The divine is everywhere I look.
sh: The Great Spirit who cannot be directly accessed — the crown chakra that feels the divine is too far away
ar: The Algonquian Supreme / The Great Spirit
cb: The individual Manitou spirits (spirits of specific places, animals, forces) counterbalance Gitche Manitou (the supreme). The universal counterbalanced by the particular.

do: The magical power, the spiritual force in all things, the living energy of creation
at: Orenda is the Iroquois concept of the spiritual power that lives within all things — humans, animals, plants, rocks, weather. Every being has its own degree of orenda. The shaman can accumulate orenda through proper living. It is what gives power to ceremony, to medicine, to song. Like Chi/Prana/Mana but in the Iroquois understanding.
of: The proper performance of all ceremonies (which accumulates orenda), righteous living, the honouring of all living beings
an: All animals have orenda — it cannot be reduced to a single animal
co: No single colour — orenda is the invisible force behind all colour
dp: All days — orenda permeates all time
cr: All stones have orenda — especially quartz (the stone that accumulates orenda most powerfully)
el: All elements — orenda is in all elements
bp: The entire body as a field of orenda, the solar plexus (the centre of personal orenda), the crown (the highest orenda)
af: The living power flows through me and through all things. My orenda is strong. I am alive with the sacred force.
sh: Otkon — the dark force that opposes orenda, the power used for harmful purposes. The shadow of orenda is otkon.
ar: The Living Power / The Force in All Things
cb: Otkon (the dark, destructive force — the shadow of orenda) counterbalances Orenda (the light, creative force). Every positive spiritual force has its shadow counterbalance in Iroquois cosmology.

do: The Cherokee supreme, the provider, the one who lives above
at: Unetlanvhi (sometimes translated as 'the creator' or 'the one who lives above') is the supreme deity of the Cherokee people. The Cherokee originally conceptualised the divine as an impersonal force permeating all nature. Unetlanvhi governs the sky, provides for humanity, and is the source from which all life comes.
of: Tobacco, cedar, sage, the sacred fire ceremony (stomp dance), prayers facing the sacred cardinal directions
an: Eagle (the messenger to the supreme), deer (the sacred provider), turkey (sacred bird of thanksgiving)
co: White (the sacred colour of the Cherokee east and the divine), the four sacred colours of the four directions
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: Quartz crystal ('living stone' to the Cherokee), turquoise, white shell
bp: Crown, the entire being, every breath as the gift of Unetlanvhi
af: The one who lives above sees me and provides for me. I live my life as a prayer of gratitude. The sacred is everywhere.
sh: The transcendent divine that loses touch with the immanent — the creator who is so 'above' that the sacredness of the immediate living world is forgotten
ar: The Cherokee Supreme / The Provider Above
cb: Selu (the Corn Mother — the immediate, the provider from the earth) counterbalances Unetlanvhi (the sky divine, the supreme above). Sky and earth, the providing father above and the self-sacrificing mother below.

do: The creative twin and the destructive twin, the great cosmic polarity of life and death
at: The cosmic twin brothers of Iroquois mythology — the grandsons of Sky Woman. Ioskeha (the right-handed twin) is the creator of all good things: useful plants, animals, clear water. Tawiscara (the left-handed twin) creates the harmful things: briars, poisonous plants, predators. Their contest shaped the world into its current form of mixed blessing and harm.
of: Offerings that acknowledge both the good and the difficult in life, the sacred corn ceremony (Ioskeha's gift), prayers of balance
an: Turtle (their grandmother is Turtle Island), deer (the sacred gift of Ioskeha)
co: White/light (Ioskeha) and dark/shadow (Tawiscara)
dp: Sunday and Saturday — light and dark, creation and destruction
cr: Clear quartz (Ioskeha) and obsidian (Tawiscara) held in balance
el: All elements — between them the twins govern all elements
bp: The entire body (they created its capacity for both life and death), the solar plexus (the contested territory)
af: I contain both the creator and the destroyer within me. I am the field on which this cosmic game is played. I choose creation.
sh: The twin who wins — the cosmic polarity requires both twins. The shadow is the world with only one twin.
ar: The Cosmic Twins / The Polarity That Made the World
cb: Sky Woman (their grandmother — the neutral source from which both twins emerged) counterbalances the twins themselves. The original unity counterbalanced by the necessary duality.

do: The supreme creator, the one above, the source of all things Blackfoot
at: Apistotoki is the Blackfoot supreme creator. He made the world, the animals, and the people. The Blackfoot worldview sees all of nature as alive with sacred power (Natoosi — the sun — is his most direct manifestation). He is invoked in the Sun Dance (Okan) ceremony.
of: Sweetgrass, sage, tobacco in the sacred pipe, offerings facing the four directions, the sun ceremony (Okan — the Sun Dance)
an: Buffalo (the supreme sacred animal of the Blackfoot), eagle, bear
co: The four sacred directions' colours: black (west), yellow (east), white (north), red (south)
dp: Sunday — the day of the sun, his primary manifestation
cr: Sweetgrass braided and offered, tobacco in the pipe, eagle feathers
bp: Crown, the entire being, all four directions of the body
af: The creator sees me walking on this sacred land. I honour the four directions. I am held by the one above.
sh: The creator who cannot be accessed when the sacred ceremonies are lost — the orphaned crown
ar: The Blackfoot Supreme / The Creator Above
cb: Natoosi (the Sun — his primary manifest face) counterbalances Apistotoki (the hidden supreme behind the sun). The visible divine counterbalanced by the invisible supreme.

do: Trickery, creativity through chaos, the violation of taboos, the teaching that comes from mistakes
at: Coyote is the great trickster of many western and central North American indigenous traditions — the divine fool who violates all taboos and through his mistakes creates the world as it is. He stole fire. He created death by accident. He cannot be destroyed because he always comes back. He is the creative chaos within the cosmos.
of: Corn meal, tobacco, the telling of Coyote stories (in the appropriate season — winter), laughter offered as prayer
an: Coyote (he IS the coyote)
co: None fixed — Coyote changes colour like he changes shape
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the trickster's planet
cr: Obsidian (the trickster's dark mirror), clear quartz (the unexpectedly clear lesson)
el: Fire / Air — the fire he stole, the wind of his laughter
bp: The sacral (unbounded appetite for sex, food, pleasure), the throat (his lies and stories), the solar plexus (his outrageous will)
af: I laugh at my own mistakes. My imperfection is the path to wisdom. The trickster in me teaches what the sage cannot.
sh: The trickster without the lesson — the chaos that never resolves into learning, the joke that destroys without wisdom emerging
ar: The Sacred Fool / The Chaos Creator
cb: The Creator / The Orderly Divine counterbalances Coyote (the chaos, the trickster, the rule-breaker). Every tradition that has Coyote also has a divine order that Coyote subverts.

do: Thunder, lightning, storms, the supernatural power of the sky, the guardian of the upper world
at: The Thunderbird is one of the most widespread divine beings in Native American traditions — appearing from the Pacific Northwest to the Great Plains to the Northeast. A giant supernatural bird whose wingbeats create thunder and whose eyes flash lightning. It governs the upper world and is often in conflict with the Great Serpent (the power of the lower world).
of: Tobacco offered to the storm, prayers of respect during thunderstorms, the Thunderbird symbol in ceremony
an: Eagle (the earthly reflection of the Thunderbird), the osprey
co: Black and red (the colours of the storm)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the thunder force
cr: Meteorite (from the sky), thunder egg (a type of geode), dark labradorite
bp: Solar plexus (the thunderous will), the crown (the upper world power), the lungs (the voice of thunder)
af: The power of the Thunderbird flies through me. My will is as strong as the storm. I am protected by the sky's guardian.
sh: The storm without the balance — the Thunderbird that battles the Great Serpent to the point where the world is destroyed
ar: The Sky Guardian / The Lightning Eagle
cb: The Great Horned Serpent / The Water Serpent (the power of the lower world and deep water) counterbalances the Thunderbird (the power of the upper world and the sky). The supreme polarity in many Native American traditions: sky and water, the eagle and the serpent.

do: The all-pervading divine power, the great mystery present in all things
at: Wakonda is the supreme concept of divine power in the Omaha tradition — similar to Wakan Tanka but specifically Omaha. It is the divine force that flows through all things: thunder, animals, places, human achievements. The shaman who has great power is said to have great Wakonda.
of: Tobacco smoke offered to the four directions, sacred ceremony, the living of a life that honours the divine in all things
an: All animals carry Wakonda — especially the eagle, the buffalo, the corn plant
co: No single colour — Wakonda is in all colours
dp: All days — Wakonda permeates all time
cr: All sacred stones carry Wakonda — especially quartz, buffalo bone, turquoise
bp: The entire body as a vessel of Wakonda, the crown (the highest concentration)
af: The divine power moves through me and through all things. My Wakonda is strong. I am alive with the sacred force.
sh: The person with no Wakonda — the desacralised life, the individual cut off from the living divine power
ar: The Omaha Divine Power / The Sacred Force in All
cb: The human ego (which believes it operates by its own power alone) counterbalances Wakonda (the divine power that actually flows through all things). The illusion of self-sufficiency counterbalanced by the divine permeation.

do: The supreme creator of the Mende, the sky god, the most high
at: Ngewo is the supreme deity of the Mende people of Sierra Leone — the creator of all things who dwells in the sky. He created the world and humanity and then largely withdrew, leaving the lesser nature spirits (Ngafanga) to mediate between him and humanity.
of: Prayers directed to the sky, offerings made on high ground, righteous living, the honouring of his gifts through care for the earth and community
an: None — Ngewo is beyond animal representation
co: White (the colour of the divine in Mende tradition)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: Clear quartz, white calcite
bp: Crown, the entire being, the breath as Ngewo's gift
af: The supreme creator knows me. I am held in the most high. My life is a gift I honour.
sh: The supreme who has withdrawn — the sky god who is now so distant that only the nature spirits can be reached
ar: The Mende Supreme / The Sky Creator
cb: The Ngafanga (the nature spirits — the immediate divine forces that mediate between Ngewo and humanity) counterbalance Ngewo (the remote supreme). The distant divine counterbalanced by the immediate divine.

do: Female initiation, women's sacred power, the Sowei mask, beauty and fertility
at: The Sande Society is the most important women's institution in Mende culture. The Sande spirit (embodied in the Sowei mask) is the divine force governing female initiation into womanhood, female sacred power, and community law. The Sande Society's masked figures are among the only masked figures in West African tradition worn by women. The Sowei mask is one of the most sacred objects in Mende culture.
of: The Sande Society initiation ceremony, the Sowei mask ceremony, white clay on the body, offerings of fertility and female wisdom
an: Crocodile (the water creature of feminine transformation), water birds
co: Black (the Sowei mask's colour — representing the deep water from which women's power comes), white (sacred white clay)
dp: Friday — the day of the feminine divine
cr: Black stones (the deep water), white clay (the initiation substance)
bp: The womb (female initiation), the sacral plexus (female sexual and creative power), the crown (the elevated female spiritual authority)
af: I am initiated into the full power of my womanhood. My feminine wisdom is sacred and protected. I carry the Sande spirit within me.
sh: The closed society — the sacred feminine power that cannot be accessed by those outside the initiation
ar: The Initiated Feminine / The Sowei Mask
cb: The Poro Society (the equivalent male initiation society) counterbalances the Sande Society (the feminine sacred). The twin institutions of Mende sacred life — male and female initiation held in permanent polarity.

do: Male initiation, the bush school, the masculine sacred, community law and governance
at: The Poro Society is the most important men's institution in Mende and related West African cultures. Boys who enter the Poro school are 'swallowed' by the Poro spirit and emerge reborn as men. The experience in the bush school is one of the most profound initiation traditions in the world.
of: The Poro initiation ceremony (only available to initiated men), offerings of service to the community, the sacred masks of the Poro society
an: The pangolin (the sacred armoured animal of the Poro), the leopard (the society's guardian)
co: White and red (the colours of initiation and the masculine sacred)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the masculine initiation
cr: Red jasper, white quartz, leopard-spot stone
bp: The entire body remade in initiation, the solar plexus (the masculine power), the root (the new ground of manhood)
af: I have been through the fire and I have emerged. My initiation is complete. I stand as a man who has been properly made.
sh: The uninitiated — in Mende tradition, the one who has not been through Poro is not considered fully a member of the community
ar: The Sacred Masculine Initiation / The Bush School
cb: The Sande Society (the feminine sacred initiation) counterbalances the Poro Society (the masculine sacred initiation). The twin institutions of Mende society in their essential polarity.

do: The land spirits, the sacred bush, ancestral land, territorial power
at: Tinggoi are the territorial spirits of the Mende people — the spirits that inhabit specific patches of land, forest groves, rivers and sacred places. They are the guardians of the ancestral territory. Before any important action on the land (clearing for farming, building, important journeys), the Tinggoi must be propitiated.
of: Libations poured at the edges of the land, the first fruits of cultivation returned to the land, ceremonies before land clearing
an: Python (the land serpent), the forest animals
co: Green (the forest), brown (the earth)
dp: Saturday / Earth
cr: Local stones from the land itself (no imported stones — Tinggoi belong to specific land)
bp: Feet (touching the sacred land), the root chakra (the land beneath), the bones (as the land's bones)
af: I walk on land that has a spirit. I ask permission before I act upon the sacred earth. The land holds the wisdom of all who walked it before me.
sh: The Tinggoi who is offended — when the land spirits are not honoured, the land becomes barren and misfortune follows
ar: The Spirit of Place / The Land Guardian
cb: The sky spirits (the rains, the sun, the divine above) counterbalance the Tinggoi (the earth spirits, the divine below). The cosmic forces counterbalanced by the local spiritual presences.

do: The great, great one, the first human and the first divine, the ancestor of all
at: Unkulunkulu is both the supreme deity and the first ancestor of the Zulu people — he emerged from the reeds (uhlanga) and brought humanity into being. He is simultaneously the most ancient ancestor and the cosmic creative principle. In Zulu cosmology the divine and the ancestral are inseparable.
of: Offerings to the ancestors (he is the first ancestor), ukubuyisa ceremonies (bringing home the spirit of the recently dead), slaughter of cattle at important ceremonies
an: Cattle (the most sacred Zulu animal), the snake (the form ancestors take when they visit)
co: White (the colour of the ancestors in Zulu culture)
dp: Saturday — the day of the ancestors
cr: White quartz, rock crystal
bp: Crown (the divine principle), root (the first ancestor, the earthly foundation)
af: I am held by my ancestors stretching back to the first great one. Unkulunkulu lives in my blood. The first human is my first teacher.
sh: The first ancestor who cannot be reached — when the ancestral connection is broken, Unkulunkulu's presence fades
ar: The First Divine Ancestor / The Great Great One
cb: The living descendants (the present community) counterbalance Unkulunkulu (the original ancestor). The first counterbalanced by the newest. The origin counterbalanced by the present expression.

do: The princess of heaven, rain, fertility, agriculture, the sacred rites of womanhood
at: Nomkhubulwane is one of the most important Zulu goddesses — the daughter of the sky. She governs rain, fertility, agriculture and the sacred first menstruation ceremony. A girl's first menstruation was traditionally celebrated by the community as a holy threshold — the sacral chakra's great awakening honoured as it always should have been.
of: Girls' first menstruation ceremony, rain-calling ceremonies, offerings of beer and food placed on the land, the yellow-wood tree (uMsengwa) is her sacred tree
an: Snake (particularly the green mamba — her sacred snake), cattle
co: White (her divine status), the green of growing things after rain
dp: Monday / Moon — the rain and the feminine cycle
cr: Moonstone, green aventurine, white calcite
bp: Womb (the first menstruation she governs), the sacral plexus (fertility), the crown (she is daughter of the sky)
af: My body's cycles are sacred. I am the princess of heaven expressing herself through the earth. The first rains within me are holy.
sh: The unmade ceremony — Nomkhubulwane's shadow is the first menstruation that goes unhonoured, the girl who becomes a woman without the community's recognition
ar: The Sacred Maiden / The Rain Daughter
cb: Inkosi yezulu (the Lord of the Sky — her father, the masculine sky principle) counterbalances Nomkhubulwane (the feminine rain principle, his daughter). Sky father and rain daughter — the generative polarity of Zulu cosmology.

do: The Lord of the Sky, thunder, lightning, the supreme masculine of the Zulu cosmos
at: Inkosi yezulu ('Lord of the Sky') is the supreme sky deity of the Zulu — he manifests primarily through thunder and lightning. He is not Unkulunkulu (the ancestral supreme) but the active divine masculine force in the sky. He sends the rain through his daughter Nomkhubulwane.
of: Prayers spoken during thunderstorms, offerings made to the sky, slaughter of a black bull during droughts to call the rain
an: Lightning bird (the impundulu — a mythological bird whose wingbeats cause lightning), cattle (black cattle for rain ceremonies)
co: Black (the storm cloud), silver-white (the lightning), deep blue (the sky)
dp: Tuesday / Mars and Sunday / Sun — the storm and the solar masculine
cr: Meteorite (from the sky), dark labradorite, black tourmaline
bp: Solar plexus (the thunderous masculine will), crown (the sky lord's domain), the eyes (lightning)
af: The thunder of the Lord of the Sky moves through me. My masculine power is royal and just. The storm within me brings the rain of blessing.
sh: The drought — when Inkosi yezulu withholds the rain, the earth suffers. The masculine divine that withholds its gifts.
ar: The Zulu Sky Lord / The Thunder Father
cb: Nomkhubulwane (his daughter — the feminine rain principle) counterbalances Inkosi yezulu (the masculine sky thunder). Father and daughter, thunder and rain, the masculine storm and the feminine precipitation.

do: The supreme deity of the Sotho and Tswana peoples, the creator of all things
at: Modimo is the supreme deity of the Sotho-Tswana peoples of South Africa, Botswana and Lesotho — the creator of all things, the omnipresent divine. Unlike many African supreme deities, Modimo is considered to be everywhere at once — in the sky, the earth, and within human beings.
of: Prayer, righteous living, the honouring of the ancestors (who are Modimo's intermediaries), offerings at sacred trees and rocks
an: None — Modimo permeates all creatures and cannot be reduced to one
co: White (the colour of the divine)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, the stones of sacred sites in Lesotho and Botswana
el: Cosmic Consciousness — he is in all elements
bp: Crown, the entire being, the divine presence within
af: The supreme is everywhere — in me, around me, in all things. I walk in the presence of Modimo. I am never alone.
sh: The divine that is everywhere but experienced nowhere — the omnipresent divine so diffuse it cannot be felt
ar: The Omnipresent Supreme / The Sotho-Tswana Creator
cb: The ancestors (the specific, personal divine that can be directly experienced) counterbalance Modimo (the universal, omnipresent divine). The infinite divine presence counterbalanced by the personal ancestral connection.

do: The Xhosa supreme creator, the provider, the all-knowing divine
at: Thixo (also called Qamata) is the supreme divine being of the Xhosa people of South Africa. Nelson Mandela was raised in the Xhosa tradition with Thixo/Qamata as the spiritual background. Qamata is the specifically indigenous Xhosa name; Thixo was used after contact with missionaries. Both refer to the supreme creator.
of: Prayer, the honouring of the ancestors (amakhosi) who mediate between humanity and Thixo, ukubethela ceremonies (ceremonial cattle slaughter)
an: Cattle (the most sacred Nguni animal), the great serpent
co: White (the colour of the ancestral and the divine in Xhosa culture)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, white calcite, ochre-red clay (the sacred Xhosa initiation clay)
bp: Crown, the entire being, the bones (the ancestors' connection to the earth)
af: Thixo sees me and provides for me. The ancestors stand between me and the supreme. I am held in the great chain of being.
sh: The colonial wound — Thixo/Qamata as the traditional divine that was systematically replaced by the missionary God
ar: The Xhosa Supreme / The Provider Above
cb: The amakhosi (the ancestral spirits — the Xhosa ancestors who mediate between humanity and Thixo) counterbalance Thixo (the remote supreme). The intimate ancestral dead counterbalanced by the all-knowing supreme.

do: Rain, rainbow, beer, agriculture, love — the Zulu goddess of joy and abundance
at: Mbaba Mwana Waresa is the Zulu goddess of rain, the rainbow, beer (umqombothi — traditional Zulu beer), and agriculture. She is depicted wearing a rainbow-shaped hut (her home). She once descended to earth to find a human husband because she could find no suitable divine husband. She is the Zulu expression of the love goddess — the one who seeks real love rather than only the divine.
of: Traditional Zulu beer (umqombothi) offered to the earth, rainbow imagery, rain ceremonies, the honouring of the agricultural cycle
an: The rainbow (her sacred symbol), cattle, the bee (her beer is honey-fermented)
co: Rainbow colours, the deep blue of rain, golden yellow (the beer and the harvest)
dp: Monday / Venus — the rain, the rainbow, the love
cr: Rainbow moonstone, citrine, blue topaz
bp: Sacral plexus (love, beer, the pleasure of abundance), heart (the love she sought on earth), womb (the rain's fertility)
af: The rainbow arches over my abundance. I seek love that is real and true. Joy and rain are the same blessing.
sh: The goddess who could find no worthy partner — the longing for love that meets no equal
ar: The Rainbow Joy Goddess / The Beer and Rain Mother
cb: Inkosi yezulu (the thunder lord, the masculine sky force) counterbalances Mbaba Mwana Waresa (the rain goddess, the rainbow, the feminine abundance). Thunder and rain, the masculine storm and the feminine flowering.

do: The supreme creator of the Venda people, the thunder and sky god
at: Raluvhimba is the supreme deity of the Venda people of northern South Africa (Limpopo). He created all things and governs the sky, thunder, and the natural order. He is sometimes depicted as a great serpent in the sky. The Venda people have an extraordinary musical tradition dedicated to his honour.
of: The tshikona pipe ceremony, the domba initiation, prayers directed to the sky, offerings during thunderstorms
an: Python (the sacred Venda snake — never killed), the eagle
co: White (the divine), the blue of the sky, the red of the sunset
dp: Sunday / Mars — the sky creator
cr: White quartz, the python's shed skin (sacred), red jasper
bp: Crown (the supreme), solar plexus (the thunder), the entire body in ceremony
af: The great creator thunders through my life with power and purpose. I honour the divine in the storm. I dance to his sacred music.
sh: The thunder that does not bring rain — the divine masculine power without the feminine receiving earth
ar: The Venda Thunder Creator / The Sky Serpent
cb: The earth (the feminine principle, the serpent's home) counterbalances Raluvhimba (the sky, the masculine principle). Also the python (the earth serpent) counterbalances Raluvhimba (the sky serpent).

do: The supreme deity of the Bemba people, the sky father, the provider of rain
at: Lesa is the supreme deity of the Bemba people of Zambia — the sky god who sends rain and governs all of creation. The Bemba people have an extraordinarily rich sacred tradition centred on the Chisungu female initiation ceremony, which is one of the most elaborate female initiation ceremonies in all of Africa.
of: Prayer and righteous living, offerings made during the Chisungu ceremony, the honouring of the divine in the initiation of young women
an: Eagle (the sky bird), the python (the earth serpent)
co: White (the colour of the divine sky)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, clear calcite
bp: Crown, the entire being
af: The supreme sky father sees and provides. The rain of blessing falls on my life. I am under divine care.
sh: The drought — when Lesa withholds the rain; the divine provision that is absent
ar: The Bemba Supreme / The Rain Father
cb: The earth (the feminine receiving principle) counterbalances Lesa (the masculine giving principle of rain). Sky and earth in Bemba cosmology.

do: The smoking mirror, darkness, sorcery, the night sky, the creative shadow
at: The supreme rival of Quetzalcoatl and one of the four creator gods of Aztec cosmology. 'Smoking Mirror' (Tezcatl-ipoca). One of his feet was replaced by an obsidian mirror in which he could see the hearts and deeds of all. He is the night sky, the jaguar, the darkness that completes the light. He and Quetzalcoatl are in eternal contest — their conflict is what drives history.
of: Obsidian mirrors (the smoking mirror is his sacred instrument), black offerings, midnight ceremonies, the jaguar as his living form
an: Jaguar (his night-form), turkey (his sacred bird for divination), spider
co: Black, midnight blue, obsidian-black
dp: Monday / Saturn — the night, the shadow
cr: Obsidian (the smoking mirror stone), black tourmaline, dark labradorite
bp: Third eye (the smoking mirror reveals all hearts), root (the night's depth), crown (the omniscience of the mirror)
af: I see what the smoking mirror shows without flinching. My shadow is my ally. The night reveals what the day conceals.
sh: The shadow without the light — Tezcatlipoca's cosmic shadow is the universe with only darkness and no Quetzalcoatl to bring learning and light
ar: The Smoking Mirror / The Night Jaguar
cb: Quetzalcoatl (the feathered serpent, light, learning, the morning star) counterbalances Tezcatlipoca (the smoking mirror, darkness, sorcery, the night sky). The supreme Aztec polarity — their eternal contest IS Mesoamerican history.

do: The dawn, joy, dance, the arts, the trance of sacred performance
at: The goddess of dawn, revelry and the arts — she is the Shinto deity who danced so joyfully and wildly that the gods laughed and Amaterasu (the sun goddess) was lured out of her cave, returning light to the world. She is the divine principle of joy as a spiritual act, of dance as sacred practice.
of: Dance offered as prayer, performance dedicated to the divine, laughter and joy as offerings, dawn ceremonies
an: Cock/rooster (the bird of dawn)
co: Red and gold (the dawn colours), the bright colours of celebration
dp: Sunday / Venus — the dawn joy
cr: Sunstone, carnelian, amber
el: Fire / Air — the dawn fire and the joyful wind
bp: Solar plexus (the joy that draws the sun), sacral (the ecstatic dance), throat (the laughter that opens the cave)
af: My joy is a sacred act. My dance draws the sun. When I celebrate fully, I restore the light.
sh: The performance without the presence — the dance that has lost its ecstatic connection to the divine
ar: The Joy Bringer / The Sacred Dawn Dance
cb: Amaterasu (the sun whose emergence Ame-no-Uzume caused) counterbalances Ame-no-Uzume (the joy that caused the emergence). The divine it serves counterbalanced by the creative art that serves it.

do: Chaos, the desert, storms, foreigners, war, the necessary disruption
at: Set (Seth) is the Egyptian god of chaos, the desert, storms, and foreigners — the eternal enemy and necessary counterpart of Osiris and Horus. He murdered Osiris and scattered his body; he blinded Horus's eye. But he also stood on the prow of Ra's barque each night and fought Apophis with his iron harpoon. Without Set fighting Apophis, Ra would not rise. He is necessary chaos.
of: Red offerings (his colour), desert sand, iron (his metal — 'Set's iron' was Egyptian for meteoritic iron), storms honoured as sacred
an: Set animal (a mythological composite creature), hippo, crocodile, ass
co: Red (his primary colour — the red of the desert, the red of blood)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the warrior's chaos
cr: Red jasper, carnelian, iron meteorite
bp: Solar plexus (the warrior's fire), root (the desert's grounding), adrenal glands
af: The chaos within me serves a purpose. I fight the serpent of destruction every night and I let the sun rise. My disruption is sacred.
sh: The Set who kills Osiris — the chaos that goes beyond necessary disruption into genuine destruction
ar: The Necessary Chaos / The Desert Warrior
cb: Osiris (order, resurrection, the green abundance) counterbalances Set (chaos, the desert, the destructive storm). Neither can exist without the other. Also Horus (the son who overcomes Set) counterbalances Set.

do: Death, the transition of the soul, the angel of grief, the compassionate presence at life's end
at: Azrael is the Angel of Death in Islamic tradition — not the fearsome skeleton of popular imagination but a compassionate divine presence who assists souls in their transition from the physical to the spiritual world. He is depicted as having as many wings as there are souls, constantly occupied with receiving the dying.
of: Prayers for the dying, offerings made for the recently deceased, the honouring of grief as sacred process, black and white candles
an: None — Azrael is pure presence
co: Black and white (the colours of transition between the worlds), sometimes deep indigo
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the transition of death
cr: Obsidian (the transition stone), jet, black moonstone
el: Ether / Earth (the body returning to earth)
bp: Root (the body at death), crown (the soul's departure), the entire being in its final transition
af: The angel of death is compassionate, not fearsome. I walk toward my own death with dignity. All transitions are held in divine care.
sh: The grief without comfort — the death that happens without Azrael's compassionate presence, the transition utterly alone
ar: The Compassionate Death Angel / The Soul Transitioner
cb: Archangel Raphael (the angel of healing — who extends life) counterbalances Azrael (the angel of death — who receives life's end). The healer and the death angel in their essential polarity.

do: The sun, the underworld, healing, abundance — the syncretic deity of Alexandria
at: Serapis was deliberately created in the early Ptolemaic period to unite Greek and Egyptian religious sensibilities — combining Osiris (death, resurrection, abundance) with the Apis bull (fertility) and Zeus/Helios (the supreme, the sun). He became one of the most widely worshipped deities in the ancient Mediterranean world.
of: Solar ceremonies, grain offerings (he governed agricultural abundance), healing prayers (his temples were healing centres)
an: Cerberus (the three-headed dog often depicted with him), serpent, bull (the Apis bull)
co: Gold (the solar), blue (the heavenly), the dark colours of the underworld
dp: Sunday / Saturn — sun and the underworld cycle
cr: Gold, lapis lazuli, yellow jasper, dark sapphire
el: Fire / Earth (the solar above and the underworld below)
bp: Crown (the sun), root (the underworld and the earth's abundance), the entire body as the site of his healing
af: The sun above and the earth below are both sacred. I am held between heaven and earth. I am healed.
sh: The deliberately created deity — Serapis's shadow is the divine manufactured for political purposes, the syncretism that loses the depth of both traditions
ar: The Syncretic Healer / The Alexandrian Sun
cb: Isis (the great Egyptian mother who was always paired with Serapis in Hellenistic religion) counterbalances Serapis (the great father). The mother and father of the Hellenistic world in their essential polarity.

do: Healing, medicine, the sacred serpent of healing, the dream-cure
at: The divine physician — originally a mortal hero who became so skilled at healing that he could raise the dead, causing Zeus to strike him down. He was then deified. His temples (Asclepeia) were the hospitals of the ancient world, where patients would sleep (enkoimesis) and receive healing visions from the god in dreams. His symbol — the Rod of Asclepius — remains the universal symbol of medicine.
of: Non-venomous serpents kept in his temples, offerings of model body parts (indicating the afflicted area), the healing sleep ceremony, honey cakes
an: Serpent (the healing serpent), dog (who would lick wounds to heal them in his temples), cock (the offering given when healed)
co: Green (healing), gold (divine medicine), white (the healer's robes)
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the healer's wisdom
cr: Emerald (the healing stone), green tourmaline, serpentine
bp: Every part of the body — he heals all of it. Specifically the hands and heart (the compassion of medicine)
af: The divine physician lives in my cells. My body knows how to heal. I sleep in the healing temple of my own rest.
sh: The healer who raises the dead — the medicine that goes too far, that refuses to allow natural death, that violates the cosmic order
ar: The Divine Physician / The Dream Healer
cb: Hypnos (Sleep — his therapeutic partner) and Thanatos (Death — the one Asclepius tried to overcome) counterbalance Asclepius (Healing). Sleep, death and healing form a sacred triangle.

do: The messenger between the divine and humanity, the sacrifice, the healer's most powerful face
at: Oshun Ibu Kole is the oldest and most powerful of Oshun's caminos (roads/aspects) — she became the vulture and flew to heaven carrying humanity's prayers to Olodumare when all the other Orishas failed. She sacrificed her beauty (her feathers were scorched, turning the vulture black) to save humanity. She is the Oshun who gave everything for love.
of: Honey, river offerings, prayers of the deepest gratitude, the acknowledgment of sacrifice made for love
an: Vulture (the sacrifice bird — her sacred bird in this aspect)
co: Black (the scorched feathers), gold (her original beauty)
dp: Saturday — the day of sacrifice and love
cr: Scorched obsidian (the sacrifice), gold (what was before the sacrifice)
el: Air / Water / Fire — she traversed all elements to reach heaven
bp: Solar plexus (the sacrifice of power), crown (the reaching to heaven), wings/shoulder blades (the flight)
af: I give what is most precious in the service of love. My sacrifice is beautiful. The divine receives what I offer and returns it transformed.
sh: The sacrifice that asks for recognition — the love that gave everything and then resented the giving
ar: The Sacred Sacrifice / The Messenger of All Prayers
cb: Olodumare (the supreme who received her sacrifice) counterbalances Oshun Ibu Kole (the one who sacrificed everything to reach the supreme). The infinite divine counterbalanced by the one who reached across the infinite to touch it.

do: The sacred fish — the wife of the supreme god, the water of the divine union
at: Yemowo is the wife of Mawu-Lisa in the Fon tradition — a water goddess who represents the feminine principle of the divine union at the highest level. She is the sacred waters that receive the divine seed. She is one of the highest expressions of the water goddess tradition in West Africa.
of: Water offerings, white and blue cloth, the sacred fish (especially the carp), river ceremonies
an: Sacred carp (the fish of divine water), serpent
co: White and blue (the divine water colours)
dp: Monday / Moon — the sacred feminine water
cr: Moonstone, aquamarine, white quartz
bp: Womb (the divine reception), sacral plexus, the crown (the union with the highest)
af: I am the sacred waters that receive the divine. My womb is consecrated to the highest. I am the meeting point of heaven and water.
sh: The sacred feminine that is always defined by its relationship to the masculine divine — the shadow is the loss of one's own sacred identity in the divine marriage
ar: The Divine Wife / The Sacred Water Union
cb: Mawu-Lisa (the supreme androgynous divine) counterbalances Yemowo (the sacred feminine water principle). The supreme counterbalanced by the receiving principle.

do: The supreme deity of the Maasai, the sky, cattle, rain
at: Ngai (the same name as the Kikuyu supreme deity but in the Maasai tradition) is the supreme sky god of the Maasai people — the creator and sustainer of all things. He gave the Maasai their cattle (the most sacred possession), established the covenant of the sky and the earth, and is invoked in all important ceremonies. Ngai has two faces: the benevolent (Ngai Narok — black god) and the wrathful (Ngai Nanyokie — red god).
of: Cattle offerings (the highest possible), the slaughter of an ox at the most important ceremonies, prayers directed to Mount Kilimanjaro (one of his homes), libations of milk (cattle's sacred fluid)
an: Cattle (the most sacred Maasai animal — their covenant with Ngai), the lion (the wild equivalent of cattle)
co: Black (Ngai Narok — the benevolent face) and red (Ngai Nanyokie — the wrathful face)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: Red ochre (used in Maasai ceremonies), black and red stones, the blood of cattle (ceremonially)
bp: Crown, the entire being, the cattle as the material extension of Ngai's blessing into the Maasai's bodily world
af: I walk in the covenant of the sky. My herd is my sacred responsibility. Ngai sees me and I see Ngai in all of creation.
sh: The drought — when Ngai withholds the rain, the cattle die and the covenant seems broken. The shadow of the sky covenant is its absence.
ar: The Maasai Sky Covenant / The Two-Faced Supreme
cb: The earth (the grass that feeds the cattle — the feminine principle) counterbalances Ngai (the sky that rains on the grass — the masculine principle). The sacred covenant between sky and earth expressed through the Maasai's sacred cattle.

do: Sovereignty, the sacred feminine ruler, resistance to oppression, the divine authority of women
at: Queen Njinga of Ndongo and Matamba (c.1583-1663) — one of the greatest rulers in African history, elevated to near-divine status in her tradition. She resisted Portuguese colonialism for forty years, conducted brilliant diplomacy, commanded armies, and ruled with extraordinary wisdom. She is invoked in Candomblé and other African diaspora traditions as a spirit of sovereignty and resistance.
of: Military respect (standing in her honour), the colour black (she wore the weapons of her enemies as ornaments), offerings of sovereignty and dignity
an: Leopard (the royal animal of Central Africa), the war horse
co: Black and gold (the sovereign's colours), red (the warrior's blood)
dp: Tuesday / Mars and Sunday — the warrior queen and the crown
cr: Black tourmaline (the warrior's protection), gold (the sovereign's crown), leopard-spot jasper
bp: Solar plexus (the warrior's sovereign will), crown (the queen's authority), throat (the voice of sovereignty that refused to surrender)
af: I stand in my sovereignty without apology. I negotiate from power. My authority is earned and it is divine.
