af: The eagle watches over me. The law was given for my flourishing. I see clearly from the highest perspective.
sh: The eagle's shadow — the supreme creator who is also a raptor: the law that can devour
ar: The Eagle Creator / The Law-Giver of the Kulin
cb: Waa (the crow — the trickster moiety) counterbalances Bunjil (the eagle — the powerful creator). The supreme Kulin polarity: eagle and crow, creator and trickster.

do: The ocean, all sea creatures, creation, the deep waters of the cosmos
at: Tangaroa (Kanaloa in Hawaiian) is one of the great primary gods of the Polynesian pantheon — the lord of the ocean and all sea creatures. In some traditions he is the creator of the world, fishing the islands from the ocean. He is the sacral chakra's ocean principle.
of: Ocean offerings, fish returned to the sea with prayer, the first catch offered back to Tangaroa, deep water ceremonies
an: Octopus (his sacred animal — his living form in many traditions), turtle, all fish
co: Blue-black (the deep ocean), silver (the sea's surface light)
dp: Monday / Neptune — the ocean god
cr: Aquamarine, ocean jasper, black pearl, dark lapis
bp: Sacral (the ocean's creative force), the lungs (breathing in the sea's domain), the entire fluid body
af: I am at home in the deep waters. The ocean of my being is vast and ancient. I trust the depths.
sh: The ocean that swallows — Tangaroa's shadow is the deep water that takes without returning
ar: The Ocean Creator / The Lord of the Deep
cb: Tāne (the sky and forest god) counterbalances Tangaroa (the ocean). Forest and ocean, sky and sea — the supreme Polynesian cosmic polarity.

do: The forest, trees, birds, fresh water, the sky, the creation of humans from clay
at: Tāne (Kāne in Hawaiian) is the god of forests, birds, and the sky. In many Polynesian traditions he created the first human woman from clay. He separated Rangi (Sky Father) from Papa (Earth Mother), letting light into the world. He is the great vertical axis connecting earth and heaven.
of: The planting of trees as sacred act, the honouring of birds (Tāne's children), fresh water returned to the earth, ceremonies in the forest
an: All birds (Tāne's children — especially the kiwi and tui), all trees (the forest is his body)
co: Green (the forest), blue (the sky), the golden green of forest light
dp: Sunday / Venus (the forest's beauty)
cr: Green jade, pounamu (New Zealand greenstone — the most sacred stone of the Māori, Tāne's gift), green aventurine
el: Air / Earth (the sky and the forest)
bp: Heart (the forest's compassion), root (the trees' roots), crown (the sky he holds open)
af: I plant what will outlast me. The forest sustains me. I hold the sky open through my love for the earth.
sh: The forest burned — Tāne's shadow is the destruction of the trees he created, the desecration of the forests that are his body
ar: The Forest Sky God / The Creator of Humans
cb: Tangaroa (the ocean) counterbalances Tāne (the forest and sky). The supreme Polynesian polarity: forest and ocean, trees and fish, the green world and the blue world.

do: The hula, the forest, flowers, love, the sacred arts
at: Laka is the Hawaiian goddess of the hula and the forest — she governs all the plants used in hula, the songs that accompany the dance, and the sacred act of the hula itself. The hula is not entertainment but sacred ceremony: a moving prayer that honours the gods, preserves history, and connects the dancer to the divine.
of: The hula as sacred offering (the hālau hula — hula school — is her temple), lei of maile and fern, yellow flowers, the lehua and ginger used in hula offerings
an: The i'iwi (scarlet honeycreeper — the forest bird), all forest birds
co: Green (the forest), yellow (the flowers), the rainbow colours of the lei
dp: Friday / Venus — the sacred beauty of the hula
cr: Green jade, yellow jasper, carnelian
el: Earth / Air (the dance and the song)
bp: Sacral (the hips of the hula), heart (the love the hula expresses), throat (the chant that accompanies the dance)
af: My body is a prayer. My dance is a ceremony. Every movement I make with intention is an offering to the divine.
sh: The hula as performance — Laka's shadow is the sacred dance commercialised, the ceremony emptied of meaning by colonial tourism
ar: The Sacred Dance / The Forest Prayer
cb: Kāne (the forest — the trees from which the hula altar is made) counterbalances Laka (the dance within the forest). The sacred space counterbalanced by the sacred act performed within it.

do: The destruction of obstacles and ego, the kila (ritual dagger), purification through wrathful means
at: Vajrakilaya (Dorje Phurba — the Vajra Dagger) is one of the most important wrathful deities in Tibetan Buddhism — a fierce yidam whose practice destroys obstacles, neutralises negative forces and cuts through the ego's deepest delusions. He is depicted in fierce embrace (yab-yum) with his consort, wielding the three-sided kila dagger.
of: The kila (ritual dagger) used in ceremony, fierce mantra recitation, fire puja (offering fire), the visualisation of obstacles being pierced by the kila
an: Garuda (the divine eagle — his upper body), makara (sea dragon — his lower body)
co: Dark blue (his fierce body), red (the fire of his wrath), gold
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the wrathful fierce
cr: Dark lapis, obsidian, fire opal
bp: Solar plexus (the ego that is cut), root (the obstacle removed), the hands (holding the kila)
af: I cut through what blocks me with the vajra dagger of wisdom. No obstacle is greater than the clarity of my mind. I pierce illusion.
sh: The kila wielded without wisdom — the wrathful force that destroys without discernment
ar: The Obstacle Destroyer / The Vajra Dagger
cb: Guru Rinpoche / Padmasambhava (who tamed the obstacles before Vajrakilaya finishes them) counterbalances Vajrakilaya (the destroyer of what was tamed). The tamer and the destroyer working in sequence.

do: The protectress of Tibet, the fierce mother, the guardian of the dharma
at: Palden Lhamo is the only female Dharmapala (protector of the dharma) in the Tibetan Buddhist pantheon — and the fiercest of all protectors. She rides a mule over an ocean of blood, wearing human skulls. She is the protectress of Tibet and Lhasa. The Dalai Lama's succession is confirmed through divination at her lake (Lhamo La-tso). She is Mahakali in Tibetan form.
of: Fierce prayers of protection, offerings at her fire puja, the Lhamo La-tso lake honoured, the protectress invoked before any important undertaking
an: Mule (her mount), the snow lion, the raven (her messenger)
co: Dark blue-black (her body), red (her fire), the white of snow and bone
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the fierce protectress
cr: Dark lapis, obsidian, red garnet
bp: Root (the fierce grounding of protection), solar plexus (the protectress's fire), third eye (the lake of divination)
af: The fierce protectress stands at my threshold. Nothing harmful can pass her. I am under the protection of the supreme fierce mother.
sh: The protectress become destroyer — the force of protection that becomes indiscriminate destruction
ar: The Fierce Protectress / The Guardian of the Highest
cb: Tara (the compassionate swift action of the feminine divine) counterbalances Palden Lhamo (the wrathful fierce protection). Compassion and wrath as the two faces of the divine feminine in Tibetan Buddhism.

do: The river spirit, the guardian of rivers, the seductive water woman of the Caribbean
at: Mama Dglo (Mama d'lo — 'Mother of the Water') is a water spirit of Trinidad and Tobago — a woman from the waist up with a serpent's tail below, who inhabits rivers and lagoons. She guards the waterways and all water creatures. She punishes those who pollute or disrespect rivers with seduction and death.
of: Offerings of flowers floated on rivers, the respectful treatment of all waterways, no littering in rivers or streams
an: Anaconda (her serpent tail), fish, the coral snake
co: Green and blue (the river's colours), silver (the water's shimmer)
dp: Monday / Neptune — the river guardian
cr: Aquamarine, green aventurine, river stone
bp: Sacral (the seductive feminine water), the lower body (her serpent tail), the feet (the river walker)
af: I respect every waterway as sacred. The river has a guardian. I pour my gratitude into the water.
sh: The seduction that lures to death — Mama Dglo's shadow is the beauty that punishes the disrespectful by pulling them into the depths
ar: The River Guardian / The Caribbean Water Woman
cb: Papa Bois (the forest spirit — her consort, the guardian of the land) counterbalances Mama Dglo (the river spirit, guardian of the water). Land and water in their Caribbean expression.

do: The forest spirit, the guardian of the animals and the forest of the Caribbean
at: Papa Bois ('Father of the Forest') is the spirit of the forest in Trinidad and Tobago — an old man with hooves and goat's horns who protects animals and trees. He punishes hunters who kill more than they need or who disrespect the forest. He is the Caribbean version of the forest guardian found across West African and indigenous American traditions.
of: Never killing more than needed, leaving a portion of the hunt in the forest, speaking respectfully in the forest, not destroying trees without purpose
an: All wild animals (he is their protector), the deer, the goat
co: Green and brown (the forest colours)
dp: Saturday / Earth — the forest guardian
cr: Brown jasper, green aventurine, wood (a sacred piece of forest wood)
bp: Feet (his goat hooves connect him to the earth), root (the forest floor), the legs
af: I take only what I need from the forest. The trees are my elders. I walk in the forest with respect and gratitude.
sh: The forest without a guardian, stripped and silent — Papa Bois's shadow is the unprotected wild
ar: The Forest Father / The Animal Guardian
cb: Mama Dglo (the river spirit — his consort, the water guardian) counterbalances Papa Bois (the forest spirit, the land guardian). Land and water in their Caribbean polarity.

do: The supreme divine of the Dinka people, the sky and rain, the source of all
at: Nhialic is the supreme deity of the Dinka people of South Sudan — the creator and sustainer of all things. The Dinka religious tradition is one of the oldest and most sophisticated in East Africa, centring on cattle as the sacred link between the human and the divine.
of: Cattle ceremonies (cattle are the sacred medium between humans and Nhialic), prayers before rain, the sacrifice of the most prized animal in times of great need
an: Cattle (the most sacred Dinka animal — the link to Nhialic), the sacred white ox
co: White (the sacred white cattle, the purity of the divine), blue (the sky where Nhialic dwells)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, white calcite, the bone of sacred cattle
bp: Crown, the entire being, the breath as Nhialic's gift
af: The sky divine watches over me. The rain is the divine's blessing. I honour the sacred cattle and through them I honour the most high.
sh: The drought — when Nhialic withholds the rain; the sky god whose blessing is absent from the earth
ar: The Dinka Supreme / The Rain-Sky Divine
cb: The earth (the receiving principle, the ground that needs the rain) counterbalances Nhialic (the sky divine, the giving principle of rain). The sacred vertical axis of Dinka cosmology.

do: The wild masculine, the hunt, nature, the cycle of death and rebirth, the sun's journey
at: The Horned God of Wicca and modern Paganism — drawing from Cernunnos, Pan, Dionysus, and other horned nature deities — is the divine masculine principle in its most natural, cyclical expression. He is the hunter and the hunted; he dies at Samhain and is reborn at Yule. He governs the masculine aspect of nature: the stag, the oak king, the dark lord of winter, the lord of the hunt.
of: Outdoor ceremonies in his honour, the oak and holly as sacred trees (the Oak King and Holly King's eternal contest), the antler worn as ritual headdress, bonfires at the cross-quarter days
an: Stag (his primary animal), goat, wolf
co: Green (the summer oak king), black (the winter holly king), gold and brown (the earth's turning)
dp: Saturday and Tuesday — the earth and the warrior of the hunt
cr: Brown tourmaline, moss agate, antler (carved), green jasper
bp: Root (the wild masculine ground), sacral (the masculine sexual force), the legs (the stag's running)
af: The wild masculine in me is sacred. I am the hunter and I am the hunted. My cycle of death and rebirth is the earth's cycle.
sh: The hunt without mercy, the stag who becomes the predator without the sacred covenant of the hunt
ar: The Wild God / The Horned One
cb: The Triple Goddess (the Maiden, Mother, Crone — the feminine principle of Wicca) counterbalances the Horned God (the masculine principle). The supreme Wiccan polarity.

do: The three phases of the feminine, the moon's three faces, the cycle of life through all women
at: The Triple Goddess of Wicca — the Maiden (new moon, spring, youth), the Mother (full moon, summer, fertility), and the Crone (dark moon, winter, wisdom) — is the supreme divine feminine principle of modern Paganism. Drawing from many traditions (Hecate, Brigid, the Morrigan, Diana and others), she represents the complete arc of feminine power across the full life cycle.
of: The esbat (full moon ceremony), the drawing down of the moon ceremony, the honouring of each phase of the moon and each phase of the feminine life cycle
an: The white hare, the cat, the owl (the crone's bird), the deer (the maiden's animal)
co: White (the maiden), red (the mother's blood), black (the crone's wisdom) — all three together
dp: Monday / Moon — she IS the moon in all its phases
cr: Moonstone (the maiden), pearl (the mother), jet or onyx (the crone)
el: Water / Earth / Air — the three elements of the three phases
bp: Sacral (maiden's awakening and mother's fertility), heart (the mother's love), crown (the crone's wisdom)
af: I am maiden, mother and crone. I honour every phase of my feminine being. The moon in all her phases is my body.
sh: The phase that is rejected — the culture that only honours the maiden and refuses the mother and destroys the crone
ar: The Three-Phased Divine Feminine / The Moon in All Her Faces
cb: The Horned God (the divine masculine, the sun, the linear) counterbalances the Triple Goddess (the divine feminine, the moon, the cyclical). The supreme Wiccan polarity.

do: The queen of the witches, the daughter of Diana, the liberator of the oppressed
at: Aradia is the goddess of Italian witchcraft — the daughter of Diana (the moon) and Lucifer (the sun in Italian folk magic). In Charles Leland's 'Aradia: Gospel of the Witches' (1899), she is the divine figure who came to earth to teach the oppressed peasants the arts of witchcraft as a form of resistance and liberation.
of: The full moon ceremony (the Charge of the Goddess is her teaching), the casting of spells for justice, silver offerings, white roses
an: Owl (the witch's bird), the black cat, the wolf
co: Silver (the moon), white (the divine feminine), black (the night magic)
dp: Monday / Moon — the queen of witches
cr: Moonstone, selenite, silver, clear quartz
bp: Crown (the witch queen's divine authority), throat (her teachings), the hands (the magic worked)
af: I claim my magic as sacred. My practice liberates. The queen of the witches lives in my knowledge of the sacred arts.
sh: The magic as escapism — the witchcraft tradition used only for personal advantage rather than for liberation and justice
ar: The Witch Queen / The Liberator Through Magic
cb: Diana (her mother — the celestial divine, the moon goddess) counterbalances Aradia (the earthly expression of that divine power, the practical magic). The celestial counterbalanced by the terrestrial.

do: The great hunter of Windsor Forest, the wild hunt, the winter masculine spirit
at: Herne the Hunter is the spectral huntsman of Windsor Great Park — said to be the ghost of a royal gamekeeper, or a specific aspect of Cernunnos in English folk tradition. He leads the Wild Hunt through the winter sky. He is the English expression of the Horned God: the wild masculine of the forest, the ghost-hunter who rides through the winter night.
of: Offerings at the oak tree (especially the Herne Oak), midnight ceremonies in forests, the acknowledgment of the wild hunt as the winter's necessary sweep
an: Stag (his horned head), owl (the winter night bird), raven
co: Black and brown (the forest at night), the grey of winter
dp: Saturday / Saturn — the winter masculine
cr: Brown jasper, obsidian, antler, dark oak wood
el: Earth / Air (the wild hunt through the winter sky)
bp: Root (the wild masculine ground), the head (his horned head), the feet (the forest runner)
af: The wild hunt sweeps through what is stale in my life. The winter masculine moves through my darkness with purpose. I am not afraid of the wild masculine.
sh: The haunted gamekeeper — the masculine energy that could not be integrated and became the hanged man, the desperate ghost who rides the winter without rest
ar: The Wild Huntsman / The English Horned Spirit
cb: The Summer King (the Oak King — the masculine in its light, growing, generative expression) counterbalances Herne the Hunter (the masculine in its dark, winter, hunting expression). The growing and the hunting masculine in eternal seasonal alternation.

do: The ocean mother, all life's origin, the mother of all Orixás, the deepest feminine compassion
at: Iemanjá (the Brazilian form of Yemoja) is perhaps the most beloved deity in all of Brazil — her festival on February 2nd fills the beaches of Rio de Janeiro and Salvador with millions of devotees. She is the mother of all life. In Brazil she receives all of Brazil's grief and longing at the edge of the ocean.
of: The February 2nd festival (boat offerings floated out to sea), white and blue flowers, white candles, perfume, mirrors, combs — all floated out to sea on decorated rafts
an: Fish (all sea fish), the mermaid (she is depicted as a mermaid in Brazil), the dolphin
co: White and blue (the ocean mother's colours), silver (the sea's light)
dp: Saturday / Moon — the ocean mother
cr: Moonstone, aquamarine, white quartz, sea glass, pearl
bp: Sacral (the ocean womb), breasts (the nursing mother), heart (the mother who receives all grief)
af: The mother of all receives my offerings at the edge of the ocean. I am held by the first water. I am never motherless.
sh: The ocean mother who swallows rather than receives — the grief cast to the sea but never transformed
ar: The Brazilian Ocean Mother / The February Festival
cb: Oxalá (the supreme white father of purity and creation in Candomblé) counterbalances Iemanjá (the supreme white mother of the ocean). The supreme father and mother in their Brazilian expression.

do: The supreme creative force in Candomblé, the father of all Orixás, purity and creation
at: Oxalá is the most revered Orixá in Brazilian Candomblé — the supreme creative force, the father of all the Orixás, the divine principle of purity and cosmic creation. He is identified with Jesus Christ in Candomblé syncretism but predates that identification by millennia in his African form (Obatala). His colour is white — absolute white.
of: White cloth, white food (white rice, coconut, white yam, white corn), cool water, silver imagery, the supreme silence of his ceremonies
an: White dove, white snail, the white ox
co: White (absolute white — the most sacred colour in Candomblé)
dp: Sunday and Friday — the supreme creator and the love
cr: White quartz, howlite, selenite, white moonstone
bp: Crown (the divine creator), bones (he sculpts bones), the head (the ori, the seat of the divine)
af: I create from the purest place within me. The white father of all creation moves through me. My crown is clear and bright.
sh: The rigidity of perfect purity — the supreme Orixá whose whiteness becomes refusal of the necessary complexity of life
ar: The Supreme Creator of Candomblé / The White Father
cb: Iemanjá (the ocean mother) counterbalances Oxalá (the supreme creator). Also Exu (the trickster, the dark, the earthly) counterbalances Oxalá (the pure, the light, the cosmic and divine).

do: The crossroads, communication, beginnings and endings, the guardian of all spaces between
at: Exu is the Brazilian Candomblé and Umbanda manifestation of Eshu/Elegua — the divine trickster and gatekeeper of all spaces between. In Brazil he has become more complex: in Umbanda he has a family of Exus and Pomba Giras (female Exus). He is the first and last in every ceremony. He is the divine face of the streets, the night, and the crossroads.
of: Cachaça (Brazilian rum), cigars, black and red offerings, Monday midnight ceremonies, offerings at the crossroads or the cemetery gate
an: Goat, rooster (like Eshu), the black cat
co: Black and red (his sacred colours in Candomblé)
dp: Monday / Mercury — the first and last
cr: Black tourmaline, red jasper, obsidian
bp: Feet (the crossroads walker), tongue (the divine trickster's speech), root (the foundation of all ceremony)
af: Exu opens the way. All paths before me are cleared. The divine trickster is my ally at every threshold.
sh: The Exu who blocks — the divine gatekeeper who refuses passage; the throat that cannot speak its truth
ar: The Brazilian Crossroads / The Night's Trickster
cb: Oxalá (the supreme purity, the divine light) counterbalances Exu (the earthly darkness, the trickster). The most fundamental Candomblé polarity: the white father and the black trickster.

do: Female sexuality, the night, the crossroads, the liberation of the oppressed feminine
at: Pomba Gira is the female manifestation of Exu in Brazilian Umbanda — the spirit of female sexuality, the night, and the crossroads. She governs all matters of love, sexuality, and the liberation of women who have been oppressed. She is the divine patroness of sex workers and all women who live outside conventional respectability.
of: Red and black roses, cachaça, cigarettes, perfume, mirrors, red candles — ceremonies at crossroads at midnight
an: Black cat (the night's companion), the owl, the black dove
co: Red and black (the colours of passion, the night and the crossroads)
dp: Monday and Friday — the night crossroads and the love
cr: Red garnet, black tourmaline, black pearl, red carnelian
bp: Sacral (the sexual liberation), the hips (the crossroads walk), the mouth (the truth spoken at the crossroads)
af: My sexuality is sacred. I walk my truth at the crossroads. No one controls what belongs to me.
sh: The untransformed Pomba Gira — the sexuality that remains in the shadow without being integrated into the full self
ar: The Sacred Feminine Crossroads / The Night's Liberatrix
cb: Exu (the male crossroads spirit — her consort and counterpart) counterbalances Pomba Gira (the female crossroads spirit). Male and female shadow at the crossroads.

do: Winter, storms, the cold, the ancient earth, the hag who shapes the landscape
at: The Cailleach (the Old Woman / the Hag) is one of the most ancient goddesses of Scotland and Ireland — the great winter goddess who brings the cold and storms, who shaped the Scottish Highlands by throwing boulders from her apron. She is older than any other divine being in the Celtic world. She alternates with Brigid — the two share sovereignty of the year.
of: Winter offerings, the honouring of cold as sacred, the respecting of storms, the acknowledgment that the winter is necessary for the spring
an: Deer, wolf, the cormorant (her favourite bird), the crow
co: White (the snow), grey (the winter sky), the deep brown of the ancient earth
dp: Saturday / Saturn — the old woman of winter
cr: Flint (the ancient stone), grey quartz, black tourmaline, the stones she threw to make the Highlands
bp: Root (the ancient earth she shaped), crown (the ancient divine she embodies), the hands (the boulders she threw)
af: The winter has its own sacred beauty. The ancient crone has shaped the landscape of my soul. I honour the cold as necessary.
sh: The endless winter — the Cailleach's shadow is the winter that refuses to yield to Brigid's spring
ar: The Winter Crone / The Ancient Earth Shaper
cb: Brigid (the spring warmth, the creative fire, the young feminine) counterbalances the Cailleach (the winter cold, the ancient earth, the crone). The supreme Celtic seasonal polarity — their alternation IS the year.

do: War, beauty, the peacock, the spear of wisdom (vel), youth, Tamil culture
at: Murugan (also known as Kartikeya or Skanda) is the son of Shiva and Parvati — the beautiful divine youth, the war god, the lord of the peacock. He is the most beloved deity in Tamil Nadu. His vel (the divine spear of wisdom) destroys ignorance. His temples (including the six sacred abodes Arupadaiveedu) are among the most visited in the world.
of: Peacock feathers, red flowers (especially red lotus), vel (spear) imagery, the Thaipusam festival, vel vel chanting
an: Peacock (his mount — the bird that eats serpents / ignorance), rooster (his banner), elephant
co: Red (his sacred colour in Tamil tradition), gold
dp: Tuesday / Mars — his sacred day in Tamil Nadu
cr: Red coral (the most sacred stone for Murugan), carnelian, ruby
bp: Solar plexus (the warrior's strength), throat (the sacred Tamil language is his gift), root (the vel that penetrates the earth)
af: The vel of wisdom destroys the ignorance within me. I am the divine youth who never ages. My beauty is the beauty of the god of war who is also the god of beauty.
sh: The beautiful warrior who becomes vain — the divine youth whose beauty and power make him distant from ordinary human suffering
ar: The Divine Youth / The Beautiful War God
cb: Ganesha (his brother — the obstacle-remover, the first-worshipped, the elder) counterbalances Murugan (the second-born, the warrior, the beautiful). Wisdom (Ganesha) and action (Murugan).

do: Fertility, the earth, the devadasi tradition, smallpox, the outcaste divine feminine
at: Yellamma (Ellamma) is the supreme goddess of the Renuke-Yellamma tradition — she is depicted as a head without a body, yet full of power. She is the patron goddess of the devadasi (temple dancers, sacred sex workers) and of the Dalit and outcaste communities. She governs fertility, disease and the earth, and is worshipped most fervently by those who have the least power in the caste system.
of: Red and yellow turmeric, the devadasi offering (the dedication of oneself to the goddess's service), red flowers, neem leaves
an: Donkey (her sacred mount — the most humble and despised animal, made sacred), cobra
co: Red (her fierce power), yellow (turmeric), green (neem)
dp: Tuesday / Mars and Monday
cr: Red coral, red jasper, turmeric crystal
bp: Root (the earth and the body that belongs to the earth), sacral (the creative and sexual power of the devadasi)
af: The most marginalised divine is also the most powerful. The goddess who is only a head needs no body to hold her power. I honour the divine in those the world has cast aside.
sh: The devadasi system's exploitation — the sacred tradition used to justify the exploitation of the women who serve her
ar: The Headless Power / The Goddess of the Marginalised
cb: Shiva (the supreme divine of the Brahminical tradition that marginalised her devotees) counterbalances Yellamma (the supreme divine of the Dalit and outcaste tradition). The high-caste divine counterbalanced by the low-caste divine — the most important spiritual polarity in Indian society.

do: The potter, the creator of human bodies on his wheel, the Nile's source, the ram-headed creator
at: Khnum is the ram-headed god of the Nile's source — the divine potter who shapes each human body and soul on his potter's wheel before placing them in the mother's womb. He governed the annual Nile flood that renewed Egypt's agricultural land. He is the divine that personally forms each individual body.
of: Clay offerings shaped on a wheel, offerings of pottery, the Nile honoured, prayers for healthy pregnancies
an: Ram (his head is a ram's head), the frog (associated with the Nile flood)
co: Blue-green (the Nile's colour), red (the clay of his wheel)
dp: Saturday / Earth and Moon
cr: Red jasper (the clay of his wheel), blue-green fluorite (the Nile's colour), carnelian
bp: The womb (he shapes the soul to be placed there), sacral (the creative potter's wheel), root (the Nile's foundation)
af: My body was personally shaped by the divine potter. I am an individual creation, made with care. The divine took time to form me specifically.
sh: The clay that is shaped without the person's consent — the fate determined before birth
ar: The Divine Potter / The Creator of Each Body
cb: Hapy (the god of the Nile flood — the water that makes Khnum's clay workable) counterbalances Khnum (the potter who shapes the clay). Water and earth, the flood and the form.

do: The Nile, crocodiles, military power, fertility, the pharaoh's protection
at: Sobek — the crocodile god — governed the Nile, military prowess, and the pharaoh's strength. Crocodiles were kept in his temples. He is both feared and revered — the crocodile that devours and the Nile that gives life. He is the root chakra's most primally dangerous earth-water power: the ancient reptile force.
of: Crocodile imagery, the Nile honoured, military offerings, the Lake Moeris temple complex (where sacred crocodiles were kept and fed by priests)
an: Crocodile (he IS the crocodile), falcon (in his solar aspect as Sobek-Ra)
co: Green (the Nile's fertile green), gold (the solar crocodile)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the military power
cr: Green feldspar, crocodile-green amazonite, gold, jade
bp: Root (the primal reptile power), solar plexus (the military strength), the jaw (the crocodile's weapon)
af: The ancient power of the Nile runs through me. The crocodile's patience is the greatest predator's skill. I wait with perfect stillness for the right moment.
sh: The crocodile's snap — the solar plexus that strikes without patience, the military power used without the wisdom of the Nile's long patience
ar: The Nile Crocodile / The Military Protector
cb: Ra (the sun — Sobek-Ra is the combined solar crocodile) counterbalances Sobek (the Nile crocodile in its watery, earthly form). Water and fire, the Nile and the sun, in their Egyptian solar-aquatic polarity.

do: Male fertility, the harvest, the eastern desert road, sexual potency
at: Min is one of the oldest Egyptian gods — his cult at Coptos predates the pharaonic period. He is depicted as an ithyphallic figure holding his flail, wearing a double-plumed crown. He is the god of male fertility, sexual potency, the harvest, and the desert road. The lettuce plant (a Viagra of antiquity) was sacred to him.
of: Lettuce (his most sacred offering), the first harvest of lettuce, festivals of athletic competitions in his honour
an: Bull (the masculine fertility animal), the white bull in particular, the bull bee
co: Black (his skin colour in Egyptian art — the black of fertile Nile soil)
dp: Thursday / Venus — the masculine fertility
cr: Black tourmaline (his black skin), carnelian (the masculine sexual force)
bp: The phallus (the most explicitly ithyphallic Egyptian god), sacral plexus, root
af: My masculine creative force is sacred. The sexual energy within me is divine and life-giving. I am fertile in all my creative endeavours.
sh: The masculine sexual force without the sacred covenant — the phallus as purely procreative tool without the divine relationship
ar: The Ithyphallic Creator / The Harvest's Masculine Power
cb: Isis (the feminine creative principle) counterbalances Min (the masculine creative principle). The phallus and the womb in their most explicitly Egyptian sacred polarity.

do: The cobra goddess, the protectress of Lower Egypt, the Eye of Ra, the royal uraeus
at: Wadjet ('the green one') is one of the oldest Egyptian goddesses — the cobra goddess of Lower Egypt, protectress of the pharaoh. She is the cobra on the royal crown (the uraeus) that rears up to spit venom at the pharaoh's enemies. She is the Eye of Ra in its most ancient expression. She governs the kundalini energy in its most Egyptian expression.
of: Cobra imagery, solar ceremonies, prayers for protection of one's sovereign space, papyrus (the plant of Lower Egypt, her sacred plant)
an: Cobra (she IS the cobra — the most sacred serpent of Egypt), vulture (paired with Nekhbet)
co: Green (the green cobra), gold (the royal crown she adorns)
dp: Sunday / Mars — the solar protective fire
cr: Green serpentine (her green colour), lapis lazuli (the royal stone she protects), gold
bp: Third eye (the uraeus sits at the third eye on the crown), solar plexus (the fire she spits), the spine (the rising serpent)
af: The cobra of my third eye rises to protect what is sacred. My sovereign space cannot be violated. The fire of the divine feminine rises from my root to my crown.
sh: The venom without wisdom — the protective fire that strikes at allies as well as enemies, the kundalini force without discernment
ar: The Royal Cobra / The Third Eye Fire
cb: Nekhbet (the vulture goddess of Upper Egypt — Wadjet's complement and counterpart) counterbalances Wadjet (the cobra goddess of Lower Egypt). The Two Ladies of Egypt — the supreme Egyptian feminine polarity: the vulture and the cobra.

do: The underworld, mummification, the weighing of the heart, the guide of the dead
at: Anubis — the jackal-headed god — is the divine embalmer, the guardian of the scales at the weighing of the heart, and the guide of souls through the underworld. He invented mummification. He is the most recognisable Egyptian god in the modern world and one of the most ancient. He is the root chakra's most sophisticated death-wisdom: the one who preserves the body so the soul can continue its journey.
of: The mummification process honoured (embalming as sacred art), incense (to preserve the body's memory), the jackal as sacred, black candles, prayers for the dead
an: Jackal (he is the jackal-headed god), dog
co: Black (the colour of mummification, of fertile Nile soil, of preserved bodies), gold (the funerary gold)
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the underworld god
cr: Obsidian (the black of preservation), black tourmaline, black onyx, dark lapis
bp: Root (the body preserved in the earth), third eye (the witness at the weighing of the heart), the hands (the embalmer's sacred hands)
af: My body is sacred enough to be preserved for the soul's journey. The jackal watches over my transitions. I am guided through my darkest passages.
sh: The embalmer's obsession — the preservation that refuses natural decay, the holding-on beyond what is sacred
ar: The Divine Embalmer / The Guide of the Dead
cb: Osiris (the king of the underworld — to whom Anubis presents the souls) counterbalances Anubis (the guide who brings the souls to Osiris). The presenter counterbalanced by the receiver. The guide and the judge.

do: The wind, breath, the creative force of air, the aspect of Quetzalcoatl that breathes life into humanity
at: Ehecatl is the wind aspect of Quetzalcoatl — the god who breathed life into humanity. He swept the primordial waters apart to make way for the sun and moon. His temples are round so the wind moves freely around them. He is the breath that gives life.
of: Wind offerings, prayers spoken into the wind, round temples honoured, the breath itself as sacred offering, cedar incense
an: Hummingbird (the fastest wind creature)
co: Black (his mask), white (the breath's purity)
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the wind of sacred communication
cr: Black obsidian (his mask), clear quartz, celestite (the wind's stone)
bp: Lungs, throat (the breath of life), mouth
af: Every breath I take is the gift of Ehecatl. The wind speaks through me. My words are the living breath of creation.
sh: The wind that scatters — the throat that speaks without direction, the breath wasted in pointless speech
ar: The Breath of Life / The Wind Creator
cb: Tlaloc (the rain — water that comes on the wind) counterbalances Ehecatl (the wind that carries the rain). Also Tezcatlipoca (the smoke) counterbalances Ehecatl (the clear wind of creative breath).

do: The sun itself, the fifth age of the world, the demanding solar force that requires feeding
at: Tonatiuh is the Aztec sun god at the centre of the famous Aztec Sun Stone. He is the demanding sun who requires human hearts to sustain his daily journey. He is the solar plexus's most unapologetic expression of power requiring sacrifice.
of: Red candles, solar ceremonies at noon, offerings of one's highest energy and effort dedicated to the sun
an: Eagle (the solar warrior bird), jaguar (his nocturnal twin)
co: Red, gold, the brilliant orange of the midday sun
dp: Sunday / Sun
cr: Gold, sunstone, fire opal, red jasper
bp: Solar plexus, the heart, the eyes (the sun that sees all)
af: I sustain my solar journey with the offering of my highest effort. I feed the sun within me with my most alive moments.
sh: The sun that consumes — the solar plexus that demands sacrifice from others rather than offering its own effort
ar: The Demanding Sun / The Fifth Age
cb: Metztli (the moon — the night when Tonatiuh descends underground) counterbalances Tonatiuh (the sun of the day). Sun and moon in their Aztec expression.

do: The lord of the dead, the underworld (Mictlan), the nine levels of the underworld journey
at: Mictlantecuhtli is the Aztec lord of the dead — depicted as a skeletal figure decorated with eyeballs, his ribs exposed. The dead must travel through nine levels of Mictlan over four years before reaching his realm. He is not evil but necessary — the inevitable final destination of those who die ordinary deaths.
of: Offerings on Día de los Muertos (November 1-2), food and drink left for the deceased, skeleton imagery, marigolds (cempasúchil — the flower of the dead)
an: Owl (the death bird), spider, bat
co: Black (the underworld's darkness), white (bone), gold (the marigold)
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the lord of the final realm
cr: Obsidian, jet, dark tourmaline
el: Earth (the deepest earth — nine levels of Mictlan)
bp: The entire body at death, the bones, the root chakra
af: I honour the dead and I honour my own mortality. The nine levels of the underworld are a journey, not a trap. I face the inevitable with dignity.
sh: The underworld that cannot be escaped — the four-year journey through Mictlan's obstacles
ar: The Lord of the Dead / The Nine-Level Journey
cb: Quetzalcoatl (who descended to Mictlan to gather the bones of the previous creation and bring life back) counterbalances Mictlantecuhtli (the lord who keeps the dead). The life-bringer counterbalanced by the death-keeper.

do: Lightning, the dog, the guide of the dead through the underworld, the deformed divine twin
at: Xolotl is the dog-headed Aztec deity who guides the dead through the underworld's rivers. The xoloitzcuintli (hairless dog) is his sacred animal. He is also the twin of Quetzalcoatl — the deformed twin, the monstrous one to the feathered serpent's beauty. He governs deformity, twins, and the sacred role of the unusual.
of: The xoloitzcuintli dog honoured (these sacred hairless dogs were buried with owners to guide them), lightning respected, care for those with physical differences
an: Xoloitzcuintli (the hairless dog — the most sacred dog in the world to the Aztec), the salamander
co: Black (the underworld's colour), red (the lightning), white (the hairless dog's skin)
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the underworld guide
cr: Obsidian (the underworld's stone), black tourmaline
bp: Root (the deepest underground guide), the feet (the walker who guides), the spine (the lightning bolt)
af: In my darkest passage through the underworld, my guide walks beside me. Even the most deformed and rejected have sacred purpose. I am guided through.
sh: The guide who runs from his purpose — Xolotl was said to have fled when asked to sacrifice himself in the final creation
ar: The Underworld Guide / The Sacred Deformed Twin
cb: Quetzalcoatl (the beautiful, the feathered, the elevated twin) counterbalances Xolotl (the deformed, the hairless, the underground twin). The supreme Aztec twin polarity: beauty and deformity, sky and underworld.

do: The earth, childbirth, the serpent woman, the patroness of those who die in childbirth
at: Cihuacoatl ('Serpent Woman') is one of the oldest and most important Aztec goddesses — the earth in its most primal, raw, maternal form. She wears a skull mask and carries a cradle (with a flint knife inside instead of a baby). She patronises women who die in childbirth — who are honoured as warriors.
of: Offerings to women who died in childbirth, the honouring of all births as sacred battlefields, midwives' ceremonies in her honour
an: Serpent (her name), eagle, owl
co: White and black (the colours of her skull and her robes)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — childbirth as warfare
cr: Obsidian, black tourmaline, flint
bp: Womb (childbirth), root (the earth she governs), the hands (midwifery)
af: Childbirth is warfare and I am a warrior. Every woman who dies in childbirth is elevated to the stars. The earth receives us all.
sh: The dangerous Cihuateteo — the ghosts of women who died in childbirth that return to steal children at crossroads
ar: The Warrior Mother / The Serpent Woman of Childbirth
cb: Xipe Totec (renewal through shedding) counterbalances Cihuacoatl (female transformation through childbirth's ordeal). The male and female initiation through the body's most extreme experiences.

do: The supreme Mayan deity, lord of the heavens, inventor of writing and medicine
at: Itzamná is the supreme deity of the Classic Maya — lord of the heavens and of day and night, the patron of knowledge, writing and medicine. He is the first shaman and the inventor of the Mayan script. Often depicted as an old man with sunken cheeks. The husband of Ixchel. He is the crown chakra in its most complete Mayan expression.
of: Copal incense (the sacred Maya incense), offerings at the sacred ceiba tree (the world tree), the writing of sacred texts as offering, cacao
an: Iguana (associated with the cosmic iguana), hummingbird, serpent
co: Blue-green (the Maya sacred colour of the heavens and jade), gold
dp: Sunday / Mercury — the supreme and the inventor of writing
cr: Jade (the Maya's most sacred stone), lapis lazuli, blue-green fluorite
bp: Crown (the supreme divine), third eye (the inventor of writing), the hands
af: The supreme divine of the Maya created the tools of knowledge. I use writing as a sacred act. My knowledge connects the levels of creation.
sh: The supreme who withholds knowledge — the deity who invented writing and medicine but whose knowledge is not shared with all
ar: The Mayan Supreme / The Inventor of Knowledge
cb: Ah Puch (the god of death) counterbalances Itzamná (the god of knowledge and life). Life and death, the supreme above and the lord below.

do: The feathered serpent of the Maya, the wind, the descent of the sun, the cosmic axis
at: Kukulkan is the Mayan equivalent of Quetzalcoatl — the feathered serpent and cosmic axis. At the spring and autumn equinoxes, the shadow at the great pyramid of Chichén Itzá creates the image of a serpent descending the steps — Kukulkan's descent. He governs the sacred calendar, the wind, and the cosmic order.
of: The equinox ceremony at Chichén Itzá, offerings of jade and copal, the feathered serpent honoured in ceremony, the sacred Mayan calendar kept
an: Quetzal bird (the feathered), serpent (the earthly)
co: Green (quetzal feathers), gold, turquoise
dp: Wednesday / Venus (as the morning star)
cr: Jade, turquoise, obsidian, quetzal feather
bp: Spine (the cosmic axis — the full kundalini pathway), crown (the feather rises), root (the serpent descends)
af: I am the axis between heaven and earth. The serpent and the bird live in my spine. My ascending energy is sacred and unstoppable.
sh: Tezcatlipoca (who drove Kukulkan into exile by showing him his shadow in the obsidian mirror) — the self that cannot face its own darkness
ar: The Mayan Feathered Serpent / The Cosmic Axis
cb: Ah Puch (the Mayan death god) counterbalances Kukulkan (the wind and life). Also Chaac (the rain god) counterbalances Kukulkan (the cosmic principle).

do: Rain, lightning, the four sacred directions, agriculture's nourishment
at: Chaac is the Mayan rain god — one of the most important deities for the agricultural civilisations of Mesoamerica. He wields a stone axe that creates lightning and brings rain. He is divided into four aspects, one for each cardinal direction and its associated colour.
of: Rain ceremonies, offerings of copal and jade, the honouring of the four directions, prayers before planting
an: Frog (the rain animal — the croaking of frogs announces rain), turtle, serpent
co: Blue (the rain), the four sacred Mayan colours: red (east), white (north), black (west), yellow (south)
dp: Monday / Moon — the rain's sacred governance
cr: Jade (the Maya's rain stone), turquoise, blue-green fluorite
bp: Sacral (the water of creative fertility), the mouth (he cries rain as he strikes with his axe), hands
af: The rain of blessing falls on my life. I open to what nourishes from above. The sky's gift waters the earth of my body.
sh: The drought — when Chaac withholds the rain, the corn dies and the people starve
ar: The Life-Giving Rain / The Four-Directional Nourisher
cb: Itzamná (the supreme above who commands the rain) counterbalances Chaac (the practical rain-bringer). Also Tlaloc (the Aztec rain god) mirrors Chaac across the Mesoamerican cultural divide.

do: The sky, the wind, kingship, the law, the commanding sky father who sent the flood
at: Enlil is the great sky god of the Sumerian pantheon — the commander of the gods, the lord of the wind, the one who decreed the great flood. He is both provider and destroyer — he gave humanity the gift of the plow (civilisation) and also sent the flood that nearly wiped them out. He is the crown chakra's most authoritarian expression.
of: The wind honoured as his breath, ceremonies of kingship and law, the passing of laws as sacred acts dedicated to cosmic order
an: Bull (the cosmic bull of heaven), eagle, the storm bird (Anzu)
co: Sky blue, deep lapis, the grey of storm clouds
dp: Thursday / Jupiter — the commanding sky king
cr: Lapis lazuli (the sky's sacred stone), blue kyanite, imperial topaz
bp: Crown (the commanding divine authority), solar plexus (the king's commanding will), lungs (the wind he governs)
af: I command my own inner cosmos with wisdom and restraint. My authority is earned, not assumed. The law I live by serves life.
sh: The flood — Enlil decreed the destruction of humanity when their noise disturbed his sleep. The shadow of authority is the power to destroy what you created.
ar: The Commanding Sky Father / The Law-Giver
cb: Enki (the clever water god who constantly outmanoeuvres Enlil to protect humanity) counterbalances Enlil (the rigid sky law). The supreme Sumerian polarity: law and compassion, sky and water, authority and wit.

do: The supreme sky deity of the Altai Siberian peoples, the light above, the creator
at: Ülgen is the supreme light deity of the Altai, Yakut and related Siberian peoples — the divine principle of the sky, light and creation. He lives in the seventeen heavens. The shaman (kam) travels to his realm in the upper world during ceremony.
of: The shamanic drum journey to the upper world, offerings of white horses, white offerings, prayers spoken upward
an: Eagle (the upper world bird), swan (the bird that bridges worlds), white horse
co: White, blue, the gold of the highest heaven
dp: Sunday — the supreme sky's day
cr: Clear quartz, white calcite, meteorite
bp: Crown, the entire being in the upward trance of the shamanic journey
af: The shaman within me knows the way to the upper world. I drum my way to the highest heaven. The light of Ülgen touches everything I am.
sh: The heights that cannot be reached — the seventeen heavens that require extraordinary effort; the crown chakra of the ordinary person who cannot make the journey
ar: The Altai Supreme / The Seventeen Heavens
cb: Erlik Khan (the lord of the lower world — the dark counterpart of Ülgen) counterbalances Ülgen (the lord of the upper world). Light above and dark below — the most fundamental Altai Siberian cosmological polarity.

do: The supreme sky deity of the Nenets people, the seven heavens, the creator of the living world
at: Num-Torum is the supreme sky deity of the Nenets people of the Arctic tundra — he created the living world and governs the seven heavens. He is the divine principle of the sky above the permafrost. The Nenets shamans (tadibey) journey to his realm.
of: The sacred song offered to the sky (the Nenets' most important offering is the sung prayer), white reindeer sacrifices, offerings from the yurt's sacred corner
an: Reindeer (the most sacred animal of all Arctic peoples — Num-Torum's gift), swan, eagle
co: White (the snow, the divine), the pale blue of the Arctic sky
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: Clear quartz, Arctic ice crystal, white calcite
el: Air / Cosmic Consciousness / Ice (the element of the Arctic divine)
bp: Crown, the entire being raised in the vast Arctic sky
af: The sky above the permafrost is my crown. The supreme who created the tundra sees me. My song rises to the seven heavens.
sh: The Arctic silence that swallows — the supreme so remote, the sky so vast, the tundra so empty that the individual disappears
ar: The Arctic Sky Father / The Seven Heavens
cb: Nga (the earth spirit of the underworld, the dark counterpart of Num-Torum) counterbalances Num-Torum (the supreme sky). The sky above the permafrost counterbalanced by the spirits below it.

do: The moon, the regulator of time, the sacred feminine of the Inca cosmic order
at: Mama Quilla ('Mother Moon') is the Inca moon goddess — the regulator of time and the calendar, the protector of women and of the Qoya (the Inca queen). She is the consort of Inti (the sun). Lunar eclipses were seen as a serpent or puma attacking her — people would shout to drive the attacker away.
of: Silver offerings (the moon's metal), the lunar calendar honoured, the Coya Raymi festival (September festival of the moon), the Qoya's prayers to Mama Quilla for protection
an: Condor (the messenger between moon and earth), the silver fish
co: Silver (the moon's metal), white, the pale blue of the mountain moonlight
dp: Monday / Moon — she IS the moon
cr: Moonstone, silver, white quartz, selenite
el: Water / Air (the moon governs tides and the mountain waters)
bp: Third eye (the moon's reflective wisdom), sacral (the lunar cycle's regulation of fertility), crown (the cosmic time keeper)
af: I live by the moon's sacred time. My cycles are regulated by the silver light. The mother moon protects all feminine life.
sh: The lunar eclipse — the moon attacked by darkness. The feminine principle under assault from the forces that would swallow her light.
ar: The Inca Moon Mother / The Sacred Time Keeper
cb: Inti (the sun — her divine consort) counterbalances Mama Quilla (the moon). The Inca's supreme cosmic polarity: gold and silver, sun and moon, masculine and feminine, day and night.

do: The sacred Odu — the 256 signs of Ifa, the divine library of all human destinies
at: Orunmila in his deepest aspect — the keeper of the 256 Odu (sacred signs of the Ifa divination system). Each Odu is a complete universe of stories, medicines, ceremonies and wisdom. Together the 256 Odu are said to contain everything that has happened, is happening, and will happen.
of: The full Ifa initiation (to receive one's own Odu), the study of the Odu corpus with a babalawo, the palm nuts (ikin) used in divination, the Opon Ifa (divination tray) as sacred object
an: Chameleon (who sees in all directions — who sees all 256 Odu simultaneously), the parrot
co: Green and yellow — but each of the 256 Odu has its own colours
dp: Thursday / Jupiter — the expansion of cosmic wisdom
cr: The palm nuts (ikin) themselves are the most sacred object, green and yellow stones
el: Ether / Cosmic Consciousness — the 256 Odu span all elements
bp: Third eye (the divination sight), crown (the cosmic architecture), the hands of the diviner
af: My destiny is one of the 256 sacred signs. The entire architecture of existence is encoded in my life. I surrender to the Odu that is mine.
sh: The misuse of the Odu — using the sacred divination system for manipulation rather than illumination
ar: The 256 Sacred Signs / The Library of All Destinies
cb: Eshu/Elegua (the trickster who can redirect what the Odu has determined) counterbalances the 256 Odu (the fixed sacred signs). Free will and fate in their most sophisticated West African theological expression.

do: Single combat, law, justice, oaths, the sacrifice of the hand for the good of all
at: Tyr is the Norse god of justice, single combat and law. When the gods needed to bind the Fenrir wolf, only Tyr would put his hand in the wolf's mouth as a pledge of faith (knowing Fenrir would bite it off when the binding held). He sacrificed his hand for the safety of all. Tuesday (Tyr's day) is named for him.
of: Oaths sworn on his name, single combat ceremonies, the acknowledgment of sacrifices made for justice, the Tiwaz rune (↑) meditated upon
an: None — Tyr is the pure principle of justice without an animal
co: Red and gold (the warrior's justice), white (the oath)
dp: Tuesday (Tyr's day — Tuesday is named for Tyr/Tiw) / Mars
cr: Red jasper (justice and war), bloodstone (the sacrifice), iron
bp: Solar plexus (the commanding justice), right hand (which he sacrificed), throat (the oath spoken)
af: I keep my word even at great personal cost. The sacrifice made for justice is worth making. My integrity is more important than my safety.
sh: The hand bitten off — the sacrifice of integrity for safety, the refusal to put one's hand in the wolf's mouth when needed
ar: The One-Handed Justice / The God of Sacred Oaths
cb: Odin (the wisdom that works through manipulation and strategy) counterbalances Tyr (the justice that works through direct sacrifice and oath). The clever god and the just god in their Norse polarity.

do: Poetry, eloquence, the divine skald, the honey of sacred speech
at: Bragi is the Norse god of poetry and eloquence — the divine skald whose tongue has runes carved on it. He welcomes the newly dead heroes to Valhalla with his poetry. He is the husband of Iðunn (the goddess of the apples of eternal youth).
of: Poetry composed and spoken aloud as offering, the skaldic tradition honoured, the first drink offered with a poem rather than a toast, the composition of praise poetry for those who deserve it
an: Swan (the poet's bird), eagle
co: Gold, the deep blue of the skald's mantle
dp: Wednesday / Venus — the poet's divine inspiration
cr: Amber (the fossilised honey of speech), citrine, lapis lazuli
bp: Tongue (runes are carved on it), throat (the sacred speech), heart (the poetry that moves hearts)
af: I speak with the tongue on which runes are carved. My words are worthy of being remembered. I compose the poem that honours what deserves honouring.
sh: The skald who exaggerates for payment — the court poet who praises the unworthy for gold. The sacred word commercialised.
ar: The Divine Poet / The Rune-Tongued Skald
cb: Iðunn (his wife — the goddess of eternal youth whom he must protect) counterbalances Bragi (the eloquent poet who cannot always protect what he loves).

do: The apples of youth, the regeneration of the gods, the sacred feminine of eternal renewal
at: Iðunn is the keeper of the apples of eternal youth — without whom the Aesir gods would grow old and die. She is the most essential and most vulnerable of all the Norse goddesses — she was stolen by the giant Þjazi (and recovered by Loki in eagle form). She is the sacral chakra's most precious renewable resource.
of: Apples offered as sacred symbols of renewal, the first apple of the harvest honoured, the eternal youth within oneself cultivated through joy and pleasure
an: Eagle (Þjazi came in eagle form to steal her; Loki came in eagle form to rescue her — she is bookended by eagles)
co: Green (the apple's colour), gold (the golden apples), the fresh colours of spring
dp: Friday / Venus and Sunday — the renewal and the eternal youth
cr: Green aventurine, apple green calcite, gold topaz
bp: Sacral (the life-force she carries), heart (the love that protects her), root (the apple tree's roots)
af: I carry the apples of my own eternal renewal. My joy is the sacred medicine that keeps the divine alive within me. I am protected and protecting.
sh: The stolen Iðunn — the sacred source of renewal that is taken away. The gods without their apples grow old.
ar: The Apple of Eternal Youth / The Keeper of Divine Renewal
cb: Þjazi (the giant who stole her) counterbalances Iðunn (the keeper of eternal renewal). Also Bragi (her husband who cannot always protect what he loves) counterbalances Iðunn (the precious one who needs protecting).

do: The sovereignty of Britain, the high places, healing wells, victory, the tribal goddess of the Brigantes
at: Brigantia is the great goddess of the Brigantes tribe (northern England and southern Scotland) — the divine queen of the high places. She governs victory, healing waters, and the sacred sovereignty of the land. She was syncretised with the Roman Minerva and Victoria. Brigid's northern British counterpart.
of: Offerings at the healing wells of northern Britain (particularly in Yorkshire and Northumbria), prayers for sovereignty and victory, the honouring of high places
an: Owl (Minerva's bird), the goose, the ram
co: Gold and red (the queen's colours), the deep blue of the northern British sky
dp: Wednesday / Minerva — the wisdom of the sovereign
cr: Carnelian (victory), blue lace agate (the healing wells), amber
bp: Crown (the sovereignty of the highest places), solar plexus (the queen's victory), throat (the proclamation of sovereignty)
af: I stand on the high places and claim my sovereignty. The healing waters of Britain flow through me. I am the queen of my own high ground.
sh: The sovereignty in exile — Brigantia syncretised into Roman Minerva and losing her specific character
ar: The High Sovereignty / The Queen of the High Places
cb: The Morrigan (the Irish sovereignty goddess — dark, death-associated, the crow queen) counterbalances Brigantia (the British sovereignty goddess — elevated, light-associated). Two sovereignty goddesses in their dark and light polarity.

do: The horse goddess, the provider, the guardian of cavalry and stables, the sacred journey
at: Epona is the Gaulish horse goddess — the only Celtic deity to receive a Roman official feast day. She was adopted by the Roman cavalry and carried across the entire Roman Empire. She rides a white mare or stands between two horses, holding a cornucopia. She governs journeys, the earth's abundance, and the sacred bond between human and horse.
of: Roses (her sacred flower — rose garlands were placed on stable doors), offerings of grain, apples and honey to horses, the blessing of horses before long journeys
an: Horse (she IS the horse goddess — depicted riding sidesaddle on a white mare), eagle, dog
co: White (the white mare), gold and rose (her Roman festival colours)
dp: Friday / Venus and Sunday — the beauty of the journey and the sun
cr: Rose quartz (her rose offerings), amber, carnelian
el: Earth / Air (the running horse spans both)
bp: Root (the horse's connection to the earth), heart (the love between rider and horse), the legs (the movement of the journey)
af: I am accompanied on every journey. The horse goddess blesses my movement. I travel with grace and provision.
sh: The rider who doesn't honour the horse — the cavalry that uses the horse without gratitude
ar: The Horse Goddess / The Journey Provider
cb: Cernunnos (the wild nature deity — the forest as the natural home the horse left when domesticated) counterbalances Epona (the domesticated horse, the sacred partnership). Wild nature counterbalanced by the sacred partnership.

do: The sacred hot spring, healing, the curse tablets thrown into her waters, solar spring justice
at: Sulis is the goddess of the hot spring at Bath (Roman Aquae Sulis) — one of the most important sacred sites in Britain. Her hot spring was considered a direct connection to the underworld. Thousands of 'curse tablets' — requests for justice — were thrown into her waters. She is a solar spring goddess: the sun's heat in the earth, rising as hot water.
of: Offerings thrown into healing springs (coins in wishing wells descend from her worship), prayers for justice, the hot spring honoured as the earth's sun-warmed gift
an: Owl (the Roman Minerva syncretised with her), the sacred fish of the spring
co: Gold (the solar heat), blue-green (the spring water), the white of the sacred steam
dp: Sunday / Mercury — the solar spring and the justice tablet
cr: Gold (her solar aspect), aquamarine (the spring water), turquoise
el: Fire / Water — the hot spring is the union of fire and water
bp: Heart (the healing she offers), root (the earth's depth from which her waters come), crown (the solar principle in the earth)
af: I throw my request for justice into the sacred spring. The earth's heat rises to heal me. The hot water carries both my offering and my prayer.
sh: The curse that boomerangs — the Sulis curse tablets called down illness on thieves. Justice-seeking that harms the seeker.
ar: The Sacred Hot Spring / The Solar Justice Well
cb: The Morrigan (the cold, dark feminine divine) counterbalances Sulis (the warm, healing, spring feminine divine). The battlefield and the healing spring.

do: Thunder, the wheel, sovereignty, the sky's commanding force
at: Taranis ('Thunder') is the Gaulish thunder god — one of the three most important Gaulish deities mentioned by the Roman poet Lucan. He carries the wheel (the wheel of thunder, the spinning disc of the sun) and the thunderbolt. He is the Celtic thunder father: the commanding sky principle that makes and unmakes kings.
of: The wheel as sacred symbol in ceremony, storms honoured as his presence, royal ceremonies acknowledging the thunder's mandate
an: Eagle (the sky bird), bull (the power of the thunder)
co: Gold (the wheel), deep blue-grey (the storm cloud), silver (the lightning)
dp: Thursday / Jupiter — the thunder king
cr: Gold (the wheel), dark labradorite, meteorite (from the sky)
bp: Solar plexus (the commanding thunder force), crown (the sovereignty of the sky), the arms (spinning the wheel)
af: The thunder of the sky speaks through me. My wheel turns with purpose. The commanding sky force moves me toward my sovereign purpose.
sh: The wheel that crushes — Taranis's shadow is the wheel of the sky that rolls over those beneath it without mercy
ar: The Thunder Wheel / The Gaulish Sky King
cb: The Dagda (the earth father, the grounded abundance) counterbalances Taranis (the sky father, the commanding thunder). Earth and sky in their Gaulish polarity.

do: Self-decapitation, the sacrifice of the ego, the feeding of others with one's own essence
at: Chinnamasta ('She whose head is severed') is one of the ten Mahavidyas — she is depicted standing on a copulating couple, holding her own severed head, with three streams of blood flowing from her neck: two feeding her two attendants, one flowing into her own severed mouth. She represents the supreme paradox: she decapitates herself to feed others, and she continues to live. The ego decapitated feeds what the ego cannot.
of: The deepest meditation on ego-death, the Mahavidya practice (after initiation), red offerings, the willingness to ask: what would I give if I gave everything — including my identity?
an: Swan (wisdom), the serpent (the energy that remains after decapitation)
co: Red (the blood she feeds), gold (the divine light of the decapitated wisdom)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the fierce self-sacrifice
cr: Red garnet, obsidian, clear quartz
bp: Crown (the severed head — the ego released), root (she stands on desire), solar plexus (the power of self-sacrifice)
af: I give from what I am. The ego I sacrifice feeds what my ego could not nourish. I remain alive without the head I thought I needed.
sh: The martyr who demands recognition — the self-sacrifice that quietly expects to be noticed. Chinnamasta's shadow is the decapitation that still wants to be praised.
ar: The Self-Sacrifice That Feeds / The Decapitated Wisdom
cb: Bhairavi (the fierce goddess of solar fire — the power that sustains rather than sacrifices) counterbalances Chinnamasta (the goddess who sacrifices rather than sustains).

do: The yoni goddess, menstrual blood as sacred, desire, tantric power, the supreme feminine creative
at: Kamakhya is one of the most important Shakta goddesses in India — she is the goddess of desire and the most important Tantric site in the world (the Kamakhya temple in Assam houses her sacred yoni, which bleeds each year). During Ambubachi Mela (June), the goddess is considered to be menstruating — the temple is closed for four days and reopened when the bleeding stops. Menstrual blood is honoured as the most sacred substance.
of: The Ambubachi Mela pilgrimage (June), red cloth offered during the goddess's menstruation period, offerings of hibiscus and bel leaves, the honouring of menstrual blood as sacred
an: Pigeon (her sacred bird at the Kamakhya temple), the fish
co: Red (menstrual blood — the most sacred colour at this temple), the deep earth colours of Assam
dp: Monday / Moon — the lunar goddess whose menstruation governs the sacred calendar
cr: Red garnet, red coral, blood jasper, ruby
bp: Sacral plexus (the yoni, the seat of feminine creative power), root (the earth's deepest feminine power), crown (the Tantric realisation that the body's most 'impure' is the most sacred)
af: My body's cycles are sacred, not shameful. The most powerful creative force in the universe is in my womb. What the world calls impure, the goddess calls most holy.
sh: The sacred taboo weaponised — Kamakhya's shadow is the menstrual prohibition used against women rather than honoured as the goddess's own bleeding
ar: The Yoni Goddess / The Sacred Menstruation
cb: Shiva (who lies beneath Kamakhya's yoni in Tantric iconography) counterbalances Kamakhya (the supreme yoni goddess). The absolute masculine counterbalanced by the absolute feminine — but she is on top.

do: The paralysis of enemies, the restraint of harmful speech, the victory over those who speak falsehood
at: Bagalamukhi ('the crane-faced one') is one of the ten Mahavidyas — she is the goddess who paralyses. She is depicted pulling the tongue of a demon, stopping his speech and thereby his power. She is the divine force that stops harmful speech, freezes the enemy in their tracks, and ensures that truth defeats falsehood.
of: Yellow offerings (her sacred colour), prayers in yellow cloth, haldi (turmeric) as sacred offering, the Bagalamukhi yantra meditated upon for protection from slander
an: Crane (the stork/crane is her bird — its stillness before the strike)
co: Yellow (the most important colour in her practice), gold
dp: Thursday / Jupiter — the victory of truth
cr: Yellow sapphire (her planetary stone), yellow topaz, citrine, gold
bp: Throat (she paralyses the throat of those who speak harmful falsehood), solar plexus (the power of the victory she grants), the tongue (she pulls it)
af: The harmful speech directed at me is paralysed. I speak truth and truth defeats all that is false. The goddess pulls the tongue of what would harm me.
sh: The paralysis turned against the self — Bagalamukhi's power can become the inability to speak one's own truth if misused
ar: The Paralysis of Falsehood / The Victory of Truth
cb: Saraswati (the goddess who gives the gift of speech) counterbalances Bagalamukhi (the goddess who removes the power of harmful speech). The giver and the restrainer of the tongue.

do: The sacred twins, the divine duality, the first Lwa, the power of the sacred double
at: The Marassa Jumeaux ('Sacred Twins') are considered the most powerful Lwa in the Vodou pantheon — more powerful than Baron Samedi, more powerful than Ogou, more powerful even than Erzulie. They are the divine twin principle: the sacred duality that is the foundation of all existence. They are children — mischievous, playful, demanding.
of: Twin plates of food (always two of everything), children's foods (candy, sweet foods), offerings made in pairs, the acknowledgment of duality in all things
an: None — the twins are the principle of doubling that underlies all animal pairs
co: Red and blue (twin colours — two different colours to distinguish them), white and gold
dp: Saturday — the sacred day of the most powerful Lwa
cr: Two matching stones, always in pairs — two pieces of clear quartz, two rose quartzes
el: All elements — the twins double all elements
bp: Sacral (the twinning creative power), root (the primal doubling), crown (the divine duality that underlies all consciousness)
af: I acknowledge the twin within me — the double nature of all things. I feed both sides of my duality with equal care. The sacred twins watch over the first and most important moments.
sh: The twins who fight — the Marassa who turn against each other rather than sustaining each other. Sacred duality that becomes destructive opposition.
ar: The Sacred Twins / The First and Most Powerful Lwa
cb: Bondye (the supreme — the one beyond all duality) counterbalances the Marassa Jumeaux (the first duality). The unity beyond the twins counterbalanced by the most primal twoness. The one and the two.

do: The first mother, the divine feminine before creation, the perfect mind, the sacred womb of the highest heaven
at: Barbelo is the supreme feminine principle in Sethian Gnostic cosmology — she is the first thought of the supreme Father (the Invisible Spirit), the divine womb from which all creation proceeds. She is described as 'the perfect power, the full Aeon, the thrice-male, the thrice-powerful, the thrice-named androgynous one.' She is the Ennoia (the first thought) — the crown chakra's most primordial feminine expression.
of: The silent contemplation of the first divine feminine thought, the honouring of the feminine principle at the very highest level, the reading of the Apocryphon of John as ceremony
an: None — she is before all animal forms
co: Pure white light, the white beyond white
dp: Sunday — the primordial divine
cr: Clear quartz (the absolute clarity of the first divine thought), white selenite, apophyllite
bp: Crown (the highest crown beyond the crown), the entire being as a thought within Barbelo's mind
af: I am a thought in the divine mother's mind. The first feminine principle contains me. I return to the womb of the highest heaven.
sh: The divine feminine so high she is beyond relationship — the transcendent feminine principle that cannot be touched
ar: The First Divine Feminine Thought / The Gnostic Mother
cb: The Invisible Spirit / The Father (the supreme masculine principle from whom Barbelo's first thought emerged) counterbalances Barbelo (the first divine feminine thought). The unknowable source counterbalanced by the knowable first expression of that source.

do: The supreme principle above all gods, the totality of light and shadow, the 365 heavens
at: Abraxas is the supreme Gnostic deity in the Basilidean school — above even the traditional God. The numerical value of his name in Greek equals 365 (the days of the year). He rules the 365 heavens. He is depicted as a being with a human body, the head of a rooster, and serpents for legs — combining the celestial (rooster), the human (consciousness), and the chthonic (serpents). He is the complete divine — above all duality.
of: The Abraxas gem (amulet) used in ceremony, the meditation on the totality of divine principles, the acknowledgment of both light and shadow as aspects of the one supreme
an: Rooster (the dawn-announcer — his head), serpent (the chthonic — his legs), the lion
co: Gold, black, the colours of both light and shadow
dp: Sunday — the supreme above all other supreme beings
cr: The Abraxas gem (ancient amulets bearing his image in carnelian or jasper), lapis lazuli, black tourmaline
el: All elements — he governs all 365 heavens, each with its own element
bp: Crown (the 365 heavens above all crowns), root (the serpent legs in the earth below), the entire body as the image of the combined divine
af: I acknowledge the totality — light and shadow, rooster and serpent, the 365 days of the complete year as my sacred body. I bow to what is above all gods.
sh: The syncretism that loses depth — Abraxas contains all so completely that he sometimes contains nothing specifically
ar: The Supreme Above All Gods / The 365 Heavens
cb: The individual Aeons (the specific divine principles that Abraxas transcends and contains) counterbalance Abraxas (the supreme that contains them all). Every individual divine name counterbalanced by the one name that contains all names.

do: The first and most supreme Shinto deity, the lord of the centre of heaven, the primordial divine
at: Ame-no-Minakanuhi ('The Lord Who Stands in the Centre of Heaven') is the first and highest deity in the Kojiki (Japan's oldest chronicle) — the primordial lord who is the absolute first principle. He appears only in the moment of creation and then withdraws utterly. He has no myth beyond his primordial existence. He is pure crown chakra: the divine before it became anything.
of: The acknowledgment of the divine that is before all things, the prayer of gratitude for the existence of existence itself, the most silent ceremony
an: None — he is before all animal forms
co: None — he is before all colour
dp: Sunday — the most primordial day
cr: Clear quartz (the crystal before colour), empty space (the most sacred offering to him)
el: Cosmic Consciousness — the void before all elements
bp: Crown and beyond — he is the principle before the crown chakra itself
af: Before all things there was the one who stands at the centre of heaven. I bow to what precedes me. I exist because the primordial divine permitted existence.
sh: The primordial that has no relationship — the absolute divine from which no personal connection is possible. The void of the first principle.
ar: The Primordial Centre / The Lord Before All Lords
cb: The manifest Shinto world (all the kami who followed Ame-no-Minakanuhi) counterbalances Ame-no-Minakanuhi (the primordial divine before manifestation). The infinite unmanifest counterbalanced by the finite manifest.

do: The primordial divine beings who came from heaven before the Orishas, the original 200
at: The Irunmole ('heavenly beings') are the primordial divine forces of Yoruba cosmology — 200 beings who came from Olodumare before the Orishas. Many Orishas are understood as Irunmole who chose to remain with humanity. They represent the divine in its most primordial, pre-individual form — before the divine took on the specific characteristics that make each Orisha recognisable.
of: The acknowledgment of the primordial divine before it became individualised, the most ancient Yoruba prayers that predate the Orisha system
an: None — the Irunmole precede animal associations
co: Pure white — the colour before all colour
dp: Sunday — the most primordial day
cr: The oldest stones — ancient fossils, meteorite, the most ancient stones
el: Cosmic Consciousness — the element before elements
bp: Crown (the primordial divine), root (the first landing on earth)
af: I am descended from the primordial 200 who came before all names. The divine in me is older than any name I could give it.
sh: The formless that cannot help — the Irunmole are so primordial they predate the capacity for human relationship
ar: The Primordial 200 / The Divine Before Individuation
cb: The Orishas (the individualised divine forces that descended from the Irunmole) counterbalance the Irunmole (the primordial collective divine before individuation). The specific counterbalanced by the primordial.

do: The land of the ancestors, the spirit world that mirrors the living world
at: Ala Mmuo ('the world of the spirits') is the Igbo conceptual realm of the ancestors — the mirror of the living world. The dead live in Ala Mmuo in a life parallel to the living world, and the relationship between the living and the ancestors is a constant exchange. The Igbo worldview does not separate the dead from the living — Ala Mmuo is adjacent, not distant.
of: Frequent libations for the ancestors, the maintenance of the ancestor shrine (obu), the speaking of ancestors' names as sacred invocation, the pouring of the first drink on the ground for the ancestors before drinking oneself
an: The snake (which ancestors often inhabit when they visit), the eagle
co: White (the colour of the spirit world in Igbo tradition)
dp: Saturday — the day of the ancestors
cr: White calcite, cowrie shells (the currency of both worlds), white quartz
bp: Root (the ancestors beneath the earth), crown (the ancestral wisdom that comes from above), the entire body that carries ancestral lineage
af: My ancestors are not gone — they are adjacent. I pour for them first. Their world mirrors mine and they guide me through the mirror.
sh: The ancestors who are not at rest — the dead who died without proper ceremony and remain between the worlds
ar: The Mirror World / The Adjacent Ancestors
cb: Ala (the earth goddess — who physically holds both the living and the dead in her body) counterbalances Ala Mmuo (the spiritual mirror of the living world). The physical earth counterbalanced by the spiritual realm it contains.

do: The outcaste goddess, the sacred pollution, the wisdom that comes from the margins
at: Matangi is the Tantric goddess of the outcaste — she accepts only polluted or leftover offerings (the most sacred Tantric teaching is that what is considered impure IS sacred). She is associated with speech, music, and the arts. She is the wisdom that comes from the margin, not the centre. She is Saraswati's shadow: the music and speech that rises from what was thrown away.
of: Polluted offerings (left-over food, that which others have rejected), the honouring of those on the margins, the worship of the divine in the rejected, music offered to her
an: Parrot (the bird of sacred speech), the mynah bird
co: Green (her body is dark green), the dark colours of the marginalised
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the marginal wisdom
cr: Dark green tourmaline, obsidian, dark jasper
bp: Throat (the sacred speech from the margins), solar plexus (the power of the outcaste who becomes the guru)
af: The rejected contains the most sacred. The leftover feeds the divine. I find the wisdom that was thrown to the margins and I honour it as most holy.
sh: The margin that has no access — the goddess of the outcaste whose devotees are perpetually excluded from the mainstream of the tradition that honours her
ar: The Outcaste Wisdom / The Sacred Pollution
cb: Saraswati (the mainstream goddess of pure speech and music) counterbalances Matangi (the marginal goddess of impure speech and outcaste music). The centre and the margin in their divine polarity.

do: The world-filling goddess, the space in which all things exist, the supreme divine mother of space
at: Bhuvanesvari ('She who is the world') is one of the ten Mahavidyas — she is the divine feminine as space itself. She is not a goddess in space; she is the space in which all things exist. All of creation lives within her. She is depicted as a beautiful woman holding a lotus and making the gesture of blessing.
of: Meditation on infinite space as the goddess's body, the Sri Yantra as her geometric form, offerings of space (silence, the acknowledgment of the void as sacred)
an: None — she is space itself, which contains all animals
co: Red (the colour of the dawn — she is the sky before the sun rises), gold (the sun within her)
