dp: Sunday / Venus — the all-encompassing feminine space
cr: Clear quartz, lapis lazuli (the sky that is her body), rose quartz (the heart of the world)
el: All elements — all elements exist within her space
bp: Crown (the infinite space above), heart (the heart of the world that she is), the entire body as a portion of her infinite form
af: I exist within the goddess. The space I inhabit is her body. I am a thought within the mind of the world-filling divine.
sh: The space that swallows — the infinite that makes the individual feel irrelevant, the cosmic mother so vast that the personal relationship to the divine is lost
ar: The World-Filling Space / The Cosmic Mother of All
cb: Shiva (who is the consciousness within Bhuvanesvari's space) counterbalances Bhuvanesvari (who is the world-space itself). The ultimate Tantric polarity: space and consciousness.

do: Storm, wind, death, the marketplace, radical change, ancestral masquerade, prophecy, the breath of the dead
at: Oya is the most multi-dimensional Orisha in the entire Yoruba pantheon — she simultaneously governs storm and wind (elemental power), the cemetery gate (death and the dead), the marketplace (commerce and transformation), the Egungun masquerade (ancestral communication), and prophecy (the wind that lifts the veil between worlds). She is the only Orisha who accompanies the dead all the way through their transition. She is the complete feminine force of transformation.
of: Nine purple aubergines, red wine, copper pennies, burgundy and dark purple cloth, cemetery gate offerings, wind offerings at crossroads, the Egungun masquerade ceremony
an: Buffalo (the wind animal), eagle (the sky prophet's bird), the cemetery owl
co: Burgundy, dark purple, all rainbow colours together
dp: Wednesday / Uranus — the lightning transformation and the radical change
cr: Labradorite (all the rainbow colours of the wind), dark amethyst, obsidian
bp: Lungs (the wind breath), solar plexus (lightning power), root (the cemetery earth), third eye (the prophetic sight)
af: I am the wind of necessary change. I cut what must be severed. I carry the ancestors' messages. I stand at every threshold.
sh: The storm that destroys without purpose — the change-addict who cannot allow anything to be stable long enough to bear fruit
ar: The Complete Transformation / The Nine-Dimensional Storm
cb: Obatala (absolute white stillness) counterbalances Oya (the absolute storm). The stillness that precedes all storms counterbalanced by the storm that precedes all stillness. The supreme Yoruba polarity of action and peace.

do: Thunder, justice, the axe of divine judgement, the fire of truth, the king who became divine
at: Xango is the Brazilian Candomblé form of Shango — the thunder Orixá who was a historical Yoruba king (Sango of Oyo) who became divine. In Brazil he has developed distinct characteristics: he is the Orixá of divine justice, the one who wields the double axe (oshe) to separate truth from falsehood. His ceremonies at Candomblé temples are among the most powerful in the tradition.
of: Bata drumming, red and white cloth, apples, red wine, the double-headed axe (oshe), the crown and the throne imagery
an: Ram (his sacred animal), the horse
co: Red and white (his sacred colours in Candomblé)
dp: Wednesday / Jupiter — the thunder king's justice
cr: Red garnet, meteorite, fire opal
bp: Solar plexus (the sovereign fire), throat (the voice of divine judgement), adrenal glands
af: My truth is as powerful as thunder. The axe of divine justice separates what is real from what is false. My sovereignty is earned.
sh: The king who abuses divine fire — the solar plexus that mistakes volume for truth, the thunder without wisdom
ar: The Brazilian Thunder King / The Divine Justice
cb: Oxalá (white, still, the supreme purity) counterbalances Xango (red, dynamic, the thunder). The stillness of the supreme creator counterbalanced by the dynamic force of divine justice.

do: The peaceful warrior, the diplomat, the one who fights with words rather than weapons
at: Ogou Badagris is the gentlest member of the Ogou family of Lwa — where Ogou Feray is the fierce warrior and revolutionary, Ogou Badagris is the diplomat, the one who solves conflicts through negotiation and ceremony rather than through force. He wears blue (rather than the red of the warrior Ogous). He governs the transition from war to peace.
of: Blue and red cloth (the Haitian flag colours), rum, cigars, the ceremony of reconciliation, offerings during peace negotiations
an: Snake (the wise diplomat's animal), the parrot (the negotiator's bird)
co: Blue (his distinguishing colour) and red
dp: Tuesday / Saturn — the warrior who chooses peace
cr: Blue lapis (the diplomat's stone), red jasper (the warrior beneath), iron
bp: Solar plexus (the warrior's power redirected to peace), the mouth (the diplomat's instrument), hands
af: My strength is greatest when I choose not to use force. I negotiate from power. The warrior who can make peace is more formidable than the warrior who only fights.
sh: The diplomat who has forgotten how to fight — the peace-making that has no iron behind it, the negotiation with no leverage
ar: The Diplomatic Warrior / The Peace-Maker with Iron
cb: Ogou Feray (the fierce revolutionary warrior) counterbalances Ogou Badagris (the diplomatic peace-maker). The sword and the negotiating table — both are Ogou.

do: The great forest, the wilderness, the plant medicines, the beginning of all Vodou initiations
at: Gran Bwa ('Great Forest/Wood') is the Lwa of the primordial forest — the wilderness that exists before and beyond human civilisation. He is also associated with the nganga (the sacred pot that contains the spirits in Palo Mayombe tradition) and with the initiatory forest. Before any major Vodou initiation, the initiate must pass through Gran Bwa's domain — the forest where the spirits live.
of: Offerings at forest clearings, rum poured on tree roots, tobacco, the bark of sacred trees, offerings made in the forest before any Vodou ceremony
an: Snake (the forest serpent), all wild forest animals, the tree itself as sacred being
co: Green and brown (the forest's colours), blue (the deep forest sky)
dp: Saturday / Earth — the primordial forest
cr: Brown jasper, moss agate, green aventurine, tree bark
bp: Root (the forest floor), heart (the forest's compassion for all life), the spine (the tree's axis)
af: The great forest holds the oldest medicine. I enter the initiation forest with respect. The wild places carry the oldest knowledge.
sh: The forest burned — Gran Bwa's shadow is the destruction of the natural world that holds the sacred medicines and the initiatory wisdom
ar: The Initiation Forest / The Sacred Wilderness
cb: Legba (the divine trickster who opens the gate to the human world) counterbalances Gran Bwa (the Lwa who governs the world before the human gate). The cultivated crossroads counterbalanced by the wild primordial forest.

do: The magic of two waters, the sorcerer at the meeting of fresh and salt water, the liminal magical vision
at: Simbi Andezo ('Simbi of Two Waters') is the Lwa who lives at the meeting of fresh and salt water — the most liminal of all magical spaces. He governs both the sweetwater magic (fresh water's healing) and the saltwater magic (the ocean's depth). He is the master sorcerer at the point where two waters meet, which in Haitian geography and cosmology is the sacred threshold of all transformation.
of: Offerings at river mouths and estuaries (where fresh and salt water meet), green and silver cloth, rum, fresh water and salt water mixed in ceremony
an: Serpent (the water spirit's animal), the chameleon (the liminal colour-changer)
co: Green (fresh water) and silver-blue (salt water) — the two waters
dp: Monday / Mercury — the magical dual nature
cr: Two-coloured stones, aquamarine, pale green fluorite, ocean jasper
bp: Sacral (the two creative waters), throat (the magical speech), third eye (the vision at the threshold)
af: I stand at the meeting of two waters and I see in both directions. My magic is amplified at every threshold. The two waters within me create the most powerful transformation.
sh: The sorcerer who manipulates — Simbi Andezo's shadow is the two-water magic used for harm rather than healing
ar: The Two-Water Sorcerer / The Threshold Magician
cb: Damballa Wedo (the pure white serpent — the simplicity of the single principle) counterbalances Simbi Andezo (the complex dual-water magic). Simplicity and complexity, the one principle and the two waters.

do: Agriculture, the peasant farmer, the earth, the humble food that sustains life
at: Kouzen Zaka ('Cousin Zaka' or Azaka) is the Lwa of agriculture and the peasant farmer — he is depicted as a simple farmer carrying a straw bag and a machete, wearing a straw hat and blue denim. He is the most humble of the Lwa, the one who tends the earth, grows the food, and feeds the people. He is the Haitian peasant farmer elevated to the divine.
of: Blue denim cloth, corn, beans, bread, the first fruits of the harvest, a small straw bag, rum and tobacco
an: None — he is the farmer who tends the animals, not an animal himself
co: Blue denim, earth brown, the green of growing crops
dp: Saturday / Earth — the farmer's day
cr: Brown jasper, green aventurine, the stones of the Haitian countryside
bp: Hands (the farmer's calloused hands), root (the earth he tends), heart (the love of the land)
af: I tend what feeds life. The most humble work is the most sacred. The earth rewards patient loving attention.
sh: The farmer who is exploited — Zaka's shadow is the peasant whose labour feeds others while he himself goes hungry
ar: The Sacred Farmer / The Divine Peasant
cb: Ogou (the warrior, the iron, the fight for justice that protects the farmer's land) counterbalances Kouzen Zaka (the peaceful farmer who needs protection). The farmer and the warrior — the two pillars of the society that feeds and defends itself.

do: The oldest deity, the primordial swamp, the grandmother of all gods, the medicine before iron
at: Nana Buruku in her fullest expression — the oldest deity in the entire Yoruba-Fon-Candomblé tradition. She is so ancient that no iron can touch her medicine (iron was not yet invented when her healing power was first given). She is the grandmother of Obaluaiye (the smallpox deity). Her wisdom is the oldest wisdom there is: the wisdom of the primordial swamp that preceded all civilisation.
of: Brooms made without iron, white and purple offerings at standing water, the oldest herbs gathered without metal tools, ceremonies in swamps or still water
an: Crocodile (the swamp creature), the oldest serpent
co: White and deep purple (the oldest and most sacred combination)
dp: Saturday / Moon — the primordial night before all other nights
cr: White quartz, dark amethyst, ancient clay, fossilised stone
el: Water / Earth (the primordial swamp where these elements are not yet separated)
bp: Bones (the oldest structures), the skin, the womb (the original swamp from which life emerged)
af: My medicine is older than iron. I draw on the wisdom that preceded all tools. The primordial grandmother knows what all the younger gods have forgotten.
sh: The ancient that refuses all renewal — Nana Buruku's shadow is the grandmother wisdom so old it cannot adapt to the present
ar: The Oldest Medicine / The Pre-Iron Grandmother
cb: Ogum/Ogun (iron, the forge, all iron tools — her complete opposite) counterbalances Nana Buruku (the pre-iron medicine, the oldest healing). The most ancient and the modern technology in their sacred and irreconcilable polarity.

do: Water, love, beauty, the feminine divine of the Fon-Ewe tradition — Erzulie Freda's West African source
at: Aziri (also Azili or Anyiewo) is the Fon-Ewe water goddess who is the direct West African antecedent of the Haitian Vodou Erzulie Freda. She governs love, beauty, sweet water, and the sacred feminine creative principle. She is the root of the most beloved Lwa in the Haitian diaspora — to know Erzulie Freda is to know Aziri before the Middle Passage.
of: Sweet water offerings, beauty offerings, the first flowers of the season, honey, the honouring of the West African feminine divine in her original context
an: Fish, the butterfly, the bee
co: Pink and gold (love and beauty)
dp: Friday / Venus — the original West African love goddess
cr: Rose quartz, citrine, amber
bp: Heart (love), sacral (the feminine creative power), skin (the beauty she governs)
af: I am connected to the feminine divine across the ocean and across the centuries. The love goddess was always there, before the diaspora, after it, and now.
sh: The love cut off from its roots — Aziri's shadow is the beauty goddess separated from her West African homeland, worshipped but disconnected from her original tradition
ar: The West African Love Goddess / The Source of Erzulie
cb: Hevioso (the Fon thunder god — the masculine storm) counterbalances Aziri (the feminine sweet water love). Thunder and water, storm and love, in their Fon expression.

do: Thunder, lightning, the ram, divine justice — the Fon thunder deity
at: Sogbo (also called Hevioso) is the supreme thunder deity of the Fon-Ewe peoples — the divine force of lightning, thunder, and the sky's power over the earth. He is the Fon equivalent of Shango/Xango. He wields the thunder axe (tobosi) and is depicted as a ram-headed deity. His justice is absolute and swift — he strikes those who commit crimes against the community.
of: Ram sacrifices at the most important ceremonies, red and white cloth, drumming, libations of blood and alcohol, the ceremony of lightning wood (wood struck by lightning is sacred to him)
an: Ram (his most sacred animal), the leopard, the snake
co: Red and white (the thunderstorm's colours in Fon tradition)
dp: Wednesday / Jupiter — the thunder's justice
cr: Red garnet, meteorite, fire opal
bp: Solar plexus (the thunderstorm's force), crown (the divine justice from above), adrenal glands
af: The thunder of divine justice moves through me. I act with the swiftness of lightning when action is required. My power serves the community.
sh: The lightning without discernment — the solar plexus that strikes without wisdom, the thunder god who punishes the innocent
ar: The Fon Thunder Justice / The Ram-Headed Storm
cb: Mawu (the gentle moon, the feminine wisdom, the cooling influence) counterbalances Sogbo/Hevioso (the fierce thunder, the masculine justice, the heating force). The moon and the thunder in their Fon divine polarity.

do: Iron, war, tools, the clearing of the primordial forest, the Fon equivalent of Ogun
at: Gu is the Fon deity of iron and war — the divine principle of the tool and the weapon. Like Ogun in the Yoruba tradition, Gu was the first to clear a path through the primordial chaos so that civilisation could begin. He is the divine ironworker, the god of all who work with metal. In Haiti he became Ogou.
of: Iron objects, palm oil poured on iron, rum, the ceremony at the forge, offerings at railway lines and machines
an: Dog (like Ogun), snake
co: Green and black
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the iron forge
cr: Iron meteorite, black tourmaline, hematite
bp: Muscles, bones, the hands that work with iron, the spine as iron axis
af: I clear the path before me with the sacred tool. My work is iron discipline and iron dedication. The forge transforms raw metal into purpose.
sh: The iron that turns against life — the tool that becomes the weapon without cause
ar: The Fon Iron Deity / The Path Clearer
cb: Aziri (water, beauty, love) counterbalances Gu (iron, war, the forge). Water and iron, love and war, in their Fon expression of the fundamental polarity between the feminine creative and the masculine transformative.

do: The earth, disease (especially smallpox), healing, the Fon equivalent of Obaluaiye
at: Sakpata is the Fon-Ewe earth deity who governs disease — particularly smallpox — and its healing. Like Obaluaiye in the Yoruba tradition, he is both the sender and the curer of the disease. He is covered in the spots of the disease he governs. He is associated with the earth and with the principle that the force of disease IS the healing force — the same divine power in its destructive and curative expressions.
of: Popcorn, straw, cowrie shells, the earth itself as altar, ceremonies of healing and purification
an: Lizard (the earth creature that moves between worlds), the tortoise
co: Black and white, red and black (the colours of earth disease and its transformation)
dp: Monday — the day of cleansing and purification
cr: Cowrie shells, black tourmaline, dark jasper, healing obsidian
bp: Skin (the disease he governs), the immune system, the body's healing capacity, the root chakra
af: The disease carries its own medicine. I honour the force that both sends and cures. The earth holds both the poison and the antidote.
sh: The plague without the cure — the disease deity unappeasable, the healing force gone fully destructive
ar: The Fon Disease Healer / The Earth's Fever
cb: Hevioso/Sogbo (the sky fire, the celestial force) counterbalances Sakpata (the earth disease, the terrestrial force). Sky and earth, fire and the earth's fever, in their Fon cosmological polarity.

do: The crossroads, communication between humans and divine, the gatekeeper, the sun and the word
at: Legba is the supreme gatekeeper of the Fon tradition — the Vodun who stands at the crossroads between the human world and the world of the spirits. He is the first invoked in all Fon and Vodou ceremonies. In the Fon tradition he is a more complex figure than Eshu/Elegua — he is associated with the sun (Legba owns the sun), with language itself, and with the full range of human communication. He is old, limping, carrying a stick — but beneath the old form is infinite cosmic power.
of: The first libation in every ceremony (always), rum and tobacco, toys and candy (he has a child-like aspect), crossroads offerings, speaking to him first before speaking to any other divine force
an: Dog (his sacred companion), the sun itself (his primary symbol in Fon tradition)
co: Red and black (his crossroads colours)
dp: Monday and Sunday — the gate between all days
cr: Red jasper, black tourmaline, obsidian, the crossroads stone
bp: Feet (the crossroads walker), throat (language itself), crown (the ownership of the sun)
af: Legba opens every door before me. I speak to the gatekeeper first. All communication flows through the divine crossroads.
sh: The locked gate — Legba's shadow is the crossroads closed, the communication impossible, the ceremony that cannot begin because the first invocation was not made
ar: The Supreme Gatekeeper / The Owner of the Sun
cb: Mawu-Lisa (the supreme androgynous divine — the source behind all gates) counterbalances Legba (the gatekeeper who stands between the source and all who seek it). The infinite counterbalanced by the threshold.

do: The supreme sky deity of many East African Bantu peoples, the thunder, the omnipresent divine
at: Mulungu is the supreme deity of the Nyamwezi, Sukuma, Yao and many other Bantu-speaking peoples of East Africa — he is the sky god, the thunder, and the omnipresent divine force. Like many African supreme deities, he was once accessible to humans but withdrew when humans became too disrespectful. He communicates through thunder. The word 'Mulungu' spread across a vast area of East and Central Africa as the name for God.
of: Thunder honoured, prayers directed to the sky, the living of a righteous life, offerings made on high ground
an: None — Mulungu is beyond animal representation
co: White (the divine), blue (the sky), the gold of lightning
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, meteorite, lapis lazuli
bp: Crown, the entire being, every breath as Mulungu's gift
af: The thunder speaks the divine will. I hear the voice of Mulungu in every storm. The omnipresent divine surrounds me on all sides.
sh: The sky god who withdrew — Mulungu retreated from the earth because of humanity's disrespect. The shadow is the sacred that has been driven away by human carelessness.
ar: The East African Omnipresent / The Thunder Supreme
cb: The ancestors (the personal divine that remained with humanity after Mulungu withdrew) counterbalance Mulungu (the supreme that withdrew). The remote divine counterbalanced by the intimate dead.

do: The supreme sky God of the Oromo people, the sky, the divine masculine principle
at: Wak (also spelled Waqa or Waaqa) is the supreme deity of the Oromo people — one of the largest ethnic groups in East Africa (over 40 million people). He is the sky god, the creator of all things. The Oromo traditional religion (Waaqeffanna — 'the worship of Waq') is one of the most sophisticated and widespread indigenous African religious systems, yet almost entirely unknown outside East Africa.
of: Prayer at dawn facing east, the sacred Irreecha festival (a thanksgiving ceremony at the sacred lake or river), offerings of milk and butter, the honouring of the natural world as Waq's creation
an: Cattle (the most sacred Oromo animal), the serpent (the earth's manifestation of Waq's power), the eagle
co: White (the colour of the divine purity), the deep blue of the Ethiopian sky
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, sky blue stone, meteorite (from the sky)
bp: Crown, the entire being, the breath as Waq's gift
af: Waaqeffanna — I worship the sky. Waq created me and sustains me. The Oromo sacred tradition is ancient and alive.
sh: The sky god who is too remote — Waq's shadow is the divine that cannot be reached without the intermediary of the Ayyaana (personal divine spirit)
ar: The Oromo Supreme / The Sky Creator of the Great Rift Valley
cb: Ayyaana (the personal divine spirit — the Oromo equivalent of Chi or Ori — the individual divine spark that mediates between humanity and Waq) counterbalances Waq (the supreme universal divine). The personal and the universal in their Oromo expression.

do: The personal divine spirit, the individual's connection to Waq, the divine spark within each person
at: Ayyaana is the Oromo concept of the personal divine spirit — the individual's unique connection to the supreme Waq. Like the Yoruba Ori, the Igbo Chi, or the Hindu Atman, Ayyaana is the divine flame that is specifically and irreplaceably yours. The Oromo Bokkuu ceremony (the ceremony of the passing of power) involves the invocation of Ayyaana. Ayyaana is also associated with luck, fortune, and the capacity to receive divine blessing.
of: The Bokkuu ceremony, personal prayer and reflection, the honouring of one's own unique divine quality, offerings made in alignment with one's personal Ayyaana
an: Personal to each person's Ayyaana — as individual as the person themselves
co: White and gold — the colours of the personal divine
dp: Sunday — the day of the personal divine spark
cr: Clear quartz (the personal clarity), whatever stone calls to your Ayyaana
el: Cosmic Consciousness / Fire (the personal divine flame)
bp: Crown (the divine spark above), solar plexus (the personal power below), the heart (the Ayyaana's seat)
af: My Ayyaana is awake and guiding me. The divine spark within me is uniquely mine. I walk in alignment with my personal divine nature.
sh: The Ayyaana that is blocked — the personal divine spark that cannot flow because of the accumulated weight of disrespectful choices
ar: The Oromo Personal Divine / The Individual Sacred Flame
cb: Waq (the universal supreme divine) counterbalances Ayyaana (the personal divine). The infinite counterbalanced by the specific. The sky god counterbalanced by the personal divine spark within each person.

do: The supreme ancestral spirit of the Great Lakes region, the imandwa, the divine hero
at: Ryangombe (also called Kiranga) is the supreme spirit of the Kubandwa cult of the Great Lakes region of Africa (Rwanda, Burundi, Uganda). He was a legendary hero who died in a hunting accident and became the supreme imandwa (spirit). He governs the cult of the dead, healing, and the relationship between the living and the divine ancestors. His cult was one of the most widespread in the Great Lakes region before colonialism almost destroyed it.
of: The Kubandwa initiation ceremonies, offerings of beer and grain, the sacred drum, fire ceremonies at night, the blood of a sacrificed animal
an: Lion (the royal beast of the Great Lakes), the leopard, the sacred cattle
co: White and red (the colours of the sacred ceremony), the deep green of the Rwanda hills
dp: Saturday — the day of the ancestral spirits
cr: Red jasper, white quartz, the volcanic stones of the Great Lakes volcanoes
bp: Crown (the supreme ancestral divine), root (the hero who died in the earth), the spine (the axis connecting the living and the dead)
af: The supreme ancestral hero watches over the Great Lakes. I honour those who became divine through their actions. The imandwa are with me.
sh: The cult suppressed — Ryangombe's shadow is the Kubandwa tradition nearly destroyed by colonial missionaries who called it devil worship
ar: The Great Lakes Divine Hero / The Supreme Imandwa
cb: The Christian God (who was imposed over Ryangombe's cult by colonial missionaries) counterbalances Ryangombe (the indigenous supreme spirit). The imposed divine counterbalanced by the suppressed indigenous divine — and the slow restoration of both.

tr: Kamba / Kikuyu / East African Coastal Bantu
do: The supreme sky deity of the Kamba and coastal Bantu peoples, the creator above all
at: Murungu (also spelled Mlungu or Mulungu in coastal variations) is the supreme deity of the Kamba people of Kenya and the coastal Bantu peoples of East Africa. He is the sky god, the creator, the all-knowing divine. The Kamba people have a rich tradition of communication with both Murungu and the ancestral spirits (aimu). He predates Christian mission contact and represents one of the oldest living monotheistic traditions in Africa.
of: Prayers at dawn, offerings made on hilltops (approaching the sky), the living of a life that honours Murungu through care for the community
an: None — Murungu is beyond animal representation
co: White and blue (the sky and the divine purity)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, clear calcite, the stone of Kamba sacred sites
bp: Crown, the entire being
af: Murungu sees me from the sky. The Kamba supreme created me with a specific purpose. I live my purpose with gratitude.
sh: The distant creator — the supreme divine who created all and now watches from too far above to be directly approached
ar: The Kamba Supreme / The East African Creator
cb: The aimu (the Kamba ancestral spirits — who mediate between the living and Murungu) counterbalance Murungu (the remote supreme). The intimate dead counterbalanced by the distant divine.

do: Overcoming oppression, the will that cannot be broken, the trickster spirit of freedom, the conqueror root
at: High John the Conqueror is the supreme spirit of African American Hoodoo tradition — the folk hero who was enslaved but whose spirit could never be chained, who tricked the slaveholders, who kept laughter and joy and freedom alive in the most brutal conditions. He is both a person and a root (the High John root). He is the solar plexus of survival — the indomitable will.
of: The High John the Conqueror root (carried or made into a mojo bag), whiskey, tobacco, red cloth, offerings made to the spirit of those who survived the unsurvivable
an: None — High John is pure spirit, the human will made divine
co: Red (the fire of the conqueror's will), gold (the wealth he won through his wits)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the conqueror's day
cr: High John root, red jasper, lodestone (the magnet that draws what you need), iron pyrite
bp: Solar plexus (the indomitable will), root (the root that bears his name), spine (the back that would not be broken)
af: My spirit cannot be chained. I carry High John's root in my heart. I trick what tries to defeat me. My will is unconquerable.
sh: The trickster without the ethics — the conqueror spirit used for personal manipulation rather than liberation from genuine oppression
ar: The Indomitable Will / The Conqueror of Slavery
cb: The system of oppression (slavery, racism, colonialism — the force that High John eternally defeats) counterbalances High John the Conqueror (the spirit of freedom). The oppression counterbalanced by the inextinguishable spirit.

do: The Voodoo Queen of New Orleans, the power of the oppressed feminine, the bridge between African and American divine traditions
at: Marie Laveau (1801-1881) is the most powerful figure in the New Orleans Voodoo tradition — the Voodoo Queen who held enormous spiritual and social power in antebellum New Orleans. A free woman of colour, she combined West African Vodou, Haitian Vodou, Catholicism, and Native American practices into the unique New Orleans tradition. She is still invoked and petitioned at her tomb in St. Louis Cemetery No. 1.
of: Offerings at her tomb in New Orleans (marking X's on the tomb, turning around three times, knocking three times, and making a wish), rum, cigars, red wine, Saint Expedite (her syncretised Catholic saint companion)
an: Snake (her famous python Zombi was her ritual animal), the crow
co: Red and black, gold (her queenly colours)
dp: Friday / Venus — the queen's day
cr: Black tourmaline, red jasper, obsidian, gold
bp: Crown (her sovereign authority), sacral (her sexual power and her healing of love problems), throat (her commanding voice)
af: The Voodoo Queen of New Orleans walks with me. The power of the oppressed feminine is a force that no law can contain. My magic is inherited from the strongest women who ever lived.
sh: The queen whose power becomes control — the spiritual authority that exploits the vulnerable rather than protecting them
ar: The Voodoo Queen / The New Orleans Divine Feminine
cb: Baron Samedi (the death master — her counterpart in the divine masculine) counterbalances Marie Laveau (the life and love master — the divine feminine of New Orleans Voodoo). Love and death, the queen and the baron.

do: The crossroads, the gatekeeper, communication between the human and divine worlds in the African American Voodoo tradition
at: Papa Legba in the New Orleans Voodoo tradition — slightly different from the Haitian Vodou Legba, he is more directly the old man at the crossroads, the one at the threshold. He was famously invoked at the crossroads at midnight by blues musicians (the 'deal with the Devil' crossroads mythology — which is a demonisation of Papa Legba through the lens of colonial Christianity). He is the divine trickster who opens all doors.
of: Rum, tobacco, candy, crossroads offerings at midnight, the first invocation at any ceremony, the red and black candle at the crossroads
an: Dog (his sacred companion), the crow
co: Red and black (the crossroads colours)
dp: Monday — the gate between all days
cr: Red jasper, black tourmaline, crossroads dirt
bp: Feet (the crossroads walker), throat (all communication), root (the crossroads embedded in the earth)
af: Papa Legba opens the door before me. All communication flows through the crossroads. I speak to the gatekeeper first.
sh: The crossroads deal — the demonised Legba of colonial Christianity, the 'deal with the Devil' mythology that erased the African divine trickster's true nature
ar: The New Orleans Crossroads / The African American Gatekeeper
cb: Marie Laveau (the divine feminine of New Orleans Voodoo) counterbalances Papa Legba (the divine masculine gatekeeper). The queen and the gatekeeper, the female sovereign and the male threshold.

tr: African American / Caribbean Diaspora Folk Tradition
do: The survival trickster, wit over power, the African trickster spirit as resistance, the enslaved person's divine weapon
at: Brer Rabbit — the African American folk trickster — is the direct descendant of the West African spider trickster Anansi and the hare trickster of various African traditions, carried across the Middle Passage and transformed into the small, quick, clever creature who outwits the powerful (Brer Fox, Brer Bear). He is the divine trickster of the enslaved: the one whose wit defeats brute power, whose cunning overcomes violence.
of: Rabbit imagery honoured, the telling of Brer Rabbit stories as sacred transmission, offerings to the trickster spirit of all who outwitted oppression through intelligence
an: Rabbit (he IS the rabbit), spider (his West African ancestor Anansi)
co: Brown and grey (the rabbit's camouflage — the wisdom of being overlooked while planning)
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the trickster's planet
cr: Obsidian (the trickster's dark mirror), jet, brown jasper
bp: Throat (the trickster's speech), sacral (the trickster's unbounded creative energy), feet (the quick feet that carry him to safety)
af: My wit is my weapon. I use intelligence where force would fail. The small creature defeats the powerful through cleverness — and this is divine.
sh: The trickster who has only tricks — Brer Rabbit's shadow is the survival mechanism that persists after the survival crisis, the cleverness that cannot stop even when it is no longer necessary
ar: The Diaspora Trickster / The Resistance Through Wit
cb: Brer Fox and Brer Bear (the powerful who are repeatedly outwitted) counterbalance Brer Rabbit (the small who repeatedly outsmarts). Power and wit, brute force and intelligence, in their eternal diaspora dance.

do: The spirit container, the power object, the sacred medicine bundle, the divine force made physical
at: Nkisi (plural: Minkisi) is a Kongo concept that is simultaneously a spirit, a power object, and a healing force. A nkisi is a container (a clay pot, a gourd, a carved figure) filled with sacred medicines (bilongo) that give the nkisi its power. The most powerful nkisi can heal disease, protect communities, and punish wrongdoers. Nkisi Nkondi (the nail fetish) is perhaps the most famous. The nkisi tradition directly influenced Vodou's nganga and Candomblé's assentamento.
of: The creation of the nkisi with the proper medicines (bilongo), the activation ceremony, the maintenance of the nkisi's power through offerings, the honoring of the nkisi's specific requirements
an: None — the nkisi's power comes from the medicines within it, not from an animal form (though many nkisi contain animal materials)
co: White (mpemba — the sacred white chalk of the Kongo world), red (the living blood), black (the dead earth) — the three sacred Kongo colours
dp: Saturday — the day of the sacred power
cr: Sacred medicines (bilongo) include: white chalk, red clay, black soil, iron, the bones of the dead, the roots of powerful plants
bp: Root (the power embedded in the earth), solar plexus (the commanding power), the entire body protected by the nkisi's field
af: I carry the sacred medicine bundle of my own power. The nkisi within me contains all the medicine I need. My power is specific, concentrated, and aligned.
sh: The nkisi turned against the community — the sacred power object used for harm rather than healing and protection
ar: The Sacred Medicine Bundle / The Power Object
cb: Nzambi (the supreme divine force behind all nkisi) counterbalances each individual nkisi (the specific, contained power object). The infinite divine force counterbalanced by the specific container of that force.

do: The rainbow, the serpent of abundance, the cosmic bridge between earth and sky in Kongo cosmology
at: Mbumba is the Kongo rainbow serpent — the cosmic bridge between the world of the living (above the Kalunga line) and the world of the dead (below it). Like Oshumare in the Yoruba tradition and Damballa in Haitian Vodou, Mbumba is the great cosmic serpent that connects the vertical axis of creation. He is associated with abundance, the rainbow's promise, and the cycle of renewal.
of: Rainbow imagery, offerings at rivers and at the places where different coloured waters meet, the honouring of the serpent as sacred, libations poured at the cosmogram (the four-point Kongo sacred diagram)
an: Serpent (the rainbow serpent), the python
co: All the colours of the rainbow, white (the Kalunga white of the cosmic boundary)
dp: Monday / Neptune — the rainbow and the serpent bridge
cr: Rainbow moonstone, all iridescent stones, the seven colours represented
el: Water / Air — the rainbow is both
bp: The entire spine (the serpent's path), sacral (the abundance), crown (the rainbow's highest arc)
af: The rainbow serpent moves through me from root to crown. My abundance comes from the cosmic connection. The bridge between the worlds lives in my body.
sh: The rainbow that fades — Mbumba's shadow is the cosmic connection lost, the abundance withdrawn, the bridge between worlds invisible
ar: The Kongo Rainbow Serpent / The Cosmic Bridge
cb: Kalunga (the sacred threshold line between the worlds of living and dead) counterbalances Mbumba (the rainbow serpent that crosses that threshold). The boundary and the bridge.

do: Rain, the sky, the rainbow, the feminine divine of the Kongo coast — one of Africa's most complete goddesses
at: Bunzi is the rain and sky goddess of the Woyo people of the Kongo coast — one of the most clearly defined and powerful female deities in the entire Kongo tradition. She governs rain, the rainbow, the sky, and female fertility. She is the wife of the sea god Mangaka. She is the divine feminine of the Kongo cosmos — the rain mother whose blessing makes all agriculture possible.
of: Rain ceremonies, offerings at sacred groves, prayers for fertility and rain, the honouring of the rainbow as her sign
an: Serpent (the rainbow serpent form), the rain frog
co: Blue and white (rain and sky), the colours of the rainbow
dp: Monday / Moon — the rain and the feminine divine
cr: Aquamarine, blue topaz, moonstone, rain water (the most sacred substance to her devotees)
bp: Sacral (the rain's fertility), crown (the sky she governs), womb (the rain mother's fertility)
af: The rain of blessing falls. The rainbow goddess sees me. My fertility — creative, physical, spiritual — is nourished from the sky above.
sh: The drought — Bunzi's shadow is the sky goddess who withholds the rain, the feminine divine who cannot be appeased
ar: The Kongo Rain Goddess / The Rainbow Mother
cb: Mangaka (the sea god — her consort, the ocean's masculine power) counterbalances Bunzi (the sky rain goddess, the feminine aerial power). Sky and sea, the rain and the ocean, feminine and masculine in the Kongo coastal cosmos.

do: The fullness of the divine light, the 30 sacred emanations of the supreme, the world before the fall
at: The Pleroma ('fullness') is the Gnostic realm of divine light — the 30 Aeons that emanate from the supreme Invisible Spirit. Each Aeon is a complete divine principle: Depth (Bythos), Silence (Sige), Mind (Nous), Truth (Aletheia), Word (Logos), Life (Zoe), and so on. Together they constitute the totality of divine reality before the fall of Sophia created the material world. The Pleroma is what the Gnostic seeks to return to.
of: Contemplation of the divine fullness, the meditation on each Aeon as an aspect of the complete divine, the study of Gnostic texts as ceremony, the inner work of reunion with the divine light
an: None — the Aeons are pure light principles, not embodied
co: Pure white light, the light that contains all colours within it
dp: Sunday — the day of the fullness of light
cr: Clear quartz (the pure light), apophyllite (the ascension stone), selenite (the light of the pleroma)
bp: Crown (the highest divine light), third eye (the vision of the fullness), the entire body as a fragment of the divine pleroma seeking to return to the whole
af: I am a fragment of the divine fullness seeking reunion with the source. The Aeons within me are the lights of my own divine nature. I return to the Pleroma.
sh: The Aeon that fell — Sophia's fall from the Pleroma created the material world. The shadow of the divine fullness is the fall that occurs when one of its members acts alone, without the consent of the whole.
ar: The Divine Fullness / The 30 Sacred Lights
cb: The Demiurge (the false creator who trapped the divine light in matter, the product of Sophia's fall) counterbalances the Aeons/Pleroma (the divine fullness before the fall). The light and its imprisonment in matter.

do: The lower Sophia, the soul of the world, the divine light trapped in matter, the grief of the divine feminine in exile
at: Sophia Achamoth is the lower Sophia in the Valentinian Gnostic system — the fragment of Sophia that fell and became trapped in matter. She is the world-soul: her grief became the water, her laughter became the light in matter, her tears became the principle of matter itself. She is the divine light in its most trapped and longing expression. She cries for the Pleroma she has lost. When the divine sends the Christ to rescue her, she weeps with joy.
of: The acknowledgment of divine light trapped in the material world, the practice of gnosis as the liberation of Sophia Achamoth from her exile, prayers for the restoration of the divine feminine in matter
an: Dove (the Holy Spirit is her divine expression), the serpent (wisdom in its trapped form)
co: The grey of matter, the gold of the divine light still shining within it
dp: Monday / Moon — the light in exile, reflecting the sun it cannot directly receive
cr: Labradorite (the divine light trapped in dark stone, flashing through), moonstone, dark iridescent stones
el: Earth / Water (her tears became matter's first water)
bp: Heart (her grief — which the divine messengers weep with to release), root (the trapped light in the material world), sacral (her laughter that became light in matter)
af: The divine light within me is not diminished by its exile in matter. My grief for what I have lost is itself sacred. The divine sends the rescue.
sh: The grief that becomes the prison — Sophia Achamoth's grief for the Pleroma IS the material world. The shadow is the mourning that becomes the substance of the cage.
ar: The World Soul / The Divine Light in Exile
cb: The Christ/Christos (the divine messenger sent to rescue Sophia Achamoth from her exile) counterbalances Sophia Achamoth (the trapped divine light). The rescuer and the rescued, the liberating light and the imprisoned light.

do: The adversary, the left side of the divine, the prosecuting angel, the ruler of Mars and severity
at: Samael ('Poison of God' or 'Blindness of God') is the Kabbalistic adversarial archangel — the divine force that functions as the accuser, the tester, the one who operates on the left side of the Sephirotic Tree. He is not simply 'the devil' but the divine principle of severity and opposition: the force that makes growth possible by providing resistance. He governs Mars and is associated with the Sephirah Geburah (Severity).
of: Red candles, iron, war magick for protective purposes, the acknowledgment of the adversarial principle in one's own path
an: Serpent (his traditional form), the scorpion, the lion
co: Red and black (the colours of severity and opposition)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the planetary correspondence of severity
cr: Red garnet, iron meteorite, red jasper, bloodstone
bp: Solar plexus (the adversarial testing force), root (the left pillar of the Sephirotic Tree), adrenal glands (the fight response)
af: The adversary makes me stronger. The divine tester reveals what is real in me. I face what opposes me with gratitude for the refinement it provides.
sh: The adversary mistaken for the enemy — the divine testing force demonised into pure evil, when it is actually the necessary principle of spiritual resistance
ar: The Divine Adversary / The Necessary Opposition
cb: Metatron (the supreme archangel of the right pillar — mercy and light) counterbalances Samael (the archangel of the left pillar — severity and darkness). The pillars of mercy and severity must both stand for the Sephirotic Tree to be in balance.

do: The first woman, the feminine divine who refused to be beneath, sexual liberation, the dark feminine wisdom
at: Lilith is one of the most dramatically vilified divine feminine figures in Western history. In Kabbalistic tradition she was the first woman created alongside Adam — not from his rib but from the same earth. She refused to lie beneath him (claiming equality) and was demonised for this refusal. She became the 'demon' of the night, the child-stealer, the seductress — all because she insisted on equality. She is the shadow of patriarchy's deepest fear: the feminine that will not submit.
of: Red candles, the owl as her sacred animal (a gift, never killed), the dark moon ceremony, offerings of autonomy and self-determination, the owl's feather
an: Owl (her sacred bird in all traditions — the owl is her animal), the serpent (wisdom), the lion (her strength)
co: Red, black, the dark crimson of the first woman's power
dp: Saturday / Pluto and Moon — the dark moon is her sacred time
cr: Obsidian, red garnet, black moonstone, labradorite
el: Air / Fire / Earth — she is claimed by no single element because she refused to be claimed
bp: Root (the earth she was made from and refused to be pressed back into), sacral (the sexual equality she demanded), crown (the divine wisdom she carries as the first woman)
af: I was not made from anyone else's body. I am made from the same earth as all who try to claim authority over me. I will not lie beneath. My refusal is sacred.
sh: The first refusal demonised — Lilith's shadow is the narrative created to punish every woman who insists on equality. Every 'Lilith' myth is the shadow of patriarchal control.
ar: The First Equal / The Demonised Sovereign
cb: Eve (the second woman — who obeyed, who was made from Adam's rib, who is the feminine principle that the patriarchal tradition preferred) counterbalances Lilith (the first woman who refused, who was made as an equal). The compliant feminine and the sovereign feminine in their eternal Biblical polarity.

tr: Thelemic / Western Occult (Aleister Crowley / Jack Parsons)
do: The divine feminine of the Western occult tradition, the liberation of sexual energy, the scarlet woman, the cup of all saints
at: Babalon is the supreme feminine divine of the Thelemic occult tradition — the divine principle of sexual liberation, the sacred prostitute elevated to cosmic significance, the Scarlet Woman of the Book of Revelation reclaimed as the divine mother. She is depicted as a naked woman riding the Beast, holding the Cup of Abominations (which Crowley interpreted as containing the blood of the saints — those who have offered themselves completely to the divine feminine). She is the sacral chakra elevated to crown.
of: The Thelemic Babalon working ceremony, scarlet candles, the chalice as sacred vessel, offerings of one's own vital energy consecrated to the divine feminine
an: The scarlet beast she rides (the primal force of the universe that the divine feminine governs), the owl
co: Scarlet and gold (the Scarlet Woman's colours)
dp: Friday / Venus — the divine feminine's planet elevated to its most occult expression
cr: Red ruby (scarlet), garnet, gold topaz
el: Fire / Water — the sacred prostitute bridges both
bp: Sacral (the sacred sexual liberation), crown (the divine feminine at the highest level), the womb (the cup that receives all)
af: My sacral energy is consecrated to the highest divine feminine principle. I am the cup that receives the divine. My pleasure is cosmic.
sh: The misuse of sacred sexuality — Babalon's shadow is the concept weaponised to justify the exploitation of women rather than their liberation
ar: The Thelemic Divine Feminine / The Sacred Liberation
cb: The Beast / Therion (the primal masculine force she rides — the untamed universal energy she governs) counterbalances Babalon (the divine feminine that directs that energy). The ultimate Thelemic polarity.

do: The wild, nature, the panic, the pipes, the masculine generative force of the earth
at: Pan is the god of the wild, the pastoral, and the generative masculine force of nature — half man, half goat, playing his pipes in the wilderness. He caused 'panic' (the word comes from his name — the terror of the wild that overwhelms the civilised mind). In the Western occult tradition he became the template for the 'devil' — horns, goat legs, sexuality — a demonisation of the sacred masculine force of nature.
of: The pipes of Pan played in nature, offerings in wild places, the honoring of the sexual generative force of the natural world, the mountain and the forest
an: Goat (half his body is goat), the serpent, all wild creatures
co: Green (the wild), brown (the earth), the golden light of the pastoral afternoon
dp: Saturday / Earth — the wild masculine earth
cr: Brown tourmaline, moss agate, green jasper, pyrite (the earth's gold)
bp: Root (the wild masculine earth force), sacral (the generative sexuality), the legs (the goat legs that connect him to the earth)
af: The wild masculine is sacred. Nature's generative force moves through me. I am not ashamed of what the civilised world called the devil — it is the earth's life force.
sh: The panic without the pipes — the overwhelming terror of the wild without the music that tames it. Pan's shadow is the panic that cannot be redeemed by his music.
ar: The Wild Generative Masculine / The Demonised Nature God
cb: Apollo (the civilised, the ordered, the rational solar god) counterbalances Pan (the wild, the chaotic, the irrational natural god). The Greek civilisation's most fundamental tension: the rational order and the wild nature that preceded and surrounds it.

do: The triple crossroads, the torchbearer, the queen of all magic, the divine that cannot be owned by any tradition
at: Hecate Trivia ('of the three ways') in her most complete expression — the torch-bearing goddess who illuminates all three roads at the triple crossroads simultaneously, who holds the keys to the underworld and the highest heaven, who governs all magic, all transition, all liminality. She is the divine force that exists between and beyond all categories. She cannot be owned by Greece, Rome, the Goetia, or modern Wicca — she belongs to all of them and none.
of: The triple crossroads ceremony at the new moon (deipnon — her monthly feast), offerings of garlic and honey, dogs howling in her honour, torches carried to illuminate the three ways, keys offered
an: Dog (always — she is always accompanied by dogs), serpent (wisdom), polecat, owl
co: Black, silver, the torch's gold — the three colours of the crossroads night
dp: Monday (dark moon) / Pluto — the queen of all magic
cr: Black moonstone, dark tourmaline, obsidian, jet, dark amethyst, labradorite
el: Earth / Ether / Air (the crossroads spans all)
bp: Third eye (the triple sight that sees all three ways), crown (the key-holder of cosmic thresholds), root (the chthonic underground from which she rises)
af: I see in all directions simultaneously. I hold the keys. I stand at the triple crossroads with my three torches raised and I illuminate all three ways at once.
sh: The night that swallows the torchbearer — Hecate's shadow is the magic that consumes the magician, the triple sight that drives mad
ar: The Triple Crossroads Queen / The Key-Holder of All Magic
cb: Demeter (the grief-stricken mother who searches with torches — Hecate guided Demeter with torches to find Persephone) counterbalances Hecate (the guide, the illuminator). The seeker and the guide.

do: The whirling, the divine love that transforms, the reed flute of longing, the heart's most articulate mystic
at: Jalaluddin Rumi (1207-1273) — Mevlana ('our master') — is the supreme poet of divine love in the Islamic mystical tradition. His Masnavi is the longest poem in the Persian language and is called 'the Quran in Persian.' His followers (the Mevlevi Order) practice the Sema — the whirling ceremony — as a form of meditation and prayer. His words have reached more people than almost any mystic in history.
of: The Sema (whirling ceremony), the reading of the Masnavi as prayer, the ney (reed flute) played as offering, the heart opened to divine love as the highest practice
an: Reed (the instrument of his longing — the ney cut from the reed bed cries for its origin, as the soul cries for the divine)
co: White (the whirling robe), green (the colour of the Prophet's tradition, the green of divine love)
dp: Friday / Venus — the love that whirls toward the divine
cr: Rose quartz (the heart of his poetry), lapis lazuli (the Persian heaven), clear quartz (the divine clarity)
el: Air (the spinning, the breath of the whirling)
bp: Heart (the centre of the whirling — the axis of love), crown (the union with the divine in the whirl), throat (the poetry that changes lives)
af: Out beyond ideas of wrongdoing and rightdoing, there is a field. I will meet you there. My heart is the whirling dervish that turns toward the divine.
sh: The whirling without the presence — the ceremony without the heart, the poetry without the yearning, the beautiful words without the love that produced them
ar: The Whirling Heart / The Reed's Longing
cb: Shams of Tabriz (the divine friend whose sudden departure caused Rumi to write the Masnavi — the loss that produced the greatest poetry of love) counterbalances Rumi (the one who whirled toward the divine). The friend-teacher and the devoted student-poet.

do: The greatest mystical theosopher of Islam, the unity of being (wahdat al-wujud), the divine feminine in Islamic mysticism
at: Muhyiddin Ibn Arabi (1165-1240) — the Greatest Master (Al-Shaykh Al-Akbar) — is the supreme theosophical mind of the Islamic mystical tradition. His Fusus al-Hikam (Bezels of Wisdom) and the Futuhat al-Makkiyya (Meccan Illuminations) constitute the most sophisticated mystical theology in any tradition. His doctrine of wahdat al-wujud ('unity of being' — the idea that all of existence is one divine reality) influenced mysticism across all traditions.
of: The study of his works as a form of worship, the contemplation of wahdat al-wujud (the unity of all being), the honouring of the divine feminine in all its forms (he wrote extensively about the divine feminine as the highest form of the divine's self-knowledge)
an: None — Ibn Arabi transcended animal correspondences in his theosophical approach
co: White and gold (the divine unity), the colours of the Andalusian Islamic tradition
dp: Wednesday / Jupiter — the expansion of the theosophical mind
cr: Lapis lazuli (the highest Islamic sacred stone), clear quartz (the unity of all being), white calcite
bp: Crown (the unity of all being experienced at the highest level), third eye (the vision of the divine reality in all things)
af: All is one divine reality. The divine finds itself through my experience of itself. I am the mirror in which the divine sees its own face.
sh: The unity that becomes indifference — wahdat al-wujud misunderstood as meaning that individual suffering doesn't matter because 'all is one'
ar: The Greatest Master / The Unity of Being
cb: The Law (the Sharia — the outer form of Islamic practice that grounds the mystical experience) counterbalances Ibn Arabi (the inner mystical reality that transcends all outer form). The outer law and the inner truth.

do: The green man, the immortal guide, the one who drinks from the water of life, the divine wisdom that transcends religion
at: Al-Khidr ('the Green One') is the mysterious immortal figure in the Quran who teaches Moses through three seemingly incomprehensible acts (making a hole in a boat, killing a young man, rebuilding a wall) that demonstrate divine wisdom beyond human logic. He drank from the Water of Life and became immortal. He is present in Islamic, Jewish and pre-Islamic traditions as the divine guide who appears at the moment of deepest need.
of: Green offerings, prayers at crossroads and at thresholds of difficulty, the acknowledgment that divine wisdom often appears incomprehensible before it reveals its meaning
an: None — Al-Khidr appears in any form, most often as a stranger
co: Green (his name means 'the Green One' — he makes the earth green where he walks)
dp: Thursday / Mercury — the divine guide's wisdom
cr: Green aventurine, emerald, jade, malachite
el: Water (the Water of Life) / Earth (the green earth he creates wherever he walks)
bp: Heart (the divine compassion that informs his seemingly cruel acts), third eye (the divine wisdom that sees the whole picture), crown (the immortality of the divine guide)
af: The divine guide appears at my moment of greatest need. The incomprehensible action hides a wisdom I cannot yet see. I trust the green man's journey.
sh: The guide whose actions cannot be questioned — Al-Khidr's shadow is the divine authority that acts beyond moral comprehension and demands complete submission
ar: The Immortal Green Guide / The Wisdom Beyond Understanding
cb: Moses (the great prophet who could not follow Al-Khidr's wisdom and had to part ways with him) counterbalances Al-Khidr (the guide whose wisdom transcends prophetic law). The known divine wisdom counterbalanced by the unknown divine wisdom. The prophet and the mystic.

do: Sky Father and Earth Mother, the primordial separation that let light into the world
at: Rangi (Sky Father) and Papa (Earth Mother) were locked in eternal embrace in darkness. Their children — the gods Tāne, Tū, Rongo, Tangaroa and others — were born in the darkness between them. Eventually Tāne forced them apart, creating the world as we know it. Rangi's tears (rain) fall to Papa (earth) eternally — their grief at separation is what waters the earth. They are the supreme Māori cosmological polarity.
of: The honouring of the sky and earth as the divine parents, the acknowledgment of the grief of separation as sacred (rain as divine tears), offerings made to the earth and the sky as one ceremony
an: All of nature — every creature is a child of Rangi and Papa
co: Blue (Rangi/sky) and brown-green (Papa/earth) together
dp: Sunday and Saturday — the sky and the earth
cr: Pounamu (New Zealand greenstone — the most sacred stone, found in the earth of Papa), sky blue stone, lapis lazuli
el: Air (Rangi) and Earth (Papa) — the two primordial elements in Māori cosmology
bp: Crown (the sky above), root (the earth below), heart (the love/grief that exists between separated lovers who were once inseparably one)
af: I carry the grief of the sky for the earth and the earth for the sky. The rain is sacred tears. The separation that created the world was an act of love.
sh: The children who force the separation — the Māori myth does not celebrate the separation of Rangi and Papa; it mourns it. The shadow is the creation that required the wounding of the divine parents.
ar: The Primordial Separation / The Sky and Earth Parents
cb: Tāne (their son who forced the separation) counterbalances Rangi and Papa (the parents who were separated). The child's growth that requires the parent's sacrifice. Creation through wounding.

do: War, mankind, human strength and will, the only god who stood firm in the storm
at: Tū (Tu-Matauenga, 'Man of Angry Face') is the Māori god of war and mankind — the only one of Rangi and Papa's children who stood firm and did not bow before the storm of Tāwhirimātea (the wind and storm god). He became the patron of humanity and the deity who gave humans the tools and knowledge to survive. He is the solar plexus of the Māori cosmos: the unyielding will.
of: The haka (the fierce Māori ceremonial dance of challenge and strength) as sacred offering, weapons blessed in his name, the acknowledgment of the will that does not bend
an: Hawk (the war bird), all of the creatures that humans hunt (they are his gifts to humanity)
co: Red (the war colour in Māori tradition), black (the fierce face), white (the bone of those who fought)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the war god's day
cr: Red jasper, bloodstone, iron, hematite
bp: Solar plexus (the unyielding will), arms (the weapons), legs (the stance that does not bow)
af: I stand firm in the storm. My will does not bend. The god of mankind gave me the tools to survive. I use them.
sh: The warrior who wages war on his own people — Tū's shadow is the strength that turns against kin when there are no external enemies
ar: The Unyielding Will / The God Who Stood Firm
cb: Tāne (the god of forest and peace — who creates and nurtures) counterbalances Tū (the god of war who destroys and hunts). Peace and war, creation and destruction, the two necessary principles of Māori cosmology.

do: Peace, agriculture, the kumara (sweet potato), the feminine-associated foods, the arts of peace
at: Rongo is the Māori god of peace, agriculture and the kumara (sweet potato — the most sacred crop of the Māori). He is associated with the feminine, with the peaceful arts, with the cultivation of food. He is the heart chakra deity of the Polynesian world: the one who makes peace possible through abundance, who sustains the community through agriculture rather than through war.
of: The first kumara (sweet potato) of the harvest returned to the earth as offering, peace ceremonies, the cultivation of gardens as sacred act, the arts of peace honoured
an: Kiwi (the gentle night bird), the garden's creatures
co: Green (the growing crop), gold (the kumara's colour), white (the peace)
dp: Friday / Venus — the peace and the cultivation
cr: Green jade, citrine (the kumara's gold), green aventurine
bp: Heart (the peace), sacral (the fertility of the garden), root (the earth that grows the kumara)
af: I cultivate peace and abundance. The garden is my prayer. The first fruits return to the earth with gratitude.
sh: The peace that requires force to maintain — Rongo's shadow is the agriculture that needs Tū's war to protect it. The peaceful cultivation always requiring the warrior's protection.
ar: The God of Peace / The Sacred Kumara
cb: Tū (the god of war — Rongo's perpetual counterpart) counterbalances Rongo (the god of peace and agriculture). War and peace, the sword and the sweet potato, in their fundamental Polynesian polarity.

do: The great goddess of death, the darkness, the night, the place where all must go
at: Hine-nui-te-pō ('Great Woman of the Night') is the Māori goddess of death — the divine principle of the night and of all who die. She was originally Hine-titama ('Dawn Maiden') — the daughter of Tāne who, discovering she had unknowingly married her own father, fled in shame to the underworld and became the goddess of death. She kills Māui when he attempts to enter her body to win immortality. She governs the final threshold.
of: Honouring the dead, prayers at the threshold of death, the acknowledgment that the death goddess was originally the daughter of creation who was wounded by its first transgression
an: The gecko (her sacred animal), the owl (the death bird)
co: Black (the night, the death), red (the blood of the first shame), the deep green-black of the night sea
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the great night
cr: Obsidian, jet, black tourmaline, dark moonstone
bp: Root (the underworld), crown (the divine principle of the night sky), the entire body in its final return to the earth
af: The great woman of the night receives all who come. Death is not the enemy — she is the daughter of creation who holds the threshold. I cross with dignity.
sh: The daughter's shame that became death — Hine-nui-te-pō's shadow is the primal wound of incest that sent the Dawn Maiden to the underworld. The light that became the dark through violation.
ar: The Dawn That Became Death / The Night's Great Goddess
cb: Tāne (her father who unknowingly transgressed — the creator of light who inadvertently created death) counterbalances Hine-nui-te-pō (the light that became death). The creator and the creation of death, the most profound Māori cosmological wound.

do: The saviour goddess, the divine light at the crossroads, the initiator into the mysteries
at: Hekate Soteira ('Hekate the Saviour') is the initiatory, saving aspect of Hekate — not the frightening crossroads crone of popular imagination but the divine light-bearer who saves through initiation into the mysteries. In the Chaldean Oracles (2nd century CE) she is the World Soul, the intermediary between the first and second divine principles. She was a major figure in late antique theology, systematically marginalised as Christianity gained power.
of: The Chaldean Oracle ceremonies, the torch as her sacred instrument of illumination (not just darkness), the initiation into her mysteries as the highest spiritual practice
an: Dog (always), lion, serpent, the torch (not an animal but her most sacred implement)
co: Gold and white (the saviour's light), not only black (the crossroads crone)
dp: Monday / Moon — but the full moon as her Soteira aspect, not only the dark moon
cr: Golden topaz (the saviour's light), labradorite, clear quartz, black moonstone
el: Fire (the torch of the saviour's light) / Earth / Ether
bp: Crown (the divine mediator between first and second principles), third eye (the vision of the cosmic fire), root (the chthonic foundation she also governs)
af: The saviour goddess lights my way at the triple crossroads. Her torch is the divine light at the threshold of initiation. I am saved through her fire.
sh: The saviour who cannot be reached — Hekate Soteira was so thoroughly marginalised that few people today know this aspect of her at all. The saving face eclipsed by the frightening face.
ar: The Saving Light / The Initiatory Goddess
cb: Hekate as the Crossroads Crone (her dark, frightening, underworld face) counterbalances Hekate Soteira (her light, saving, initiatory face). The same goddess in her complete polarity — the dark face and the light face of the threshold.

do: The infinite sky, the body of stars, the darkness and the light, the space in which all things occur
at: Nuit in Aleister Crowley's Thelemic system — drawn from the Egyptian sky goddess Nut — is the supreme feminine cosmic principle: the infinite body of the night sky, the space in which all events occur, the 'continuity of existence' (as Crowley described her). Her complement is Hadit (the infinitely small point of existence). The opening of the Book of the Law: 'I am Nuit, and my word is six and fifty.' She is the crown at its most absolute cosmic scale.
of: The Thelemic ceremony under the night sky, the contemplation of the infinite space as the divine body, the Star Ruby and Star Sapphire rituals, offerings made under the stars
an: None — she IS the sky and all the stars
co: Deep black (the infinite space), gold (the stars that are her body)
dp: Sunday — the day of the cosmic light
cr: Clear quartz (the transparent infinite), dark lapis, star sapphire, meteorite (from her body)
bp: Crown (the infinite above), the entire body as a fragment of her infinite body of stars
af: I am Nuit. I am space and all that is in space. There is no part of me that is not of the gods. Come unto me. I am the infinite sky.
sh: The infinite that overwhelms the individual — Nuit's shadow is the cosmic scale that makes the personal seem irrelevant, the vastness that swallows rather than contains
ar: The Infinite Star Body / The Space That Contains All
cb: Hadit (the infinitely small point — the complementary Thelemic cosmic principle) counterbalances Nuit (the infinitely large space). The Thelemic supreme polarity: infinite space and the infinite point, the macrocosm and the microcosm.

do: The crow of the battlefield, the cauldron of prophecy, the screaming fate-seer, one face of the Morrigan
at: Badb ('the scald-crow') is the most psychic face of the Morrigan — the crow who screams over battlefields and prophesies death and victory before the battle begins. She is the 'cauldron of death' — the battlefield from which the dead emerge to fight again in myth. Her screaming cry (the 'Badb's screech') was the Celtic version of the banshee's wail. She was systematically marginalised as Irish society Christianised.
of: Crow feathers honoured (never taken without permission), the acceptance of prophetic visions (however unwelcome), the willingness to know what is coming rather than avoiding the knowledge
an: Crow (she IS the crow), the raven, the hooded crow
co: Black (the crow), blood red (the battlefield), the grey of the death-fog she can create
dp: Monday and Tuesday — the prophetic moon and the battlefield
cr: Obsidian (the crow's dark wisdom), dark garnet, black kyanite
bp: Third eye (the prophetic crow's sight), solar plexus (the battlefield), root (the fate already written before the first blow is struck)
af: I see what is coming. The crow's sight is mine. I face what I know with the courage of the battlefield prophet who cannot change what she sees but can prepare those she loves.
sh: The prophecy that becomes the cause — the crow's sight that, by predicting the death, makes the warrior give up before the battle. The doom that fulfils itself through the despair it creates.
ar: The Battlefield Prophet / The Crow of Fate
cb: Brigid (the healer who tends the wounds after the battle Badb prophesied) counterbalances Badb (the crow who sees the battle before it happens). The prophet of death counterbalanced by the healer of the wounded.

do: The supreme sky deity of the Luhya people, the benevolent creator
at: Wele Gumali ('the creator high up') is the supreme sky deity of the Luhya people of western Kenya and eastern Uganda — a gentle, benevolent creator who made the world in stages. The Luhya cosmological narrative describes creation over six days (interestingly parallel to Genesis but entirely independent). He is the supreme divine of one of the largest Bantu peoples of East Africa.
of: Morning prayers facing the rising sun, the honoring of the creative process, righteous living
an: None — Wele Gumali is beyond animal representation
co: White (the divine sky), blue (the sky where he dwells)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: Clear quartz, white calcite
bp: Crown, the entire being, every breath
af: The benevolent creator sees me each morning. I face the sunrise as prayer. The creator who made all things in stages made me too.
sh: The distant creator — the supreme who created in six days and now watches from above
ar: The Luhya Supreme / The Gentle Creator
cb: The earth (the feminine receiving principle of the Luhya) counterbalances Wele Gumali (the sky masculine creator). Sky and earth in their East African expression.

do: Iron, war, labour, the forge, clearing paths, surgery, railroads, all metal industry
at: Ogun in the Cuban Santería/Lucumí tradition has developed specific characteristics: he is the patron of surgeons and of all who work with metal tools, he lives in the forest in a shack (not a palace — he is the divine worker, not the divine king), and his sacred colours are specifically black and green. He is syncretised with Saint Peter in Cuban Catholicism. He is the working man's deity: the iron that builds the modern world.
of: Iron objects, railroad spikes (his sacred object in Cuba), palm oil poured on iron, green and black cloth, cigars, rum, machetes
an: Dog (his constant companion), snake
co: Black and green (his specific Cuban colours)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the Cuban Ogun's most sacred day
cr: Iron meteorite, hematite, black tourmaline, green jasper
bp: Muscles and bones, the surgeon's hands, the root chakra (the iron in the earth), the throat (the oath sworn on iron)
af: My work is sacred. The iron in my hands creates and protects. I clear the path with discipline and precision.
sh: The warrior who cannot put down his weapons even in peace — the iron that becomes violence rather than construction
ar: The Cuban Iron Deity / The Working Man's Orisha
cb: Oshun (honey, sweetness, the feminine river) counterbalances Ogun (Cuba) (iron, the machete, the masculine forge). The iron and the honey — the myth of Oshun drawing Ogun from the forest with honey is the most important mythology of their relationship.

do: The hunt, justice, the law, precision, the tracker who never misses — especially the Cuban jail system
at: Oshosi in the Cuban Santería tradition has taken on specific significance: because so many enslaved Cubans were imprisoned, Oshosi became associated with the legal system and with prisons — the Orisha who governs detention, legal proceedings, and justice within the system. He is invoked to protect from false arrest and to ensure justice in the courts. He lives in the forest with Ogun but his arrow reaches the city's courtrooms.
of: Blue and yellow cloth (his Cuban colours), honey and tobacco, a small iron bow and arrow, offerings for those who are imprisoned or facing legal troubles
an: Hawk (the hunter's bird), the hound (the tracker's animal)
co: Blue and yellow (his specific Cuban colours)
dp: Monday / Mercury — the hunter's precision
cr: Blue sapphire, citrine, yellow jasper, flint
bp: Eyes (the hunter's sight), hands (the bow), root (the forest tracking)
af: My aim is true and my cause is just. The hunter's arrow reaches through courthouse walls. Justice will find me.
sh: The law weaponised — Oshosi's justice in the hands of an unjust system becomes the instrument of oppression
ar: The Cuban Justice Hunter / The Prison Protector
cb: Ogun (iron, the law's enforcement) counterbalances Oshosi (the hunt for justice, the tracker). The enforcer and the tracker, both in service of justice.

do: The wounded wife, fidelity, the love that sacrifices too much, the river Obba
at: Obba in the Cuban Santería tradition has become the most poignant Orisha of wounded love — she cut off her ear and put it in Shango's stew as a love potion (at the malicious suggestion of Oshun), and when Shango discovered the deception he banished her. She is the patron of those who have been betrayed in love, of those who have sacrificed too much for a love that did not receive the sacrifice gracefully.
of: Purple and pink candles (her Cuban colours), offerings at rivers where the Obba and Osun rivers meet, prayers for healing of the deepest love wounds
an: Cow (the gentle, loyal animal), the turtle
co: Purple and pink (her specific Cuban colours — darker than her general Yoruba representation)
dp: Saturday — the day of love's enduring grief
cr: Amethyst, rose quartz, dark pink tourmaline
bp: Heart (the wound), ear (the ear she cut), root (the abandoned wife's earth)
af: My love was real even if its expression was misguided. The wound of betrayal does not mean my love was wrong. I am healed.
sh: The sacrifice that becomes the wound — Obba's shadow is the one who loves so desperately they destroy themselves trying to be loved back
ar: The Wounded Love / The Cuban Ear Goddess
cb: Oshun (who tricked Obba — the rival in love, the one who suggested the disastrous love potion) counterbalances Obba (the wounded wife). The bitterest feminine polarity: the rival and the betrayed.

do: The deep ocean, the unfathomable, the Middle Passage, the ancestral wealth of African identity
at: Olokun in both the Nigerian and Cuban traditions — but with a profound addition in the diaspora context. Olokun is the Orisha who holds the memories of the Middle Passage: the enslaved Africans who were thrown overboard, who died at sea, who rest in the deepest part of the Atlantic Ocean. Olokun holds these ancestors in the deepest water. To propitiate Olokun in the diaspora is also to acknowledge the Middle Passage.
of: Deep blue and black candles, offerings of clay pots sealed and lowered into water, prayers for those lost in the Middle Passage, the acknowledgment of the ocean's depths as ancestral holding space
an: Fish (deep-sea fish), the whale, the octopus
co: Deep blue, black, silver — the colours of the unfathomable
dp: Monday / Neptune — the depth
cr: Dark aquamarine, black pearl, dark sapphire, labradorite
bp: The deep unconscious body, the lungs, the ancestral memory stored in the deepest tissue
af: I honour those who rest in the deep ocean. Olokun holds the ancestors of the Middle Passage. The deepest water holds the deepest memory and the deepest wealth.
sh: The depths that cannot be healed — the ancestral trauma so deep it has never been acknowledged, the Middle Passage grief that has never been given sacred space
ar: The Middle Passage Memorial / The Unfathomable Depth
cb: Yemoja (the ocean's nurturing surface) counterbalances Olokun (the ocean's unfathomable depth). The known face of the ocean counterbalanced by the unknown face — both necessary, both real.

do: The divine trickster, the gatekeeper, the first and last invocation, the divine child who is also the eldest
at: Eshu Elegba (also Elegua in Santería, Exu in Candomblé, Legba in Vodou) in his complete pan-diaspora expression — the divine principle that is simultaneously the first and the last, the youngest and the oldest, the smallest and the most powerful. He stands at every crossroads in every African diaspora tradition. Without his permission nothing can happen. With his blessing everything becomes possible.
of: The first and last libation in every ceremony, rum, candy, cigars, black and red offerings, crossroads ceremonies, the number three (his sacred number)
an: Rooster (the announcer), dog (the crossroads animal), the mouse (the tiny trickster), the spider (Anansi's relative)
co: Black and red (his universal diaspora colours)
dp: Monday — the gate between all days, the first and the last
cr: Obsidian (the trickster's dark wisdom), red jasper (the crossroads fire), black tourmaline
el: Earth / Ether — the crossroads spans all elements
bp: Feet (the crossroads walker), throat (all communication goes through him), root (the first foundation), crown (he owns the road to the divine)
af: Eshu Elegba opens the way before me. The first word I speak is his name. Every threshold I cross is his. Thank you, Eshu.
sh: The closed crossroads — the divine trickster whose tests cannot be passed, the gate that remains locked, the communication that cannot flow
ar: The Universal Gatekeeper / The Pan-Diaspora Divine Trickster
cb: Olodumare / Bondye / Nzambi (the supreme — the destination beyond every crossroads) counterbalances Eshu Elegba (the gatekeeper at every crossroads). The infinite destination counterbalanced by the keeper of every threshold.

do: The Ogun River, the mother of all waters in Nigeria, the original Yemoja before the diaspora
at: Yemoja in her original Yoruba-Nigerian context — she is specifically the Orisha of the Ogun River in Nigeria (not the ocean, which belongs to Olokun). She is the source from which the diaspora expressions (Yemanjá in Brazil, La Sirène in Haiti) flow. She governs the river's fresh, nurturing water. She is the mother whose river feeds the land.
of: River ceremonies on the Ogun River, blue and white offerings, fish, the honouring of the river itself as the goddess's body
an: Fish (river fish — she is a river goddess in Nigeria, not an ocean goddess), the mermaid (her diaspora image), duck
co: Blue and white (her universal colours), the green of the river's banks
dp: Saturday / Moon — the water mother
cr: Blue quartz, aquamarine, white calcite, river stones
el: Water (the river — specifically the fresh, nurturing, maternal water)
bp: Womb (the river mother's nurturing), sacral (the creative maternal water), heart (the mother's love for all her children)
af: I come from the river. The mother of waters holds me. I am nourished by the fresh maternal water of Yemoja's original Nigerian home.
sh: The river gone dry — Yemoja's shadow is the river that has been polluted or dammed, the mother whose water cannot flow
ar: The Original River Mother / The Ogun River Goddess
cb: Olokun (the deep ocean — who governs what is beyond the river's reach) counterbalances Yemoja (the nurturing river). The river and the ocean, the known water and the unknowable depth.

do: The thunder, the supreme divine of the Guaraní, the creative force behind all things
