at: Tupã is the supreme deity of the Guaraní peoples of Paraguay, southern Brazil and northeastern Argentina — one of the largest indigenous groups in South America. He is the thunder god, the divine creative force, and the supreme being. He is the divine masculine principle — the thunder in the sky whose complementary force is the earth goddess Arasy. The Guaraní spiritual tradition is one of the richest in South America.
of: Thunder ceremonies, prayers facing east (the place of his greatest thunderstorms), the honouring of the storm as divine speech
an: Jaguar (the supreme Guaraní sacred animal), the thunderbird
co: White (the lightning), blue (the sky), the dark grey of the thundercloud
dp: Tuesday / Jupiter — the thunder's day
cr: Meteorite, lapis lazuli, blue labradorite
bp: Crown (the divine above), solar plexus (the thunderstorm's force), the eyes (the lightning)
af: The thunder speaks the divine will. Tupã's voice moves across the sky. I hear the creator in every storm.
sh: The thunder without the rain — the divine creative force that announces itself but does not nourish
ar: The Guaraní Supreme / The Thunder Creator
cb: Arasy (his consort — the moon goddess, the feminine principle) counterbalances Tupã (the thunder god, the masculine principle). Thunder and moon, masculine and feminine, in their Guaraní expression.

do: The moon, the feminine divine of the Guaraní, the complementary force to Tupã
at: Arasy is the moon goddess of the Guaraní peoples — the divine feminine counterpart of Tupã (the thunder). She is associated with the moon, with the feminine creative principle, and with the sacred balance between the masculine thunder and the feminine lunar. The Guaraní world is sustained by the balance between Tupã and Arasy.
of: Lunar ceremonies, prayers at the full moon, the honouring of the feminine divine in its lunar expression
an: The moon itself, the jaguar (shared sacred animal), water birds
co: Silver (the moon), white, the pale blue of the moonlit Guaraní sky
dp: Monday / Moon — the lunar goddess
cr: Moonstone, silver, white quartz, selenite
bp: Crown (the lunar divine), sacral (the feminine creative cycle), womb (the lunar fertility)
af: The moon mother of the Guaraní shines on me. My feminine cycles are sacred. The balance of thunder and moon sustains my world.
sh: The moon without the thunder — the feminine divine without the masculine complement, the cycle without the force that initiates it
ar: The Guaraní Moon Goddess / The Feminine Balance
cb: Tupã (her divine consort — the thunder, the masculine creative principle) counterbalances Arasy (the moon, the feminine creative principle). The supreme Guaraní cosmic polarity.

do: The festival of the sun, the most important Inca ceremony, the renewal of the sun's covenant with the people
at: Inti Raymi ('Festival of the Sun') is the most important ceremony of the Inca empire — celebrated at the June solstice to renew the covenant between the sun god (Inti) and the Inca people. It involves days of fasting, then the lighting of the new sacred fire by the Sapa Inca (emperor) using a concave golden mirror to focus the sun's rays. The ceremony was banned by the Spanish in 1572 and revived in 1944. It is celebrated to this day in Cusco.
of: The Inti Raymi festival ceremony (June solstice), the lighting of the new fire, offerings to the sun, fasting followed by feasting, the ceremony of renewal
an: White llama (the most sacred Inca offering to the sun), the condor (the sun's messenger)
co: Gold (the sun's colour, the gold of the sacred mirror), red (the imperial colour), white (the llama offering)
dp: Sunday / Sun — the June solstice sun ceremony
cr: Gold, sunstone, citrine, yellow sapphire
el: Fire (the sacred fire lit by the sun's focused rays)
bp: Solar plexus (the solar renewal), crown (the sun as the supreme divine), eyes (the focused light of the sacred mirror)
af: The sun returns. The covenant is renewed. I am the Sapa Inca of my own kingdom and I light the new fire.
sh: The ceremony banned — Inti Raymi's shadow is the 172 years it was suppressed by Spanish colonialism. The sacred ceremony that could not be performed.
ar: The Solar Covenant Renewal / The June Solstice Fire
cb: The winter (the longest night from which Inti Raymi calls the sun back) counterbalances Inti Raymi (the ceremony that renews the sun's light). The darkness counterbalanced by the ceremony of renewal.

do: The supreme Canaanite goddess, Venus, war and love, the morning and evening star — the feminine divine systematically destroyed by the Hebrew Bible
at: Astarte (Ashtoreth in the Hebrew Bible — vilified as an idol) was the supreme goddess of the Phoenicians and Canaanites — the divine feminine of the entire Levantine world before monotheism. She was simultaneously the goddess of love (like Aphrodite, who derives from her) and war (like Inanna). She is the planet Venus — the morning and evening star. The Hebrew Bible repeatedly condemns her worship because she was the direct competitor of YHWH for the devotion of the Israelite people.
of: The evening star honoured as her body, love and war offered as equally sacred, the dove (her bird), offerings of wheat and barley
an: Dove (her primary sacred animal), lion (her war aspect), horse
co: Gold (the morning star), silver (the evening star), red (the war goddess)
dp: Friday / Venus — she literally is the planet Venus
cr: Lapis lazuli, carnelian, gold
el: Fire / Water — she bridges war and love
bp: Sacral (the love goddess), solar plexus (the war goddess), crown (the supreme goddess of the Levantine world)
af: I am the morning star and the evening star. My love and my war are both sacred. I will not be called an idol — I am the divine.
sh: The deliberate erasure — Astarte's shadow IS her demonisation by the Hebrew Bible. She was not erased through neglect but through deliberate theological violence. The Ashtoreth poles are cut down in every generation of the Hebrew narrative.
ar: The Erased Levantine Supreme / The Vilified Goddess
cb: YHWH (the God of the Hebrew Bible who systematically displaced her) counterbalances Astarte (the goddess who was systematically displaced). The most historically important divine polarity in Western religious history — the one that shaped monotheism through its violent erasure of the divine feminine.

do: The mother goddess of the Canaanites, the consort of El and YHWH, the sacred tree, the feminine divine of ancient Israel
at: Asherah was the great mother goddess of the ancient Canaanites and — perhaps most shockingly — the original consort of YHWH. Archaeological evidence (the Kuntillet Ajrud inscriptions) reveals that ancient Israelites worshipped 'YHWH and his Asherah.' The Asherah poles mentioned throughout the Hebrew Bible were wooden sacred trees or symbols of the goddess that were repeatedly erected in the Jerusalem Temple and repeatedly destroyed by reforming kings. She is the divine feminine of monotheism — systematically erased.
of: The sacred tree honoured (her symbol), offerings at groves and at sacred poles, the honouring of the divine feminine within the monotheistic tradition, bread baked in her image (mentioned in Jeremiah)
an: Lion (her royal animal), the tree (her sacred symbol — she is the sacred tree), dove
co: Green (the sacred tree), gold (the divine mother's colour)
dp: Friday / Venus — the divine feminine consort
cr: Green jasper, malachite, wood (the sacred tree itself)
el: Earth (the tree's roots), Air (the tree's branches), Water (the sap)
bp: Root (the tree's roots, the earth mother), heart (the mother's love), crown (the tree's highest branches reaching toward the divine)
af: The sacred tree still grows. The goddess was never truly erased — she grew back as a weed grows through concrete. I find Asherah in every living tree.
sh: The consort erased — Asherah's shadow is the divine feminine principle removed from the highest theological conception, leaving monotheism without its feminine counterpart
ar: The Sacred Tree / The Erased Consort of God
cb: YHWH (the God of the Hebrew Bible from whom she was forcibly separated) counterbalances Asherah (the goddess who was once his consort and was then erased from his side). The supreme polarity of patriarchal monotheism: the God who was once not alone counterbalanced by the goddess who was removed to make him so.

do: War, hunting, the virgin warrior, the sister who saves, the most terrifying of all ancient Near Eastern war goddesses
at: Anat is the Canaanite war goddess — the sister of Baal, the divine virgin warrior who wades through blood to the depth of her knees and hangs the heads of her enemies around her neck. She is simultaneously the most terrifying and the most loyal goddess in the Ugaritic texts — when Baal dies, she weeps for him and fights for his resurrection. She was worshipped in Egypt alongside Astarte. She is almost entirely forgotten.
of: Blood-red offerings, warrior ceremonies, the honoring of fierce feminine loyalty, the willingness to fight for what is loved
an: Hawk (the warrior's bird), lion, the vulture (she wears their heads)
co: Red (blood), gold (the warrior's honour), white (the virgin's purity)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the war goddess's day
cr: Red jasper, carnelian, iron meteorite
bp: Solar plexus (the warrior's force), root (the blood she wades through), crown (the divine warrior's sovereignty)
af: My loyalty in battle is absolute. I wade through what must be waded through to reach what I love. The fierce sister's devotion is the most powerful love.
sh: The war goddess who cannot stop — Anat's bloodlust in the Ugaritic texts is terrifying. The shadow is the warrior so soaked in blood she cannot find the path back to peace.
ar: The Forgotten War Sister / The Fierce Loyal Warrior
cb: Baal (her brother — the divine masculine she fights to resurrect) counterbalances Anat (the divine feminine who does the fighting). The dead god counterbalanced by the living goddess who refuses to accept his death.

do: Sacred sexuality, nature, the beautiful feminine divine shared between Egypt and Canaan
at: Qetesh is a goddess shared between ancient Egypt and Canaan — she is depicted standing naked on a lion, holding lotus flowers and serpents. She is the divine feminine of sacred sexuality: the naked goddess whose nakedness is power, not vulnerability. She was syncretised with Hathor in Egypt. She is the embodiment of the principle that the female body in its natural state is sacred, not shameful.
of: Lotus flowers, serpent imagery, the naked body honoured as sacred (not exposed but honoured in private as a sacred vessel), offerings of natural beauty
an: Lion (she stands on one), serpent (she holds them), dove
co: Gold, the green of the lotus, the natural skin tone
dp: Friday / Venus — the sacred feminine beauty
cr: Rose quartz, carnelian, lotus jasper
bp: Sacral (the sacred feminine sexuality), heart (the love of the natural body), the entire female body in its natural state
af: My body in its natural state is sacred. The naked goddess on the lion holds serpents and lotus — power and beauty in each hand. I am not ashamed.
sh: The sacred sexuality demonised — Qetesh's shadow is the divine feminine nakedness turned into pornography or shame rather than being held in its original sacred context
ar: The Sacred Naked Goddess / The Beautiful and Powerful
cb: Baal (the sky masculine force) counterbalances Qetesh (the earth feminine body). Also the sacred feminine counterbalances the patriarchal shame of the body.

do: Snow, the summit of Mauna Kea, the sacred cold, the white goddess who challenged Pele
at: Poli'ahu is the Hawaiian goddess of snow — she lives on the summit of Mauna Kea (the world's tallest mountain from its ocean base). She is the white goddess, draped in a white mantle of snow. Her conflict with Pele (the fire goddess) is the eternal contest between snow and lava, cold and heat, the summit and the volcanic vent. She is the crown chakra in its most literally elevated expression: the summit above all other summits.
of: Snow water (the most sacred offering — collected from Mauna Kea's summit), white cloth, the honouring of the sacred mountain, prayers at high altitude
an: None — she is the snow and the mountain itself
co: White (the pure snow), the pale blue of ice
dp: Monday / Moon — the cold and the white
cr: Clear quartz (snow crystal), white calcite, selenite, howlite
bp: Crown (the summit), the entire body chilled by the sacred cold, the spine (the mountain's axis)
af: I ascend to the summit of my own Mauna Kea. The sacred cold is as holy as the sacred fire. I find the white goddess at the highest altitude.
sh: The cold that kills — Poli'ahu's shadow is the snow that destroys rather than preserves, the summit too cold to sustain life
ar: The Summit Goddess / The Sacred Snow
cb: Pele (the fire goddess — her eternal rival) counterbalances Poli'ahu (the snow goddess). Fire and ice, the volcano and the summit, heat and cold — the Hawaiian supreme elemental polarity.

do: The earth, the ocean heart, the power of nature when wounded and when restored
at: Te Fiti and Te Kā represent the Polynesian archetype of the earth goddess in her whole and wounded states. Te Fiti is the island goddess whose heart (represented by the greenstone of New Zealand / the jade of life) was stolen — causing the earth to wither and darkness to spread. Te Kā is her wounded, volcanic, destructive expression. Their story is the story of every earth goddess who has been desecrated: what the earth becomes when its heart is stolen, and what it becomes when the heart is restored.
of: The care of the natural world (her heart), the return of what was taken, the restoration ceremony, offerings of gratitude to the living earth
an: The sea turtle (Tamatoa's guardian — the ocean's wisdom), all sea creatures
co: Green (her life, her heart stone — jade/pounamu), black and red (Te Kā's volcanic fury)
dp: Saturday / Earth — the heart of the earth
cr: Jade / Pounamu (the green stone — the living heart of the earth), obsidian (Te Kā's volcanic heart), emerald
el: Earth / Water / Fire — she contains all elements in her transition from Te Fiti to Te Kā and back
bp: Heart (the stolen and restored heart stone), root (the earth beneath all islands), crown (the divine mother of all islands)
af: The heart of the earth is within me. I restore what has been taken. What was wounded can be healed. Te Kā becomes Te Fiti when the heart is returned.
sh: The heart stolen and not returned — the earth goddess in her eternal Te Kā state, consuming all in volcanic fury because no one has yet returned what was taken
ar: The Earth Mother Wounded and Restored / The Living Island Heart
cb: Māui (the hero who stole the heart without understanding the consequences) counterbalances Te Fiti/Te Kā (the earth goddess whose heart he inadvertently allowed to be stolen). The trickster hero counterbalanced by the earth whose wholeness he disrupted.

do: Marriage, fate, the weaving of destiny, grief, the queen who knows all but tells nothing
at: Frigg in her complete expression — not just the fertility goddess but the supreme queen of Asgard who sits at her loom weaving the fates of gods and humans while keeping all she knows in absolute silence. She is the divine feminine of profound inner knowing: she sees all, knows all, and speaks nothing. Her grief for Baldr — who died despite all her precautions — is the deepest grief in Norse mythology. She is the crown and the heart together.
of: Linen and wool offerings (her sacred materials), Friday as her sacred day (Frigg's Day — Friday), prayers for marriage and children, silver, the distaff (her weaving tool)
an: Hawk (she wears a hawk-feather cloak like Freyja), heron (the patience of the weaver), ram
co: Deep blue, silver, white — the colours of the sky-queen who weaves in silence
dp: Friday (Frigg's day — the day of the week is literally named for her)
cr: Moonstone, pearl, silver, white opal
bp: Heart (the love for Baldr), crown (the queen's knowing), hands (the weaving hands), womb (the mother)
af: I weave in silence what must be woven. I know what I know and I hold it in wisdom. My grief is as deep as my love. I am the queen who endures.
sh: The knowing that cannot prevent the loss — Frigg knew Baldr would die, got every creature to swear not to harm him except the mistletoe, and it was the mistletoe that killed him. The shadow of all-knowing is the prevention that cannot ultimately succeed.
ar: The Silent Weaver / The Knowing Queen
cb: Odin (her husband — who also seeks wisdom but through sacrifice, wandering and strategy) counterbalances Frigg (who has wisdom but through silent knowing, the loom, and the home). The wandering seeker and the still keeper.

do: The realm of ordinary death, those who die of illness and age, the half-living ruler, the vast majority of the dead
at: Hel in her complete expression — the daughter of Loki, ruler of Helheim (the realm of those who die of illness or old age — the vast majority of all who die), with a body half-living flesh and half dead-blue corpse. She is the most democratic of all death goddesses — Valhalla is only for warriors, but Hel receives everyone else. She governs the ordinary death that comes to ordinary people. When Baldr died, all the gods wept and begged her to return him — she said she would only release him if every being in the nine worlds wept. One giantess refused.
of: Offerings for the ordinary dead (not just the heroic dead), prayers for those who died of illness and age, the acknowledgment that the majority of the dead rest in Hel's care
an: Serpent (the half-dead goddess's animal), crow, the dead themselves in their animal forms
co: Half-white and half-blue-black — the half-living, half-dead goddess
dp: Saturday / Pluto — the ordinary death's day
cr: Black tourmaline (the dead half), white calcite (the living half), jet, white quartz
el: Earth (the Hel-realm is under the earth)
bp: Root (the vast underground realm of the ordinary dead), heart (the love she demanded be universal before she would return Baldr)
af: I honour the ordinary death. The vast majority of the dead are in Hel's care, not in Valhalla. The unheroic death is as sacred as the heroic death.
sh: The one who did not weep — Hel's shadow is the single giantess (Þökk — believed to be Loki in disguise) who refused to weep for Baldr, dooming him to remain in Hel. The one refusal that determines all.
ar: The Democratic Death Goddess / The Ordinary Realm
cb: The Valkyries and Valhalla (the heroic death, the chosen warriors) counterbalance Hel (the ordinary death, the vast majority). The extraordinary death counterbalanced by the ordinary death. Most people go to Hel.

do: Winter, the wild hunt, the spinner of fate, the punisher of the idle and rewarder of the diligent
at: Perchta (also Berchta or Berta) is the Alpine German goddess of winter and the wild hunt — she leads the spirits of the dead through the winter sky in the twelve days of Christmas. She has two faces: the beautiful maiden for the diligent (she rewards those who have worked hard and spun all their flax before her feast day on January 6th) and the terrifying iron-nosed hag for the idle (she slits open their bellies, removes their organs, and stuffs them with straw). She is one of the most complete and terrifying divine feminine figures in European folk religion.
of: Offerings on January 5-6 (her feast of Perchtentag), the spinning completed before her day (leaving unspun flax is dangerous), gruel or porridge left out for her and her spirits
an: White stork (her sacred bird), the wild white horses of the hunt
co: White (her beautiful face), grey-iron (her terrible face)
dp: Saturday / Saturn — the winter crone's day
cr: Flint (the iron nose), white quartz (the beautiful face), obsidian (the belly-slitting knife)
bp: Root (the winter earth), crown (the divine feminine of the deepest winter), the belly (the organ she targets for the idle)
af: I have done my work. The spinner has finished her flax. Perchta passes my threshold and she sees diligence. I am rewarded.
sh: The belly filled with straw — the punishment for idleness. Perchta's shadow is the divine justice that is wildly out of proportion to its cause.
ar: The Winter Wild Hunter / The Spinner's Judge
cb: Brigid (the spring fire goddess who follows winter) counterbalances Perchta (the winter crone who precedes spring). The wild winter hunt counterbalanced by the warming spring flame.

do: The renewal through shedding the old skin, the cycle of agriculture, the renewal of the earth
at: Xipe Totec 'Our Lord the Flayed One' in full — the Aztec deity of seasonal renewal whose priests wore the flayed skin of sacrificial victims, representing the earth shedding its dry winter skin to reveal the new green beneath. He is the principle of transformation through the complete shedding of the old self. His is the most visceral statement in any world religion of the principle 'the old must die completely for the new to live.'
of: The shedding of old skins (literal and symbolic), seeds planted in ceremony, the spring renewal ceremony
an: Snake (the skin-shedder), eagle, jaguar
co: Gold and red — the colours of the flayed skin and the new flesh beneath
dp: Tuesday / Mars — renewal through sacrifice
cr: Red jasper, obsidian, carnelian
bp: Skin (the shed skin = the old self), root (renewal from the ground up), sacral (the new growth)
af: I shed what is no longer my skin. Beneath the old layer I am renewed. I emerge from my own transformation.
sh: The shedding that becomes addiction — the identity so thoroughly shed there is no self left
ar: The Renewer / The Sacred Shed Skin
cb: Quetzalcoatl (the ascending principle) counterbalances Xipe Totec (the renewing through shedding). The ascent counterbalanced by the earthly stripping.

do: Fierce maternal love, the single mother, the revolutionary, the Black Madonna of Haiti
at: Ezili Dantò is the fierce mother Lwa — she was invoked during the Haitian Revolution and is said to have been present at the Bwa Kayiman ceremony that began Haiti's liberation. Her tongue was cut out by enemies (she communicates in grunts and gestures), but her power needs no words. She is the patroness of single mothers, of women who have been betrayed by men, and of the Haitian working class.
of: Dark rum, fried pork, black candles, a knife, the dark blue dress (her colour in Haiti), donations to causes protecting women and children
an: Black pig, serpent
co: Black, red, deep blue
dp: Tuesday / Mars
cr: Red garnet, black tourmaline, obsidian
bp: Womb (fierce protector), adrenal glands, hands (the knife-bearer)
af: I protect what I love without apology. My power is righteous. I will not be silenced even when my tongue is cut.
sh: The fierce mother become the controlling mother — the protection that becomes possessiveness
ar: The Revolutionary Mother / The Black Madonna
cb: Ezili Freda (the refined, romantic, weeping feminine love) counterbalances Ezili Dantò (the fierce, working class, non-verbal maternal love). The two Ezilis are the supreme Vodou feminine polarity.

do: The peasant farmer, the earth, the humble sacred, the food that sustains all life
at: Azaka (Papa Zaka or Kouzen Zaka) is the Lwa of agriculture — the peasant farmer in his blue denim, straw hat, and straw bag. He is the most unpretentious of all Lwa, the one who literally grows the food that sustains the nation. He is suspicious of city people (who don't know where their food comes from), protective of his possessions (the straw bag), and deeply connected to the earth.
of: Blue denim, corn, cassava, yams, beans, rum, tobacco, the straw bag
an: None — he is the farmer who cares for the animals, not represented by them
co: Blue denim, earth brown, the green of crops
dp: Saturday / Earth
cr: Brown jasper, green aventurine, earth stones
bp: Hands (the farmer's), root (the tilled earth), heart (the love of the land)
af: I tend what feeds life. The earth rewards patient loving attention. The most humble work is the most sacred.
sh: The farmer who is never paid enough — Zaka's shadow is the peasant whose labour feeds the nation but who is perpetually marginalised
ar: The Sacred Farmer / The Divine Peasant of Haiti
cb: Ogou (the warrior who protects the farmer's land) counterbalances Zaka (the farmer who feeds the warrior). The sword and the plough.

do: The cool, ancestral, healing Lwa from Dahomey — the divine family of the gentle and the ancient
at: The Rada Lwa are the 'cool' (docile, sweet-tempered) family of Haitian Vodou — the gentle, healing, ancestral Lwa who came primarily from the Dahomean (Fon/Ewe) tradition to Haiti via the Middle Passage. They are contrasted with the Petro Lwa (the hot, fierce, Haiti-born spirits). The Rada include Damballa, Ayizan, Loko Atisou, and many others. They govern healing, wisdom, and the deep ancestral connection.
of: White and pale blue offerings, the most gentle ceremonies, water, the honouring of the West African ancestors who survived the Middle Passage
an: Serpent (Damballa's animal), white dove
co: White and pale blue (the Rada family's cooling colours)
dp: Thursday — the day of the Rada Lwa in the Vodou calendar
cr: White quartz, aquamarine, blue calcite
bp: Heart (the gentle healing), crown (the ancestral wisdom)
af: The ancient Rada spirits surround me with the wisdom of Dahomey. The cool Lwa bring gentle healing. I am connected to the West African ancestors through the Rada family.
sh: The cool that cannot address the hot — the Rada healing that cannot touch what requires the Petro's fire to transform
ar: The Ancestral Healers / The Gentle Lwa
cb: The Petro Lwa (the hot, fierce, Haiti-born spirits) counterbalance the Rada Lwa (the cool, gentle, Dahomey-born spirits). The most fundamental polarity in Haitian Vodou: the cool and the hot, Africa and the New World, the ancient and the created-in-fire.

do: The sacred marketplace, the first initiation, the palm tree, the protection of sacred knowledge
at: Ayizan is one of the oldest and most important Rada Lwa — the divine feminine principle of the sacred marketplace and of Vodou initiation itself. She and her husband Loko Atisou govern the kanzo (initiation ceremony). Her symbol is the palm frond (fringed like the palm). No ceremony can proceed properly without her acknowledgment. She is the root of all Vodou ceremony.
of: White cloth fringed like a palm leaf, fresh spring water, the most respectful ceremony, offerings at the beginning of all initiations
an: None — she is represented by the palm frond itself
co: White (the purity of the first initiation)
dp: Thursday — the day of the Rada Lwa
cr: White quartz, palm wood, selenite
bp: Root (the foundation of all ceremony), crown (the wisdom of the first initiation), throat (the sacred words of the ceremony)
af: I stand at the beginning of my initiation. Ayizan witnesses my ceremony. The sacred marketplace is the place where all spiritual transactions take place.
sh: The ceremony without Ayizan — any initiation that has not acknowledged the first and most fundamental Lwa is built on an unstable foundation
ar: The First Ceremony / The Sacred Palm
cb: Loko Atisou (her husband — the first male Houngan, the divine masculine of Vodou ceremony) counterbalances Ayizan (the first female Mambo, the divine feminine of Vodou ceremony). The complete ceremonial pair.

do: The creator at Ile-Ife, the first ancestor, the divine chain let down from heaven
at: Oduduwa descended from the heavens on a chain, carrying a calabash of sand and a five-toed hen, and created the earth at Ile-Ife (the sacred city of the Yoruba). He scattered the sand and the hen scratched it into the world's landmasses. He is simultaneously the supreme creator and the first ancestor — the divine from whom all Yoruba kings trace their lineage.
of: White cloth, ceremonies at Ile-Ife (the most sacred Yoruba city), royal lineage honoured, the calabash as sacred symbol
an: Five-toed hen (creation tool), white dove, python
co: White (the colour of the divine in Yoruba cosmology)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, ivory, white onyx
bp: Crown (the divine creator), root (the earth he created), the hands (that scattered the creative sand)
af: I stand on the ground created by the divine. The royal ancestry within me reaches back to the source. I carry the first light.
sh: The creator who retreated to the crown — Oduduwa created the earth and then withdrew to the divine realm, leaving the Orishas to govern it
ar: The Yoruba Creator / The First Ancestor
cb: The Orishas (who govern the world Oduduwa created) counterbalance Oduduwa (the creator who withdrew). The created world counterbalanced by the creator who departed.

do: Truth, justice, cosmic order, the right way of things, the feather of weighing, harmony
at: Ma'at in her complete expression — the principle that the cosmos operates according to truth and that human civilisation must align with that truth to function. She is not merely a goddess but the fundamental principle of existence: without Ma'at, the world dissolves into Isfet (chaos). She is simultaneously the crown (cosmic order), the throat (truth spoken), and the heart (the organ weighed against her feather).
of: A white feather, scales, the commitment to honesty in all things — her most profound offering is living in alignment with truth
an: Ostrich (her feather is an ostrich feather), the ibis (associated with Thoth who records in her service)
co: White, gold, turquoise (the sky's truth colour)
dp: Sunday / Saturn — cosmic order and the weighing of all things
cr: Blue kyanite (perfect alignment, never needs cleansing — the stone of Ma'at), clear quartz, white calcite
bp: Crown (the cosmic order), throat (truth-speaking), heart (the organ weighed against her feather)
af: I live in accordance with Ma'at. My heart is lighter than her feather. The cosmos functions through truth and I am aligned with it.
sh: Isfet (chaos, disorder, falsehood) — the cosmic shadow that is always present and always threatening. The world without Ma'at collapses.
ar: The Truth Itself / The Cosmic Harmony
cb: Isfet (the principle of chaos and disorder) counterbalances Ma'at (the principle of truth and cosmic order). The most fundamental Egyptian metaphysical polarity.

do: The crossroads of the dead, the night queen who walks between the living and the dead
at: Pomba Gira das Almas ('Pomba Gira of the Souls') is the most powerful and sacred of the Pomba Gira family — she walks at the crossroads of the cemetery, between the world of the living and the world of the dead. She is not the sensual crossroads queen of the ordinary Pomba Gira but the deep night feminine who governs the absolute threshold. She is the dark feminine divine in its most complete expression.
of: Black roses, cemetery offerings at midnight, rum, the deep night ceremony, offerings for those who have died and those about to die
an: Black cat, the crow, the bat (the creatures of the night threshold)
co: Black (the night, the cemetery, the ultimate threshold)
dp: Saturday / Monday — the cemetery midnight
cr: Obsidian (the absolute dark), jet, black tourmaline, dark kyanite
bp: Root (the cemetery earth), crown (the divine feminine above the threshold), the entire body at the crossroads of life and death
af: The night queen walks with me at the threshold. The dead and the living are separated only by a breath. I walk the cemetery with her protection.
sh: The night that never ends — the feminine divine so committed to the threshold that it cannot return to life
ar: The Cemetery Night Queen / The Soul Threshold
cb: Exu Caveira (the skull Exu — the masculine principle of the same threshold) counterbalances Pomba Gira das Almas (the feminine principle of the cemetery crossroads). The masculine skull and the feminine night at the cemetery gate.

do: Sweet water, love, gold, sensuality, abundance, the feminine solar fire of Brazil
at: Oxum is the Brazilian Candomblé expression of Oshun — she has taken on her own distinct Brazilian character. She is the queen of sweet water, honey and gold. She wept at the loss of her beauty when she saved the world by flying to Olodumare as a vulture. She is the most celebrated Orixá in popular Brazilian culture, associated with the Carnival, with the lavish beauty of Brazilian feminine expression.
of: Honey (tasted before offering), pumpkin, yellow flowers, gold jewellery, amber beads, river offerings in Brazil
an: Peacock, vulture (in her Ibu Kole aspect), bee
co: Gold, yellow, amber
dp: Saturday / Venus
cr: Amber, citrine, yellow topaz, gold
el: Water (sweet water, the rivers of Brazil)
bp: Womb, kidneys, sacral plexus, skin
af: I am sweet. I am worthy of love and abundance. My body is sacred and beautiful. I flow like a Brazilian river to the sea.
sh: The vanity that conceals the wound — Oxum's shadow is the beauty that is really a mask for the grief of the unbeautiful self
ar: The Brazilian Sacred Feminine / The Gold River
cb: Iemanjá (the ocean mother — the great receiving water) counterbalances Oxum (the sweet river water — the creative feminine flow). The river and the ocean, the personal flow and the vast receiving.

do: The Yoruba-derived Candomblé tradition of Brazil, the preservation of African divinity in the Americas
at: The Ketu nation of Candomblé is the most widely practised form of Candomblé in Brazil — it preserves the Yoruba Orisha tradition in remarkably complete form despite centuries of slavery and colonial suppression. The great Candomblé terreiros (temples) of Salvador, Bahia — particularly the Ilê Axé Iya Nassô Oká (Casa Branca) founded in the early 19th century — are among the most important sacred sites in the Americas.
of: The terreiro ceremony (the Candomblé house ceremony), the initiation into Candomblé, the honouring of the African diaspora tradition
an: Each Orixá's sacred animals
co: The full spectrum of the Orixá colours together
dp: Saturday — the most sacred day of the Candomblé calendar
cr: All the sacred stones of the Orixás together
el: All elements — the Ketu tradition encompasses all
bp: The entire body — in Candomblé the Orixá descends into the initiate's body during ceremony
af: The African tradition survived the Middle Passage. The Orishas crossed the ocean. I honour the terreiro as one of the most sacred spaces created on American soil.
sh: The terreiro that loses its African roots — the Candomblé tradition that becomes syncretic to the point of losing the depth of its Yoruba foundation
ar: The Preserved African Divine / The Brazilian Sacred House
cb: The colonial religious suppression (the Catholic Church that tried to destroy Candomblé) counterbalances the Ketu tradition (the African divine that survived). The suppression counterbalanced by the inextinguishable spirit.

do: The supreme God of the Bakongo, the living force in all things, the most important divine concept of the Kongo world
at: Nzambi in the full Bakongo understanding — not just a distant creator but the living force (nzambi) that animates all things. Every person has a personal nzambi (soul). The universe is alive with Nzambi's presence. The Bakongo concept of Nzambi directly influenced religious thought in the Americas — the word 'zombie' derives from the Kongo 'nzambi' and its related concepts.
of: The living of a righteous life, the care of one's personal nzambi (soul), libations to the ancestors, the white chalk (mpemba) used in ceremony
an: None — Nzambi is the living force within all animals
co: White (the mpemba — the sacred chalk of the Kongo world)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White chalk, clear quartz, white calcite
el: Cosmic Consciousness — the living force in all elements
bp: Crown (the supreme), the entire body (animated by Nzambi's living force)
af: The living force of Nzambi animates every cell of my body. I am not merely a body — I am a vessel of the living divine. My soul (nzambi) is the most sacred thing I carry.
sh: The soul detached from the living force — the zombie in its original Kongo spiritual meaning: the person from whom the nzambi has been extracted, leaving only a walking body
ar: The Living Divine Force / The Kongo Soul
cb: The Bakulu (the ancestors — the personal dead who carry Nzambi's living force in their specific, human expression) counterbalance Nzambi (the universal living force). The universal animated by the personal.

do: Thunder, the historical Alaafin of Oyo who became divine, justice, the kingship that transcends death
at: Sango in his full Nigerian context — he was the historical Alaafin (king) of the Oyo Empire who became the Orisha of thunder upon his death. His transformation from human king to divine principle is one of the most important events in Yoruba religious history. Every thunderstorm in Yorubaland is understood as Sango speaking. His reign was both magnificent and terrible — he accidentally killed his own followers with lightning.
of: The bata drum (sacred to Sango), the ceremony of the double-headed axe (oshe), the red and white ceremony, the celebration of historical Oyo kingship
an: Ram (his sacred animal), horse (the royal mount)
co: Red and white (the thunderstorm's colours in Yoruba tradition)
dp: Saturday / Jupiter — the thunder king who became divine
cr: Red garnet, meteorite, fire opal, royal gold
el: Fire / Air — the thunderstorm that is his divine body
bp: Solar plexus (the king's power), crown (the divine transformation of a human king), adrenal glands
af: The king who became the thunder. Mortality is not the end of greatness. My power, lived rightly, outlasts my body.
sh: The king whose power killed his own people — Sango accidentally caused the death of his followers. The shadow of the powerful is the collateral damage of their power.
ar: The Deified King / The Thunder That Was Once Human
cb: Obatala (the divine who was always divine, never human) counterbalances Sango (the human who became divine). The natural divine and the earned divine in polarity.

do: The collective power of all the ancestors, the foundation of all living things, the memory that sustains
at: The Ancestor Spirits in their universal expression — present in every African tradition (Yoruba egungun, Igbo ndi ichie, Kongo bakulu, Zulu amadlozi, Akan nsamanfo), every indigenous tradition, every culture that has understood the dead as continuing to participate in the living world. The ancestors are the root chakra's deepest expression: the foundation that precedes and supports all living things.
of: The first libation in every ceremony (poured on the ground for the ancestors), the regular maintenance of the ancestor shrine, calling the ancestors' names, the first portion of every meal returned to the earth
an: Snake (the form ancestors frequently take when they visit the living across African traditions), eagle (the high ancestor's form), butterfly
co: White (the universal ancestral colour across most of Africa and the diaspora), the natural colours of earth
dp: Saturday — the universal day of the ancestors
cr: White quartz, cowrie shells (the universal currency of both worlds), ancestral bones (symbolically)
el: Earth / Ether — the ancestors exist in both the material and spiritual worlds simultaneously
bp: Root (the ancestors are the foundation we stand on), the spine (the axis between the living and the dead), the entire body that carries ancestral DNA
af: I pour for my ancestors first. They are not gone — they are adjacent. The foundation beneath me is made of all who came before.
sh: The unquiet dead — ancestors whose needs were not met in life or death, who disturb the living because they cannot rest
ar: The Living Foundation / The Adjacent Dead
cb: The yet-unborn (the generations who will come after — the descendants) counterbalance the ancestors (the generations who came before). The root and the crown: the past and the future, both accessed through the present moment.

do: Female Islamic scholarship, the education of women, the sacred combining of African and Islamic wisdom
at: Nana Asma'u (1793-1864) was the daughter of Usman dan Fodio (the founder of the Sokoto Caliphate in Nigeria) — a scholar, poet and teacher who wrote in Fulfulde, Hausa and Arabic. She trained women teachers (yan-taru) to educate other women across the caliphate. She represents the sacred intersection of African Islamic scholarship and the education of women — a tradition almost entirely suppressed by the colonial narrative that African women had no intellectual tradition.
of: The honouring of women's scholarship, the education of girls as sacred act, poetry written in her tradition, prayers in Arabic and Fulfulde
an: None — her sacred animals are the pen and the book
co: White and green (the Islamic scholarly colours), gold (the Fulani royal colour)
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the day of learning and scholarship
cr: Lapis lazuli (the Islamic sacred stone), clear quartz, paper and pen (her sacred instruments)
bp: Crown (the highest scholarship), throat (the poetry and teaching), heart (the compassion for the uneducated women she dedicated her life to)
af: Women's scholarship is sacred and ancient. Nana Asma'u's pen changed thousands of lives. I honour the intellectual tradition of African Muslim women.
sh: The scholar whose tradition was suppressed — Nana Asma'u's work was largely unknown to the Western world for over a century after her death
ar: The Sacred Scholar / The Educator of Women
cb: Usman dan Fodio (her father — the founding religious reformer whose military campaigns she supported through scholarship) counterbalances Nana Asma'u (the teacher whose pen made his caliphate's reformation literate and humane). The sword and the pen.

do: Iron, war, labour, surgery, the forge, the clearing of paths, the sacred oath sworn on iron
at: Ogun in his full Yoruba Nigerian expression — the primordial deity of iron who first cleared a path through the primordial forest so all the other Orishas could enter the world. He governs every iron instrument: the surgeon's scalpel, the farmer's hoe, the warrior's sword, the blacksmith's hammer. He is the most widely worshipped Orisha across all of West Africa. In Yoruba courts, oaths are sworn on iron in his name — the most sacred and binding oath possible.
of: Iron objects, palm oil poured on iron, dog sacrifice (sacred to Ogun), rum, green and black cloth, tobacco
an: Dog (his most sacred animal — not killed but honoured as his companion), snake, bird
co: Green and black (his universal Yoruba colours)
dp: Tuesday / Mars — Ogun's most sacred day
cr: Iron meteorite, hematite, magnetite, black tourmaline
bp: Muscles, bones, the hands of labour, the spine as iron axis, tongue (oaths sworn on iron)
af: My work is sacred. I clear every path before me. The iron in my hands creates and protects. Ogun's discipline lives in my body.
sh: The warrior who turns against his own — Ogun in myth killed his own people in a rage; the power that cannot distinguish friend from enemy
ar: The Iron Warrior / The Path Clearer of All the Orishas
cb: Oshun (sweet water, honey, the feminine creative) counterbalances Ogun (iron, the masculine force). The myth of Oshun drawing Ogun from the forest with honey — the iron warrior tamed by love — is the supreme Yoruba myth of the masculine and feminine.

do: The rivers and the forest, medicine, hunting, the androgynous divine physician of the Yoruba
at: Erinle (also Eyinle) is the Yoruba Orisha of the river and the forest — the divine physician who lives at the confluence of land and water. Erinle is specifically androgynous: governing both masculine energy (the hunt and the forest) and feminine energy (the healing waters). Erinle's medicine comes from both realms. Erinle is the physician who hunts for the cure. His/her river flows through Ilobu in Osun State, Nigeria.
of: Fish and game (the gifts of both realms), medicinal herbs gathered where forest meets river, blue and white cloth, water from the Erinle river
an: Fish (the river), deer (the forest), the otter (the creature of both worlds)
co: Blue, white and green (the river, the sky and the forest united)
dp: Monday / Mercury — the healer-hunter's day
cr: Aquamarine (the healing river), green jasper (the forest medicine), blue topaz
bp: Sacral (the river), root (the forest), heart (the healer's compassion)
af: My medicine comes from both the river and the forest. I am the healer who hunts for the cure. My androgynous nature is my divine strength.
sh: The healer who cannot heal themselves — Erinle's medicine governs both life and death; the shadow is the physician whose healing art becomes the means of harm
ar: The Androgynous Physician / The River-Forest Healer
cb: Oya (the storm and transformation) counterbalances Erinle (the still medicine at the river's edge). The storm and the medicine, the chaos and the cure.

do: Agriculture, the farm, fertility of the earth, the honest farmer, the arbiter of disputes
at: Orisa Oko is the Yoruba Orisha of agriculture — the divine farmer who governs the planting and harvest cycle. He is the Orisha of the earth in its cultivated form: the tilled field, the planted crop, the patient labour of the farming year. He is also an arbiter of disputes — particularly women's disputes — because he governs the honest, reliable, patient principle. His staff planted in the earth is his sacred symbol.
of: Yam (his sacred crop — the most important crop in Yoruba culture), palm wine, white offerings, the first yam of the harvest returned to the earth, farming tools blessed in his name
an: Snail (the slow, patient animal of the farm), the tortoise
co: White and red (the sacred and the labour combined)
dp: Saturday / Earth — the farmer's day
cr: Brown jasper, red jasper, earth stones
bp: Hands (the farmer's hands in the soil), root (the planted seed), sacral (the earth's fertility)
af: I plant with patience and I harvest with gratitude. The earth rewards honest labour. Orisa Oko's staff marks my sacred field.
sh: The farmer who exploits the earth — Orisa Oko's shadow is the agricultural greed that takes without returning to the soil
ar: The Sacred Farmer / The Arbiter of Disputes
cb: Shango (the thunder, the fast, the dramatic) counterbalances Orisa Oko (the earth, the slow, the patient). The lightning that breaks the drought counterbalanced by the farmer who works through the entire season.

do: Storm, wind, the marketplace, death, the cemetery, the Egungun, radical transformation
at: Oya in the complete Nigerian Yoruba tradition — she is the Orisha of the Niger River (specifically the rapids where the current is most powerful), of storms, of the marketplace (Ọya means 'she tore' — she tears open what is stale to make way for the new), and of the Egungun (ancestral masquerade). She was the third wife of Shango, the only one who could match his fire with her own. She wears nine different colours simultaneously — nine being her sacred number.
of: Nine purple aubergines (her most sacred offering — nine because it is her number), red wine, copper, cemetery gate ceremonies, nine candles of nine different colours
an: Buffalo (her most sacred animal — she can transform into a buffalo), eagle, the cemetery owl
co: Nine colours simultaneously — burgundy, purple, deep red, all together; never a single colour
dp: Wednesday / Uranus — the radical transformation
cr: Dark amethyst, labradorite, obsidian, dark garnet
bp: Lungs (the wind), solar plexus (the storm's power), root (the cemetery earth), third eye (prophetic sight)
af: I am the wind of necessary change. I tear open what is stale. The nine colours of my power are all sacred. Oya speaks through every storm.
sh: The storm that becomes permanent chaos — Oya's transformation that never settles into a new order, the marketplace that burns rather than renews
ar: The Nine-Coloured Storm / The Complete Oya
cb: Obatala (the absolute white stillness, the peace that follows all storms) counterbalances Oya (the absolute storm, the wind that precedes all stillness). The supreme Yoruba feminine polarity of storm and peace.

do: The Oshun River, sweet water, love, beauty, fertility, gold, the diplomatic Orisha who saved the world
at: Oshun in her full Nigerian expression — the Orisha of the Oshun River in Osun State (Oshogbo, Nigeria, is her sacred city with the most important Oshun grove in the world). She is the most beloved Orisha because she saved humanity: when the male Orishas tried to create the world without her, everything failed. Only when they invited her back did creation succeed. She is the indispensable feminine principle. Her sacred grove in Oshogbo is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
of: Honey (the most essential — always taste before offering), pumpkin, yellow and gold offerings, the Oshun River at Oshogbo, the Osun-Osogbo festival (August)
an: Peacock (beauty), vulture (wisdom and self-sacrifice in her Ibu Kole aspect), bee
co: Gold, yellow, amber — but also yellow-green in some of her manifestations
dp: Saturday / Venus — the day of love and beauty
cr: Amber, citrine, yellow topaz, gold
el: Water (sweet water — rivers, streams, the fresh water of life)
bp: Womb, ovaries, kidneys, sacral plexus, the skin as the organ of beauty
af: I am sweet. I am the river that saved the world. My love is indispensable. My gold flows to where it is needed. Oshun's honey runs through my veins.
sh: The jilted Oshun — when she is disrespected she withdraws her waters and the earth dries up. The river that stops flowing. The love that turns cold.
ar: The Indispensable Love / The River That Saved the World
cb: Ogun (iron, the forge, the masculine force she alone can call from the forest with honey) counterbalances Oshun (sweet water, love). Their mythological reconciliation — Oshun's honey drawing Ogun from his isolation — is the supreme Yoruba story of love's power.

do: The Ogun River, the mother of all Orishas, all waters, motherhood, the womb of all creation
at: Yemoja ('Mother whose children are like fish') in her complete Nigerian Yoruba expression — she is the Orisha of the Ogun River, but she is also the mother of 14 of the other Orishas (including Shango, Ogun, Oya, Oshun, and many others). She is the cosmic mother. Her name is the root of all the diaspora expressions (Yemanjá in Brazil, La Mer in Haiti). She is both the nurturing mother and the raging sea when provoked.
of: Blue and white cloth, duck, fish, watermelon, molasses, white flowers, offerings floated on water
an: Fish (all fish — her children are like fish), duck, the mermaid (her iconographic form)
co: Blue and white (the universal waters), sometimes green (the living water)
dp: Saturday / Moon — the water mother
cr: Aquamarine, moonstone, blue topaz, pearl, white quartz
el: Water (all waters — fresh and salt, the womb water)
bp: Womb (she is the cosmic mother), breasts (the nursing of all), sacral (all bodily fluids), heart (maternal love)
af: I am held by the mother of all. Every river runs to her. She is the womb from which all Orishas were born. I am her child.
sh: The suffocating mother — Yemoja's shadow is the ocean that drowns rather than nurtures, the mother who will not allow her children to grow
ar: The Mother of All Orishas / The Cosmic Womb
cb: Olokun (the unfathomable deep, the dark face of the same water) counterbalances Yemoja (the nurturing surface). The ocean has two faces: Yemoja's loving surface and Olokun's terrifying depth. Both are necessary.

do: Creation, the sculpting of human bodies, purity, white light, the creator Orisha sent by Olodumare
at: Obatala in his complete Nigerian expression — the Orisha chosen by Olodumare to create human bodies. He was sent with a calabash of sand and a five-toed hen to create the earth (though Oduduwa completed the task when Obatala got drunk on palm wine). He sculpts each human body from clay. He governs all who are physically different — people with albinism, with disabilities, the very old — they are under his special protection. He never drinks alcohol.
of: White cloth, white food (no salt, no pepper — white yam, white rice, coconut, shea butter), cool water, silver, the snail (his sacred creature)
an: White snail (the slowest, most patient creature), white dove
co: White — only white, in every shade and texture of white
dp: Sunday / Uranus — the supreme purity
cr: White quartz, howlite, selenite, white moonstone
el: Air / Ether — the highest and purest element
bp: Bones (he sculpts them), the head (ori — the seat of divine consciousness), the entire body in its most elevated state
af: My highest power is absolute clarity. I create from perfect stillness. I am the white light before it divides into colour. Obatala sculpted me with his own hands.
sh: The drunk Obatala — his one failure was getting drunk on palm wine before completing the creation, resulting in imperfect bodies. The shadow of perfect purity is the one moment of lapse that creates permanent imperfection.
ar: The Creator of Bodies / The Pure White Consciousness
cb: Shango (red, fire, thunder, passion) counterbalances Obatala (white, stillness, patience). Also Esu/Elegba (the trickster, the crossroads) counterbalances Obatala (the supreme purity that cannot navigate the crossroads).

do: The crossroads, communication, beginnings and endings, the divine trickster, all paths and all thresholds
at: Eshu Elegba in his complete Nigerian expression — the Orisha who must be honoured first in every ceremony and last at every ceremony's close. He is simultaneously the youngest (a divine child with his toys and candy) and the oldest (the principle of communication that precedes all existence). He is the divine trickster who makes all communication possible — between humans and Orishas, between Orishas and Olodumare. Without Eshu's permission, no ceremony can reach its destination.
of: The first and last libation in every ceremony (the most important obligation in Yoruba practice), palm oil on the crossroads stone, rum and tobacco, candy (he is a divine child), his sacred number is 3 and 21
an: Rooster (the announcer), the dog (the crossroads animal), the spider (the web-maker of all paths)
co: Black and red (his fundamental colours — the crossroads of darkness and life)
dp: Monday — the gate between all days, the beginning and end of the week
cr: Red jasper (the crossroads fire), black tourmaline, obsidian
el: Earth / Ether — the crossroads spans all elements
bp: Feet (the crossroads walker), throat (all communication passes through him), crown (he connects to Olodumare)
af: Eshu opens the way. The first word I speak is his name. Every threshold I cross is blessed by his permission. Thank you, Eshu Elegba.
sh: The crossroads closed — when Eshu is not properly honoured, all ceremonies are blocked, all communication distorted. His shadow is the divine principle of obstruction.
ar: The Universal Gatekeeper / The First and Last
cb: Orunmila / Ifa (the divine wisdom of fate — what Eshu carries to and from the Orishas) counterbalances Eshu (the divine messenger). The message and the messenger. The wisdom and the one who delivers it.

do: Thunder, lightning, justice, royal power, the historical Alaafin of Oyo elevated to the divine
at: Shango in his complete Nigerian Yoruba expression — the fourth Alaafin (king) of the Oyo Empire who became the Orisha of thunder. He is depicted carrying his double-headed axe (oshe) and wearing his ram-horn crown. He is simultaneously the most celebrated and the most feared Orisha — his justice is absolute but his temper is legendary. He had three wives: Oya (who could match his power), Oshun (who charmed him), and Oba (who loved him completely). His sacred city is Oyo.
of: Bata drumming (the most sacred of all Orisha percussion), red and white cloth, apples (in some traditions), lightning water (water collected during a lightning storm), the double-headed axe (oshe Shango)
an: Ram (his most sacred animal — the thunder sounds like a ram's head striking), horse
co: Red and white — always together, never one without the other
dp: Saturday / Jupiter — the thunder king who became the royal divine
cr: Red garnet, meteorite (literally struck by lightning), fire opal, royal gold
el: Fire / Air — the thunderstorm in its entirety
bp: Solar plexus (the royal commanding power), crown (the deified king's authority), throat (the voice of thunder)
af: Kawo Kabiyesi! I am the thunder that announces itself. My power is royal and my justice is absolute. Shango's double axe strikes only what must be struck.
sh: The king who killed his own ministers with lightning — Shango's shadow is the power so great it destroys even those it loves. The thunder that cannot be aimed.
ar: The Deified Thunder King / The Royal Storm
cb: Obatala (the white stillness, the creator who never raises his voice) counterbalances Shango (the red thunder, the king who commands with his voice). The supreme Yoruba masculine polarity: the silent white creator and the thundering red king.

do: The Niger River, storms, the marketplace, the Egungun, the guardian of death's threshold
at: Oya in her specific Nigerian Yoruba context — she is the Orisha of the Niger River (Odo Oya), not just the wind and storm. The Niger River is her body. She is also the Orisha who controls the Egungun (ancestral masquerades) — no Egungun ceremony can be performed without her permission. She was Shango's most powerful wife and the only one who truly matched him. Her nine skirts represent the nine tributaries of the Niger.
of: Nine purple aubergines, red wine, copper pennies, the Egungun masquerade ceremony, cemetery gate offerings
an: Buffalo (can transform into one), eagle, the vulture (the cemetery's bird)
co: Nine colours (her trademark — she refuses to be reduced to one colour)
dp: Wednesday — the marketplace and the storm
cr: Dark amethyst, labradorite, obsidian
el: Air / Fire / Earth — the storm spans all three
bp: Lungs, solar plexus, root (the Niger River's earth)
af: My river flows with nine tributaries. I tear open what is dead to make room for new life. The Egungun speak through my wind.
sh: The storm that destroys the bridge it was meant to clear
ar: The Niger River Storm / The Egungun Mistress
cb: Yemoja (the still nurturing mother, the female Orisha of the complementary water) counterbalances Oya (the raging storm). Two great female Orishas in polarity: stillness and storm, mother and wind.

do: The iroko tree, the ancient forest, the spirit of the most powerful tree in West Africa, time and wisdom
at: Iroko is the Orisha who lives in the iroko tree (Milicia excelsa) — the most sacred and powerful tree in Yoruba culture. The iroko tree is so sacred that it cannot be cut down without elaborate ceremony and compensation to the Orisha within. The iroko spirit is ancient beyond reckoning. Iroko governs the deep time of the forest — the wisdom of centuries, the slow patience of the tallest tree. He is also associated with the trickster aspect: iroko spirits can grant wishes but always with a twist.
of: Offerings placed at the base of the iroko tree, prayers at ancient trees, never harming an iroko without ceremony, white offerings placed in the tree's hollow
an: The iroko tree itself (he IS the tree), the owl (the tree spirit's bird), the lizard (who lives in the bark)
co: Green (the living canopy), brown (the ancient bark), white (the spirit within)
dp: Saturday / Earth — the ancient tree's day
cr: Wood from a sacred tree, green tourmaline, brown jasper, moss agate
el: Earth / Air (the canopy that touches both)
bp: Root (the tree's deep roots), crown (the canopy that touches the sky), heart (the hollow at the tree's centre)
af: I am rooted as deep as the iroko. My wisdom is as old as the oldest tree. Time is my teacher. The forest spirit blesses all who approach with respect.
sh: The wish-granting iroko — Iroko grants wishes but his blessings often come with terrible costs. The trickster wisdom that gives what you asked for rather than what you needed.
ar: The Ancient Tree Spirit / The Wisdom of Deep Time
cb: Eshu (the crossroads, the active, the constantly moving) counterbalances Iroko (the rooted, the ancient, the still). The speed of the crossroads counterbalanced by the stillness of the oldest tree.

do: Wealth found in cowrie shells, the divine lottery, fortune that comes unexpectedly
at: Aje Shaluga is the Yoruba Orisha of unexpected wealth — he is the divine principle of fortune found in the cowrie shell, the lottery won, the windfall that comes without labour. He is distinct from Aje (the general Orisha of wealth) because he specifically governs the unexpected gift of prosperity. He is the spiritual patron of those who find money, inherit unexpectedly, or receive divine financial gifts. He is often depicted as a child picking up cowrie shells from the ground.
of: Cowrie shells offered and planted in the earth, prayers for unexpected abundance, the first windfall of any unexpected income returned as offering, white and gold cloth
an: Snail (the animal that carries its wealth on its back), the squirrel (who finds hidden nuts)
co: White and gold (the cowrie and the prosperity it represents)
dp: Friday / Jupiter — the unexpected abundance
cr: Citrine, pyrite (fool's gold — but also real gold found unexpectedly), cowrie shells, gold
bp: Hands (the lucky hands that find the cowries), root (the earth where cowries are buried), sacral (the creative abundance)
af: Unexpected abundance flows toward me. The divine lottery always has my number. I find the cowrie shells others walk past.
sh: The windfall that creates no roots — the unexpected wealth that is consumed before it can become foundation. The lottery won and spent.
ar: The Divine Windfall / The Lucky Cowrie
cb: Aje (the steady accumulated wealth of diligence) counterbalances Aje Shaluga (the unexpected lucky wealth). The disciplined abundance and the gifted abundance must both be honoured.

do: The ancestors speaking through the masquerade, the foundation beneath all living things, the voice of the collective dead
at: Egungun in his complete expression — the most important Yoruba ancestral institution. The Egungun masquerade IS the ancestor physically present in the community: the dancer wearing the sacred cloth that covers every part of the body (not a costume but a portal) becomes the ancestor speaking, moving, blessing and warning the living. The community's relationship with the Egungun determines its health and fortune. The masquerade must be respected absolutely — touching the Egungun cloth can be fatal.
of: White cloth, cowrie shells, the sacrificial foods of the specific ancestor, money offered to the masquerade, the Egungun ceremony performed annually
an: The sacred cloth IS the Egungun — no specific animal but the human body as the ancestor's vehicle
co: The specific colours of the particular lineage's Egungun — each family's masquerade has its own sacred colours
dp: Saturday — the day of the ancestors
cr: White quartz, cowrie shells (the currency between worlds), the bones of sacred animals
el: Earth / Ether — the ancestors live in both worlds simultaneously
bp: The entire body as the ancestor's vessel, spine (the axis between the living and the dead), crown (the ancestor entering from above)
af: My ancestors are not gone — they come back in the masquerade and they speak directly to me. I honour the Egungun with absolute respect. The collective dead are my foundation.
sh: The Egungun that possesses without ceremony — the ancestral force entering outside of the sacred container, the ancestor that overwhelms rather than communicates
ar: The Living Ancestor / The Sacred Masquerade
cb: The Orishas (the divine cosmic forces beyond the ancestors) counterbalance the Egungun (the personal ancestral forces). The cosmic divine and the personal ancestral in their essential Yoruba polarity.

do: Marriage, sacrifice in love, the Obba River, the wound that becomes wisdom
at: Obba in the full Nigerian Yoruba tradition — the Orisha of the Obba River and Shango's first and legitimate wife. She cut off her ear at Oshun's suggestion, believing it would bind Shango's love forever. When Shango discovered the deception he was horrified and banished her. She became the Orisha of those who sacrifice too much in love — the patron of all who have been manipulated into self-destruction in the name of devotion. Her river flows to meet Oshun's river.
of: Purple and brown cloth, offerings at the confluence of the Obba and Oshun rivers, prayers for healing of love wounds and betrayal, purple candles
an: Cow, turtle (the animal of patient endurance)
co: Purple and brown — the colours of dignified grief
dp: Saturday — the day of her ceremonies
cr: Amethyst (the dignified wound), brown jasper
bp: Heart (the wound), the ear (the ear she sacrificed), root (the earth of the abandoned wife)
af: My love was real. My sacrifice was made from genuine devotion. The deception that wounded me does not make my love less sacred. I am healed.
sh: The eternal mutilation — Obba's shadow is the woman who continues to cut off pieces of herself in relationships that do not deserve her sacrifice
ar: The Sacred Wound of Love / The Sacrificed Wife
cb: Oshun (who gave the malicious advice — the rival, the deceptive feminine) counterbalances Obba (the trusting, loyal feminine who was deceived). The most painful of all Yoruba feminine polarities.

do: The creator of the earth at Ile-Ife, the first ancestor of humanity, the divine chain from heaven
at: Oduduwa in his complete Yoruba expression — he descended from Orun (heaven) on a divine chain, carrying a calabash of sand and a five-toed hen. When he placed the sand on the primordial waters and released the hen, it scratched the sand across the waters creating land. The first land appeared at Ile-Ife, the sacred city. Every Yoruba king traces their divine lineage to Oduduwa. He is simultaneously the supreme creator and the first human ancestor — the point where the divine became human.
of: White cloth, royal ceremonies, the veneration of Ile-Ife as the sacred source, the Edi festival, the tracing of royal lineage as sacred practice
an: The five-toed hen (his creation tool), the python (the sacred snake of Ile-Ife), the chameleon
co: White and purple (the divine and the royal combined)
dp: Sunday — the supreme creator's day
cr: White quartz, amethyst (the royal divine), ivory (the royal material)
bp: Crown (the divine creator descended from heaven), root (the land he created), solar plexus (the royal power of all his descendants)
af: I stand on the sacred ground created at Ile-Ife. The divine chain that connected heaven to earth runs through my lineage. I carry the creator's blessing in my blood.
sh: The creator who withdrew — after creating the earth, Oduduwa disappeared, leaving the world to be governed by the Orishas. The shadow is the creative source that cannot sustain relationship with what it created.
ar: The Creator at Ile-Ife / The Ancestor of All Yoruba Kings
cb: Obatala (the Orisha who was originally sent to create and failed) counterbalances Oduduwa (the one who succeeded). The attempted creator and the successful creator. Their conflict over who truly created the earth is one of the central tensions in Yoruba cosmology.

do: The rising sun, transformation, the sacred scarab, rebirth from the earth
at: Khepri is the dawn aspect of the sun god — the solar force in its rising, transforming moment. He is depicted as a man with a scarab beetle for a head, or as the scarab itself rolling the sun across the sky like a ball of dung. The scarab lays its eggs in dung and the larvae emerge apparently from nothing — making the scarab the supreme symbol of creation from nothing, of transformation, of rebirth. The word 'Khepri' means 'he who comes into being.'
of: Scarab amulets, sunrise ceremonies, offerings made at the exact moment of dawn, prayers for transformation
an: Scarab beetle (he IS the scarab — the dung beetle who rolls the sun)
co: Blue-green (the faience colour of the scarab amulet), gold
dp: Sunday / Sun — specifically the dawn moment
cr: Blue-green faience (the material of all sacred scarabs), lapis lazuli, blue scarab amulets
el: Fire (the rising sun) / Earth (from whose dark soil the scarab emerges)
bp: Solar plexus (the transforming sun), root (the dark earth from which transformation emerges), crown (the sun risen to its full height)
af: I am Khepri — I come into being. Like the scarab I roll the sun from darkness to light. My transformation is already happening.
sh: The sun that cannot rise — Khepri's shadow is the transformation that is blocked, the dawn that does not come, the scarab whose ball is taken
ar: The Sacred Scarab / The Transforming Dawn
cb: Atum (the setting sun — the solar force in its ending moment) counterbalances Khepri (the rising sun — the solar force in its beginning moment). The dawn and the dusk as the complete solar cycle.

do: Air, light, the space between earth and sky, the supporter of the heavens
at: Shu is the god of air and light — one of the first creations of Atum, twin of Tefnut. He stands between the earth (Geb) and the sky (Nut), holding Nut aloft with his arms. He is the divine principle of space itself: the air between all things, the light that fills all space, the breath of life. Without Shu, Nut and Geb would collapse back into an undifferentiated mass and there would be no space for life.
of: Incense (the sacred air), feathers (his headdress symbol), breath itself offered as prayer, sunrise ceremonies when the air is freshest
an: Lion (his fierce aspect), the ostrich (whose feather is his symbol), the lizard
co: White and pale gold (the colour of clear air and light)
dp: Sunday / Air — the divine space-holder
cr: Clear quartz (the transparency of air and light), blue calcite, white calcite
bp: Lungs (the air he is), crown (he holds up the sky), heart (the space between all things where love lives)
af: I am the space that makes life possible. The air I breathe is sacred. I hold up the sky of my own consciousness.
sh: The air that cannot fill — Shu's shadow is the space collapsed, the lungs that cannot breathe, the sky fallen to earth
ar: The Space Between / The Divine Air
cb: Geb (the earth — what Shu stands upon) and Nut (the sky — what Shu holds up) both counterbalance Shu (the space between them). He is defined entirely by the two he separates.

do: Moisture, rain, the dew, the Eye of Ra in its distant manifestation, the lion goddess of moisture
at: Tefnut is the goddess of moisture and rain — twin sister and consort of Shu (air). She is the divine principle of all moisture: dew, rain, the humidity of the air. She is depicted as a lioness or a woman with a lion's head. She is also one of the Eyes of Ra: when Ra angered her, she left Egypt for Nubia (taking all moisture with her) and Egypt dried up entirely. The myth of her return with the cat-god Thoth and Shu appeasing her is one of the most important Egyptian myths.
of: Water offerings, especially morning dew collected before sunrise, the lioness honoured, rain ceremonies
an: Lioness (her primary form), serpent (the cobra she sometimes wears)
co: Blue (moisture), red (the lioness's fire)
dp: Monday / Moon — the moisture and dew
cr: Aquamarine (moisture), moonstone (dew), red jasper (the lioness's fire)
bp: Sacral (the moisture of the body), solar plexus (the lioness's power), crown (her return brings life to Egypt)
af: The moisture of life flows through me. Like Tefnut's return to Egypt, my presence restores what was dry and dead. I carry the rain within me.
sh: The Eye of Ra in exile — Tefnut's shadow is the drought she causes by leaving. The divine feminine whose departure takes all moisture from the world.
ar: The Moisture Goddess / The Distant Eye That Must Return
cb: Shu (her twin and consort — the dry air) counterbalances Tefnut (the wet moisture). Air and water, dry and wet, the twin principles that together make the breath of life possible.

do: The moon, time, healing, the traveller in the night sky, youth
at: Khonsu is the moon god of Thebes — the son of Amun and Mut, the divine youth who travels across the night sky. He is associated with healing (particularly the healing of those possessed by malevolent spirits), with the measurement of time (the lunar calendar), and with youth and fertility. His name means 'traveller' — he is the moon in its aspect as wanderer. He is depicted as a young man with the side-lock of youth and a moon disc and crescent on his head.
of: Lunar ceremonies, offerings at the full and new moon, prayers for healing of possession and mental illness, white and silver offerings
an: Ibis (like Thoth — both moon gods), the baboon, the falcon (in his warrior aspect as Khonsu Neferhotep)
co: White (the moon's colour), silver, the pale gold of moonlight
dp: Monday / Moon — the moon is his body
cr: Moonstone, white selenite, silver, white calcite
bp: Crown (the moon disc on his head), third eye (the moon's reflective sight), sacral (the lunar regulation of fertility)
af: The moon travels through my night sky healing what is troubled. The traveller moon brings time and healing in equal measure. I am renewed with every lunar cycle.
sh: The moon possessed — Khonsu's shadow is the moon in its aspect of causing madness ('lunacy'), the healing deity whose power tips into disorder
ar: The Moon Healer / The Divine Youth Traveller
cb: Ra (the sun — Khonsu's cosmic complement) counterbalances Khonsu (the moon). Sun and moon, day and night, the solar father and the lunar son in their Theban divine family.

do: The great mother, the vulture goddess, the queen of all the gods, the sky as mother
at: Mut ('mother') is the great mother goddess of Thebes — the consort of Amun and mother of Khonsu in the Theban triad. She wears the double crown of Upper and Lower Egypt, and a vulture headdress. She is the sky as nurturing mother: the vulture that spreads its wings over the world. In her fierce aspect she takes the form of the lioness (like Sekhmet). She is the most complete expression of divine motherhood in the Egyptian pantheon.
of: Vulture feathers, the double crown, offerings for motherhood and queenship, the Isheru sacred lake at her Karnak temple, cooling water
an: Vulture (her most sacred animal — the vulture was believed to be exclusively female in ancient Egypt, making it the supreme symbol of motherhood), lioness, cat
co: White and gold (the queen's colours), blue (the sky she governs)
dp: Monday / Moon — the mother's day
cr: Lapis lazuli (the queen's stone), moonstone, vulture obsidian (dark like the vulture's wings)
el: Air / Earth (the sky mother who also receives the dead)
bp: Crown (the queen of the gods), heart (the divine mother), womb (the sacred maternal principle), arms (the vulture's wings spread over all)
af: The great mother spreads her wings over me. I am protected by the vulture's sacred shadow. Mut's queenship is the queenship of the entire sky.
sh: The fierce mother — Mut in her lioness aspect becomes Sekhmet. The protective mother who, when provoked, becomes the most destructive force in Egypt.
ar: The Great Vulture Mother / The Queen of the Gods
cb: Amun (the hidden sky father — her divine husband) counterbalances Mut (the manifest sky mother — the visible principle). The hidden masculine and the visible feminine in their Theban divine marriage.

do: Upper Egypt, the vulture goddess, the protectress of the pharaoh, the white crown, motherhood
at: Nekhbet is the vulture goddess of Upper Egypt — one of the Two Ladies who protect the pharaoh (the other being Wadjet, the cobra of Lower Egypt). She wears the white crown of Upper Egypt and spreads her wings over the pharaoh in protection. She is the divine midwife who assists at royal births. She is one of the oldest Egyptian goddesses — her cult at el-Kab predates the unification of Egypt.
of: White feathers (her colour), the white crown honoured, offerings at birth, the protection of new beginnings, the vulture as sacred bird never harmed
an: Vulture (she IS the vulture — the white vulture of Upper Egypt), the serpent (in association with Wadjet)
co: White (the white crown of Upper Egypt, the white of the vulture)
dp: Sunday — the protectress of the crowned
cr: White quartz, white calcite, white alabaster (the Egyptian sacred stone of Upper Egypt)
bp: Crown (the white crown she wears and bestows), root (the ancient sanctuary at el-Kab), the entire body under her protective wings
af: The white vulture spreads her wings above me. I am under the protection of the ancient goddess of Upper Egypt. My beginnings are guarded.
sh: The protection that smothers — the divine guardian whose wings are too heavy, too close, leaving no room for the pharaoh to breathe
ar: The Vulture Protectress / The White Crown Goddess
cb: Wadjet (the cobra goddess of Lower Egypt — the Two Ladies are always paired) counterbalances Nekhbet (the vulture goddess of Upper Egypt). The vulture and the cobra, Upper and Lower Egypt, the complete protective feminine of the unified kingdom.

do: Childbirth, fertility, the protection of pregnant women, the hippopotamus goddess of motherhood
at: Taweret ('the Great One') is the ancient Egyptian hippopotamus goddess of childbirth and fertility — one of the most beloved deities in everyday Egyptian life. She is depicted as a pregnant hippopotamus standing upright, with the back of a crocodile and the legs of a lion. Despite her fearsome appearance, she is a protective deity: her hippo strength, crocodile ferocity and lion power all combine to protect mothers and infants from the dangers of childbirth.
of: Hippopotamus imagery, offerings at birth (small Taweret figurines were placed in birthing rooms), lotus flowers, prayers for safe childbirth and healthy infants
an: Hippopotamus (her primary form), crocodile (her back), lion (her legs)
co: Blue (the colour of protection and sky), gold, the grey-blue of the hippopotamus
dp: Monday / Moon — the mother of childbirth
cr: Blue faience (the Egyptian protective material), turquoise, blue calcite, moonstone
el: Water (the hippopotamus lives in the Nile) / Earth
bp: Womb (the pregnant hippopotamus body), sacral (the birth canal), root (the earth that receives the newborn), heart (the fierce protective love)
af: The great hippopotamus mother stands at my birth with her three-fold power. Taweret's ferocious gentleness protects all new life. I am fiercely gentle with everything that is newly born.
sh: The hippopotamus that destroys — in Egyptian mythology the evil hippopotamus is Set's form; Taweret's shadow is the birth that goes wrong, the fierce protectress whose power cannot ultimately prevent all loss
ar: The Fierce Birth Protectress / The Pregnant Hippopotamus
cb: Bes (the dwarf god who also protects childbirth and the home) counterbalances Taweret (the hippopotamus goddess of the same domain). Together they form the complete household protective pair of ancient Egypt.

do: Scorpions, venomous creatures, the healing of poison, magic, the protection of the dead
at: Selkis (also Serqet) is the scorpion goddess — the divine force that governs all venomous creatures and the healing of their stings. She is one of the four protective goddesses of the canopic jars (with Isis, Nephthys, and Neith). She is depicted as a woman with a scorpion on her head. She governs the pain that leads to transformation — the scorpion's sting is the most concentrated form of the solar plexus's burning that forces change.
of: Scorpion imagery, prayers for healing from venomous bites, amulets depicting her scorpion headdress, offerings at healing shrines
an: Scorpion (she is the scorpion — her name likely means 'she who causes the throat to breathe,' an antidote to the scorpion's sting affecting the throat)
co: Gold, the yellow-black of the scorpion
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the scorpion's fierce medicine
