The Kongo cosmogram (the four-moment cycle) is governed by Simbi forces at the water line between the living and the dead.
of: Spring water, river water, white chalk (mpemba — the white clay of ancestral power), tobacco, rum, white cloth, crossroads offerings
an: Snake (their most common manifestation — the shining water snake), the lizard, the crab, all fresh water creatures
co: White (Simbi Mpemba/the dead), red (Simbi Lendo/the force), green (Simbi Masa/the water and life)
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the crossroads knowledge, the magical power of the written and spoken word
cr: Clear quartz, white calcite, green fluorite, aquamarine, serpentine (named for their snake nature)
el: Water — specifically fresh water springs and rivers, and the rain
bp: The nervous system (lightning moving through water — the electrical body), the cerebrospinal fluid (the sacred inner spring), the lymph (the internal fresh water)
af: I live at the crossing point. Between the living and the dead. Between the fresh water and the salt. This is not a dangerous place — it is the most alive place in the universe.
sh: The Simbi who does not return — those who enter the water world of the Simbi sometimes do not come back to ordinary life. The shadow is the seduction of the spirit world that makes the human world pale in comparison.
ar: The Crossroads Water Intelligence / The Kongo Cosmogram Guardian
cb: The Ancestors (the dead, who the Simbi mediate access to) counterbalance the Simbi (the living nature spirits who bridge the worlds). Also Nzambi Mpungu (the highest creator) counterbalances the Simbi (the earthly and watery manifestations of power).

do: The protective dwarf god — guardian of childbirth and the home, protector of women in labour, fighter of serpents and night demons, the most beloved household deity of ancient Egypt
at: Bes is unique in the Egyptian pantheon: he is depicted full-face (almost all Egyptian deities are shown in profile), he is a dwarf with leonine features, a feathered crown, protruding tongue, and a fierce yet joyful expression. He is both terrifying and loving — exactly what is needed to drive away the demons of the night from sleeping infants and labouring mothers. Bes predates the formal Egyptian pantheon — he likely entered Egypt from Nubia or Sub-Saharan Africa, making him one of the oldest documented African nature spirits to enter a major formal religious tradition. He danced, sang, played musical instruments and made noise to both drive away evil and celebrate new life. Bes images were placed in birth chambers, sleeping areas, and tattooed on the bodies of Egyptian dancers and midwives. He was so beloved that his cult survived into the Christian era, with his temples still active into the 4th and 5th centuries CE. He was worshipped across the Mediterranean and Near East — one of ancient Egypt's most internationally distributed deities.
of: Music and dancing (he adores both), mirrors, rattles and musical instruments, red cloth, the protection of new mothers, offerings at birth, protective amulets worn on the thigh (his favourite placement)
an: Lion (his fierce protective aspect — he wears its skin), the serpent (which he constantly battles to protect the home)
co: Red, gold, the warm colours of the hearth
dp: Saturday / Mars — the protective warrior in his most domestic and loving form
cr: Red jasper, carnelian, red coral, bloodstone
el: Earth (the home, the threshold, the most intimate sacred space)
bp: The uterus (he protects birth and delivery), the inner ear (he fights demons with sound), the sexual organs (he governs sexuality and fertility)
af: I am not beautiful in the way the world expects beauty. I am beautiful in the way that protects. My ugliness is my power — it frightens what needs frightening.
sh: The guardian who cannot be everywhere — Bes's shadow is the birth that goes wrong despite all protection, the darkness that enters through the gap between the guardian's outstretched arms
ar: The Beloved Protector / The Guardian of New Life
cb: Apep/Apophis (the great chaos serpent that Bes fights nightly to protect the sleeping) counterbalances Bes (the joyful warrior of domestic safety). Also Sekhmet (the destroyer goddess) counterbalances Bes (the protective joy — the same leonine nature, entirely different application).

tr: Pan-African / West, Central & South Africa / Caribbean / Americas
do: The great water spirit — beauty, wealth, healing, dangerous desire, the mysterious depths of the water world, the spirit who takes the chosen beneath the surface and returns them transformed
at: Mami Wata is one of the most widely venerated spirit beings in the world — worshipped from West Africa to South Africa to the Caribbean to Brazil to North America. She is typically depicted as a beautiful woman with a fish or serpent lower body, holding a large python (or two snakes coiled around her arms), and sometimes a mirror. She may be human above and fish below, or wholly human but surrounded by water and serpents. She governs beauty, wealth, sexuality, healing and dangerous desire. Those she chooses are 'married' to her — taken beneath the water during a trance or near-drowning experience and returned transformed, sometimes with gifts of healing, wealth or prophetic sight. Her 'husbands' must observe taboos (no sex with earthly partners, certain dietary restrictions). She can cause illness and misfortune as readily as she grants blessings. Her mirror reflects the truth of what you are, not what you wish to be. Her origin predates the Transatlantic Slave Trade — ancient images of snake-holding water deities from the ancient Near East (possibly 3rd millennium BCE) may be her most distant ancestors. The trade routes carried her cult across the entire African continent and then across the Atlantic.
of: Mirrors (she is never without her mirror), fine combs and hairbrushes, perfume and cologne, champagne, candy and sweets, gold and silver jewellery, fish, clear water in white bowls, red and white cloth, images of beautiful women
an: Python (her constant companion and symbol of wisdom and danger), fish (all fish belong to her domain), the mermaid (her most recognised form)
co: Red and white (her primary devotional colours), gold, silver, any colour associated with water and beauty
dp: Friday / Venus — the water goddess of beauty, love, wealth and dangerous desire
cr: Mirror-clear crystals, selenite, aquamarine, pearl, moonstone, clear quartz
el: Water — rivers, lakes, the ocean, all bodies of water
bp: The skin (her beauty), the eyes (her mirror), the sexual organs (her domain of desire), the womb (healing and dangerous gifts)
af: She who holds the mirror holds the truth. Not my preferred truth, not my comfortable truth — the truth that the water sees when I come to its edge.
sh: The obsession she induces — the marriage to Mami Wata makes ordinary human life feel insufficient. The shadow is the spiritual calling that isolates rather than connects.
ar: The Mirror Goddess / The Transformative Water Mother
cb: The Earth (the dry land, ordinary human relationship, domestic life) counterbalances Mami Wata (the water world, impossible beauty, the spirit marriage). Also Olokun (the deep unchanging abyss) counterbalances Mami Wata (the active surface seductress of water).

do: The good people of the sand — the small benevolent nature spirits of the Senegalese coast and the Petite Côte, dancers and feasters beneath the baobab trees and in the earth
at: The Yumboe (also called Bakhna Rakhna, meaning 'the Good People' — a direct parallel to the Scottish fairy term 'Good Neighbours') are the nature spirits of the Wolof and Serer peoples of Senegal. They are described as small, white-coloured beings about two feet tall who live in underground caverns beneath the baobab trees and in the earth of the Petite Côte. They feast and dance at night — their festivals are heard but not seen by ordinary people. Those who stumble upon their midnight feasts are welcomed if respectful and harmed if intrusive. They share their food with the poor and the respectful. Like the Kontomblé, they represent the universal 'little people' archetype in West African form. The parallel naming — 'Good People' / 'Good Neighbours' — across Senegal and Scotland, with no cultural contact between them, is one of the most striking examples of convergent mythological evolution in the world.
of: Food left outside at night (they share it reciprocally), acknowledgement before entering areas near baobab trees, gratitude for unexpected gifts or good fortune
an: The baobab tree itself (their home), night birds
co: White (their skin colour in Wolof descriptions), the golden brown of the Senegalese sand
dp: Monday / Moon — the night feasters, the beings of the liminal darkness
cr: White selenite, moonstone, fossilised sand, baobab seeds as offerings
el: Earth (the underground caverns beneath the baobab roots)
bp: The stomach (they are feasters and they share their feasts), the feet (they dance all night), the inner ear (their music is heard but their forms are not seen)
af: The Good People have been feasting in this land since before there were names for anything. I walk over their table every day without knowing it. I honour what I cannot see.
sh: The food that is taken without offering reciprocity — the harvest festival that does not leave a portion for the Bakhna Rakhna. The community that forgets the invisible ones living beneath its feet.
ar: The Good People / The Underground Feasters
cb: The visible human community (the village above ground) counterbalances the Yumboe (the invisible community below ground). Also the spirits of the ancestors (the recent dead) counterbalance the Yumboe (the much older, non-human beings who predate the ancestors).

do: The mother of the waters of the Surinamese and Guyanese rivers — the African-descended mermaid spirit of the Amazon watershed, protector and sovereign of the inland waterways of the Guiana Shield
at: Watramama (literally 'water mother' in Surinamese Creole) is the mermaid spirit venerated among the Maroon peoples of Suriname and Guyana — descendants of escaped enslaved Africans who built free communities in the interior rainforest. She governs the rivers that are the lifeblood of Maroon existence: the Suriname River, the Marowijne, the Cottica. She combines the West African Mami Wata tradition with the Amazonian water spirit traditions of indigenous peoples, creating a uniquely syncretic being. She appears as a beautiful woman in the river, sometimes with a fish tail. Her relationship with the Maroon community is one of deep mutual responsibility: she protects those who respect the river and withdraws her protection from those who pollute or disrespect it. She is related to the Afro-Trinidadian La Diablesse and the Haitian La Sirène, all expressions of the same African water mother in different Caribbean and Amazonian contexts.
of: Flowers floated on the river surface, songs sung at the water's edge, libations poured into the river before crossing, white cloth offered on the bank, fish returned to the river after the fisherman's daily catch
an: Anaconda (the great water serpent of the Amazon), the river dolphin (boto), all freshwater fish of the rivers
co: White and green (the river and its forest banks), silver
dp: Monday / Moon — the tidal mother in her inland river form
cr: River-polished stones, aquamarine, green aventurine, moonstone
el: Water — the Surinamese and Guyanese river systems
bp: The womb (she governs birth and the continuation of the Maroon people), the ears (she is heard in the river's voice), the feet (those who cross her river respectfully)
af: The river carries everything that was ever thrown into it. It also carries the prayers. My ancestor stood at this river and asked for safe passage. The water mother heard.
sh: The river crossing that is not completed — Watramama's shadow is the flood, the capsizing, the body that does not come home from the river. The boundary between living and drowned.
ar: The Maroon Water Mother / The African River Spirit of the Americas
cb: The forest (the Maroon people's land domain) counterbalances Watramama (the river domain). Also the colonial plantation (the land of bondage from which the Maroons escaped across rivers) counterbalances Watramama (the river spirit who received the escaped, who swam toward freedom).

do: The mermaid Lwa of Haiti — the mistress of the sea, beauty, wealth, music, trance, romance and the possibility of death, the song that calls the sailor and the fisherman
at: Lasirèn (from French 'la sirène' — the mermaid) is one of the most beloved and powerful Lwa in Haitian Vodou. She is the mermaid queen — the mistress of the sea in all its aspects: the fortune of fishermen, the beauty of the ocean, the wealth hidden in the depths, the trance of deep water. She rules alongside her husband Agwé (the sea god) and alongside La Balènn (the whale) as the trinity of Haitian ocean spirits. She is depicted as a beautiful mermaid with flowing hair, carrying a mirror and a trumpet shell. In possession ceremonies, those she mounts behave as if swimming, diving and breathing underwater. She may take her devotees beneath the ocean for transformative periods — they are 'below' (in trance) — and return them as healers, musicians or diviners. She governs music at a cosmic level: the singing of the sea itself. Her colours are blue, white and silver — the ocean at noon. She is syncretised with the Virgin Mary (Notre Dame de Czestochowa) in Haitian Catholic-Vodou syncretism.
of: Watermelon, white rum, champagne, blue and white cloth, mirrors, combs, perfume, seafood, blue candles, small boats with offerings sent to the sea, the sound of the conch trumpet
an: The mermaid (her own form), fish, the whale (La Balènn, her companion), the dolphin
co: Blue, white and silver — the noon ocean light
dp: Friday / Venus — the water goddess of love, beauty and dangerous desire in her Vodou form
cr: Blue lace agate, aquamarine, moonstone, pearl, white selenite
el: Water — the Caribbean Sea and all salt water
bp: The lungs (the breath held underwater in her possession), the ears (the singing of the sea she governs), the womb (the ocean as the great mother's body)
af: Lasirèn, Lasirèn, Lasirèn. She calls beneath the waves. What she offers cannot be bought with money. What she takes cannot be demanded back.
sh: The beauty that lures to death — La Sirène's shadow is the romance that ends in drowning, the wealth that comes from the sea and returns to it, the song you cannot stop following
ar: The Mermaid Queen / The Song of the Deep
cb: Baron Samedi (the lord of death on land) counterbalances La Sirène (the death-possibility at sea). Also Erzulie Freda (the goddess of love on land, beauty as luxury) counterbalances La Sirène (love and beauty as oceanic depth and danger).

do: The mother of the water — guardian of rivers, streams and forest pools of Trinidad, half-woman half-anaconda, the spirit who punishes those who harm the natural world
at: Mama Dlo (from the French patois 'Maman de l'eau' — mother of the water) is one of the most significant supernatural beings in Trinidadian folklore. She is depicted as a beautiful woman with the lower body of a massive anaconda — not a fish but a serpent — who rules the rivers and forest pools of Trinidad and Tobago. She is both a protector and an avenger of nature. Those who pollute rivers, waste water, harm fish unnecessarily or disrespect the forest may be claimed by her — dragged into the river and killed, or kept as her lover in the water world. She is sometimes said to be the lover of Papa Bois (the forest father) — together forming the complete nature guardian pair of Trinidad. Unlike many water spirits, Mama Dlo's anaconda body specifically roots her in the indigenous Amazonian water tradition of Trinidad's pre-colonial past, surviving through Afro-Trinidadian folk religion.
of: Offerings placed at river banks, water poured before crossing any river, silence and respect near forest pools, never taking more fish than needed
an: Anaconda (her lower body), all fish of Trinidadian rivers, the heron (the river-watching bird)
co: Green (the river and forest), brown (the earth and serpent), white (the water's reflection)
dp: Monday / Moon — the tidal, serpentine, feminine water power
cr: Green serpentine, malachite, river-polished stones, obsidian
el: Water (rivers and forest pools) and Earth (the anaconda's earth wisdom)
bp: The spine (the serpentine movement of the snake body), the womb (the river's generative power), the skin (the serpent's sensitive awareness of the environment)
af: The river knows everything that has been thrown into it. The forest knows everything that has been cut. I am accountable to what I cannot see watching me.
sh: The man who meets Mama Dlo alone in the forest — if he is disrespectful or has harmed the natural world, he will not return. The shadow is the nature revenge that human arrogance invites.
ar: The Anaconda Water Mother / The Forest River Guardian
cb: Papa Bois (the forest father, her lover and counterpart) counterbalances Mama Dlo (the water mother). Forest and river as the two domains of Trinidadian nature guardianship. Also human industry (oil, pollution) counterbalances Mama Dlo (the natural water world she defends).

do: The pan-Caribbean water mother spirits — the African-descended mermaid tradition carried across the Caribbean by enslaved West Africans, each with her own island name and nature
at: Across the Caribbean, the West African Mami Wata tradition spawned distinct regional water spirits in each island context. Mamadjo (Grenada) is the Grenadian mermaid spirit who appears at the confluence of rivers and the sea. Lamanté (Martinique) is the spirit of the sea channels between islands — her name echoes both the French 'lamantin' (manatee) and the African water mother. These island water spirits all share the same essential nature: beautiful women of the water who govern the abundance of the sea, the fortune of fishermen, and the transformative experience of near-drowning and return. They represent the extraordinary resilience of African spiritual tradition — carried across the Middle Passage in human memory, preserved in new island contexts, and continuing to offer the same healing relationship with the water world in their new homes. Each is a local expression of the eternal water mother.
of: White flowers in the sea, coconut oil on the water's surface, rum poured into the sea, prayers before ocean fishing, respect for the tides
an: Manatee (the original 'mermaid' sighted by sailors — her name in Martinique), fish, the frigate bird (which never lands on water but always skims it)
co: White, blue, turquoise — the colours of the Caribbean sea
dp: Friday / Venus and Monday / Moon — the dual planetary governance of all Caribbean water mothers
cr: Turquoise (the Caribbean sea stone), aquamarine, white coral, pearl
el: Water — the salt water of the Caribbean archipelago
bp: The lungs (the breath between worlds), the eyes (the vision of the sea horizon), the womb (the oceanic birth origin)
af: In every island, in every name, in every colour of the Caribbean water — the same mother is calling. She crossed the ocean in the memory of her children and she arrived.
sh: The forgetting — the water spirit who is no longer called by name, whose offerings are no longer made, who watches her rivers filled with plastic. The shadow is the neglect of the water relationship.
ar: The Diaspora Water Mothers / The African Ocean in Island Form
cb: The colonial God (who tried to replace the water mothers with the Virgin Mary — and was only partially successful, as the water mothers survived in syncretised form) counterbalances the island water spirits. Also the hurricane (the violence of the Caribbean sea) counterbalances the gentle water mother.

do: The Lwa of love, beauty, luxury, art, jewellery, romance, and the divine feminine in her most adorned and heartbroken form — the goddess who weeps because no human love is ever sufficient
at: Erzulie Freda (full name Erzulie Freda Dahomey) is the most complex and beloved Lwa of love in Haitian Vodou. She is the spirit of love, beauty, jewellery, luxury, art, sensuality, femininity, flowers and romance. When she possesses her devotees (male or female) she paints her face, preens, flirts, dances with exquisite grace and demands the finest offerings. She has three husbands: Ogou (the iron warrior), Damballa (the serpent), and Agwé (the sea). At the end of every ceremony where she appears, she weeps — because no human love, no matter how devoted, no matter how rich the offerings, is ever complete enough to match her vision of what love should be. This weeping is not a flaw — it is the very nature of the highest love: it always wants more, it always sees further than what is currently possible. She is syncretised with the Virgin Mary (Mater Dolorosa — the Mother of Sorrows) whose tears mirror her own.
of: Gold jewellery, pink and gold cloth, perfume (her absolute requirement), champagne and sweet wine, pink roses and white flowers, mirrors and beauty items, fine food, chocolate, the finest of everything
an: The dove (love's bird), the peacock (beauty and display)
co: Pink and gold — the colours of love, luxury and beauty in their highest expressions
dp: Friday / Venus — the pure Venus frequency in her most complete and heartbroken form
cr: Rose quartz, pink tourmaline, rhodonite, gold, morganite
el: Water / Air — the lightness of love and the depth of tears
bp: The heart (the love she governs and the tears she weeps), the skin (her beauty and sensuality), the throat (the songs she sings before she weeps)
af: Erzulie Freda, je vous aime. The love I carry is too large for this world. This is not my failure. This is my nature. I weep because I can feel how much more love is possible.
sh: The heartbreak that cannot be consoled — Erzulie's tears at the end of every ceremony represent the permanent incompleteness of love within human experience. The shadow is the love that becomes bitterness when it cannot achieve its ideal.
ar: The Divine Heartbreak / The Lwa of Perfect Love and Its Impossible Fulfilment
cb: Erzulie Dantor (her dark sister — the fierce, scarred, independent mother who needs no man) directly counterbalances Erzulie Freda (the romantic, adorned, multiply-husbanded love goddess). They are the two faces of the sacred feminine: adornment and battle scars, romance and fierce independence.

do: The mother of the waters of the Amazon — the irresistible singing mermaid of the rivers and lagoons, the beautiful indigenous water spirit whose voice calls men to a death they welcome
at: Iara (also called Mãe d'Água — mother of the water) is the most important water spirit in Brazilian indigenous tradition, rooted in the Tupi-Guarani cosmology of the Amazon. She is described as a woman of extraordinary beauty with long black hair and green eyes who sings with a voice so irresistibly beautiful that fishermen and travelers are drawn into the water and lost forever. Those who hear her and can resist become obsessed with returning, going mad with longing. Her name means 'water woman' or 'lady of the waters' in Tupi. She likely absorbed elements of the European mermaid through colonial contact, but her core is indigenous Amazonian — rooted in the Tupi understanding of water as a domain of irresistible spiritual power. She is the Amazonian expression of what Mami Wata represents in Africa: the beautiful dangerous woman of the waters who transforms those she chooses, offering a crossing into another world.
of: Tobacco smoke offered at the river's edge, flowers floated on the water, the sound of music played before fishing, respectful silence before crossing a river at night
an: The anaconda (her companion and the master of the Amazon's rivers), fish, the caiman
co: Deep green (the Amazon's colour), dark blue (the deep river), the brown of river silt
dp: Monday / Moon — the night singer, the river's feminine pull
cr: Green fluorite, aquamarine, malachite, amazonite (named for her river)
el: Water — the Amazon River and its tributaries
bp: The ears (those who hear her song are never the same), the eyes (her beauty is the first thing that captures), the throat (the voice that carries across the water)
af: The Amazon is 6,400 kilometres long. In all that length, there is not one place where the water does not carry a consciousness. I do not enter this river without greeting what lives in it.
sh: The man who follows the song to his death — Iara's shadow is the beauty that calls beyond the boundary of life, the desire that dissolves all caution
ar: The Amazon Water Singer / The Mermaid of the Great River
cb: Caipora (the fierce protector of the forest) counterbalances Iara (the beautiful seducer of the river) — the forest that chases you away vs the river that draws you in. Also the boto/Encantado (the male dolphin shapeshifter) counterbalances Iara (the female water singer) — the Amazon's twin-gender water spirit tradition.

do: The shapeshifting pink river dolphin of the Amazon — the magnetic seducer who comes ashore at festivals as an elegantly dressed stranger, the liminality of the sacred animal who lives between the human and the water world
at: The Boto Encantado is the Amazon River dolphin (Inia geoffrensis — the boto, the largest freshwater dolphin on earth, distinctively pink) in its sacred mythological form. The dolphin is believed to transform at night, especially during full moon festivals, into a strikingly handsome young man dressed in white, his blowhole concealed beneath a hat that he refuses to remove. He dances with perfect skill, selects the most beautiful woman, seduces her, and disappears before dawn — returning to the river as a dolphin. Children with no known father are called 'children of the boto' across the Amazon. Beyond the seduction myth, the boto is sacred: killing a river dolphin brings the worst misfortune, and the dolphins are believed to guide drowning fishermen to the riverbank. They work cooperatively with fishermen to drive fish into nets. The Encante — the paradisiacal underwater world of the boto people — is described as a mirror of human society but perfect: full of music, beauty, technology and joy, without pain or death. Those taken to the Encante never want to return.
of: Never harming a river dolphin (the greatest taboo), offering the first fish of any catch back to the river, not pointing at a boto in the river (this brings bad luck), music played at riverside festivals acknowledging the boto's presence
an: The Amazon river dolphin itself — the boto is simultaneously the animal and the spirit, not a spirit residing in the animal but the animal as spirit
co: Pink (the boto's actual skin colour), white (the disguise suit), blue (the deep river)
dp: Monday / Moon and Friday / Venus — the night seducer, the lover who comes with the moon and the festival
cr: Pink rose quartz (the boto's colour), moonstone (the full moon under which he transforms), river pearl
el: Water — the Amazon and its tributaries specifically
bp: The blowhole (the biological feature that cannot be fully concealed — the body's truth that breaks through the human disguise), the hips (the dancer's seduction), the reproductive organs (the boto's primary mythological function)
af: The Encante is real. There is an underwater world that mirrors this one but without suffering. The boto knows the way there. I ask only to be shown the door — not to stay forever.
sh: The seduction that results in a child with no father — the boto's shadow is the responsibility that does not come with the desire, the transformation that takes but does not sustain
ar: The Sacred River Dolphin / The Shapeshifter of the Festival Night
cb: Iara (the female water spirit of the Amazon) counterbalances the Boto (the male water spirit) — together they form the complete twin-gender water spirit tradition of the Amazon. Also the real boto (the endangered species, now vulnerable to extinction from river pollution and dam-building) counterbalances the mythological Boto Encantado — the physical reality and the spiritual reality of the same being.

do: The fierce indigenous forest guardian of Brazil — the protector of all animals and the forest ecosystem, the being who confuses hunters with false trails and howls in the night to defend nature
at: Caipora (from Tupi 'Caá-pora' — inhabitant of the woods) is one of the most important nature guardian spirits in Brazilian indigenous tradition. They are a small, wild person — sometimes male, sometimes female, sometimes child-like — who lives in the depths of the Brazilian rainforest and savannah. Their primary mission is the protection of all animals: they will confuse hunters with false trails, create phantom sounds to disorient those who take too much from the forest, and physically chase those who threaten the ecosystem. They have the power to control animals and to resurrect injured wildlife. They appear in the forest if someone whistles — the whistle is their call. They ride a peccary (wild pig) through the forest at tremendous speed. Unlike the Encantado's seduction, Caipora does not try to charm — they confront, confuse and chase. They are one of the earliest documented forest guardian spirits in the Americas, first mentioned in Jesuit missionary accounts of the 16th century, and they have survived 500 years of colonialism and evangelisation.
of: Never whistling in the forest without intent to call (they answer), offering tobacco to the forest before hunting, taking only what is needed, never leaving wounded animals, leaving portions of the hunt at forest crossroads
an: Peccary/wild pig (their mount), jaguar (they command the jaguar to protect the forest), all forest animals (they are the universal protector of all of them)
co: Dark forest green, deep red-brown (the colour of the forest floor), black
dp: Tuesday / Mars — the fierce protector warrior of the natural world
cr: Deep green jade, black tourmaline (the forest's protection), red jasper (the hunter's accountability)
el: Earth — the deep forest, the roots, the floor of the Amazon
bp: The feet (reversed in some versions — Curupira's feet face backwards to deceive, Caipora runs forwards but faster than any human can follow), the nose (they smell human presence in the forest), the voice (the howl that terrifies hunters)
af: The forest hears every footstep. If I walk through it taking everything and offering nothing, I am not the apex of the food chain — I am the first thing that will be confused, exhausted and lost.
sh: The hunter who ignores the warnings — the forest that the Caipora no longer bothers to protect because the damage is too great. The silence that falls when even the guardian has given up.
ar: The Fierce Forest Guardian / The Protector of All Animals
cb: The Amazon coloniser / the deforester (who represents the most profound violation of what Caipora protects) directly counterbalances Caipora. Also Iara (the water domain) counterbalances Caipora (the forest domain) — the two great nature guardians of the Amazon between them cover the entire ecosystem.

do: The flame-haired backward-footed protector of the forest — the trickster guardian whose reversed feet leave tracks pointing the wrong direction, luring poachers into the forest's depths never to return
at: Curupira is one of the most iconic figures in Brazilian indigenous mythology, first documented by the Jesuit priest José de Anchieta in 1560 — making him one of the earliest recorded indigenous spirits in the Americas. He is a small, boyish figure with wild flame-red hair and feet that face backwards. His backward feet leave tracks pointing away from his actual direction of travel, perfectly confusing any hunter who follows his trail. He is the arch-protector of the Brazilian forest: if you hunt a pregnant or nursing animal, if you take more than you need, if you waste what you kill, Curupira will find you. He leads you deeper and deeper into the forest until you are completely lost and exhausted. He makes sounds like enormous animals to frighten. He is mischievous rather than malevolent — he does not kill unless pushed, but he will exhaust and confuse without mercy. The Curupira myth is one of the oldest documented forms of conservation ethics in the world: do not take what the forest cannot afford to give.
of: Tobacco (he smokes a pipe in many descriptions), offerings left at forest edges before hunting, the first fruits of the forest harvest, never taking pregnant or nursing animals
an: All forest animals (he is their protector), especially the deer (the most commonly hunted), his feet-reversed tracks are his signature
co: Flame red (his hair colour — unusual and alarming in the forest context), forest green, the orange of fire
dp: Wednesday / Mercury — the trickster's intelligence, the forest's cunning
cr: Carnelian (his fire-red hair), green aventurine (the forest protection), smoky quartz (the confusion he creates)
el: Earth / Fire — the forest floor and the fire of his hair
bp: The feet (reversed — the directional confusion that is his primary weapon), the nose (he can smell human presence from great distances), the ears (attuned to every sound of the forest)
af: My footprints do not lead where you expect. The forest uses me to confuse the part of you that thinks it knows where it is going. Being lost is the forest's invitation to pay attention.
sh: The Curupira who encounters someone who has truly given up trying to take more than they need — he has no business with them. His shadow is the total absence of the conservation ethic: the forest stripped bare, where even Curupira has nothing left to protect.
ar: The Backward-Footed Trickster / The Forest's Conservation Guardian
cb: The logging company / the commercial hunter (the industrial-scale violation of the forest ethic) counterbalances Curupira (the individual scale forest guardian whose tricks work on humans, not corporations). Also Iara (the water spirit of seduction) counterbalances Curupira (the forest spirit of confusion) — attraction vs disorientation as the two great forest strategies.

do: The sacred nature spirits and deities of the Philippines — guardians of forests, mountains, rivers and seas, the animist intelligences of the Philippine archipelago who bring blessing or curse in proportion to the respect shown to the natural world
at: Diwata (from Sanskrit devata — deity, through Indonesian dewata) are the foundational sacred beings of pre-colonial Philippine spirituality. The word encompasses gods and goddesses, nature spirits, ancestral deities and elemental intelligences — a broad term comparable to the Japanese 'kami.' They live in ancient trees (especially the sacred balete and acacia), mountains, rivers, and the sea. Their most famous individual is Maria Makiling, the Diwata of Mount Makiling in Laguna — a beautiful, kind but deeply private guardian of the mountain who punishes those who harm her domain. The colonial Spanish systematically reduced the meaning of Diwata from 'deity' to 'fairy' or 'enchantress' in Tagalog regions, but in non-colonised areas of the Philippines (Visayas, Mindanao) the full divine meaning was preserved. The babaylan (female spirit mediums) and katalonan (male spirit mediums) communicated with the Diwata through ritual possession. Philippine cosmology describes seven layers of the universe, with Diwata inhabiting multiple levels including underwater kingdoms filled with sea fairies.
of: Offerings at the base of old trees before cutting (never cutting without asking permission), rice wine (tapuy) poured at forest boundaries, specific rituals conducted by the babaylan before entering sacred mountains or rivers, flowers placed in rivers, the pagdiwata ritual (singing and dancing to honour the Diwata)
an: The Philippine eagle (the sovereign of the sky), the crocodile (river guardian), the balete tree (which IS a Diwata's home), the tarsier
co: Gold (divine origin), deep green (the forest), blue (the ocean Diwata)
dp: All planetary correspondences — Diwata exist at all levels of the Philippine cosmos simultaneously
cr: Philippine jade (nephrite), coral, pearls from Philippine waters, gold
el: All elements — Diwata govern forests (Earth), rivers (Water), mountains (Fire and Air) and the sea (Water and Ether) simultaneously
bp: The entire body (they created the human body according to some Philippine creation myths), the immune system (they govern whether blessings or illnesses reach the community)
af: I walk in the land of the Diwata. Every old tree is a home. Every river is a corridor. Every mountain is a temple. I am not in nature — I am inside a living intelligence.
sh: The colonial diminishment — the Spanish made the Filipino gods into fairies. Diwata's shadow is the deity reduced to an entertainment, the nature god demoted to a fairy tale.
ar: The Philippine Nature Deities / The Animist Intelligences of the Archipelago
cb: The aswang (the dark supernatural beings of Philippine lore — the negative inversion of the Diwata's protective nature) counterbalances the Diwata. Also the Catholic saints (who the Spanish used to replace the Diwata — and who now often share feast days with them in Filipino Catholic-animist syncretism) counterbalance the Diwata.

do: The guardian Diwata of Mount Makiling — the most beloved nature spirit in Philippine folklore, kind and generous to the respectful, silent and wounded at human ingratitude, the embodiment of nature's love and nature's grief
at: Maria Makiling is the most famous and beloved Diwata in the Philippines — the guardian of Mount Makiling, an active volcano and biodiversity hotspot in Laguna Province, now a UNESCO-protected natural heritage site. She is described as a beautiful woman of extraordinary kindness who helps lost travellers, heals the sick, and guides fishermen to good waters. She falls in love with human men but is always abandoned or betrayed, and with each betrayal the mountain grows less generous: medicinal plants become harder to find, the forest yields less to hunters, the springs dry up. Her grief is the mountain's grief. She is the living expression of the Philippine ecological relationship: the mountain gives in proportion to the love shown to it. When she is honoured, the mountain is abundant. When she is abandoned, the mountain mourns. Her story has been told and retold by Filipino writers from Jose Rizal to modern authors — she is the defining nature spirit of Philippine literary consciousness.
of: Offerings left at the foot of Mount Makiling, respect shown before entering the mountain, never taking medicinal plants without asking permission, the pagdiwata ritual of song and offering, flowers placed at forest streams
an: The Philippine eagle (sovereign of Makiling), the firefly (guides the lost through her forest at night), all endemic species of the mountain
co: White (her purity), deep forest green, gold (her divine nature)
dp: Friday / Venus and Monday / Moon — the loving, generous, heartbroken feminine divine
cr: Philippine jade, clear quartz (the mountain springs), green tourmaline (the forest's vitality)
el: Earth (the mountain) and Water (the springs that flow from it)
bp: The heart (her generosity and her repeated heartbreak), the hands (the healing she offers to the sick), the tears (which become the mountain's streams)
af: The mountain does not withhold out of cruelty. It withholds out of sorrow. If the forest is no longer generous, I must ask — not what the forest owes me, but what I have taken without honouring.
sh: The human lover who abandons Maria Makiling — the one who takes the mountain's gifts (health, food, shelter, beauty) and then returns to ordinary life as if none of it were alive. Her abandonment is the myth of every environment being destroyed by those it sustained.
ar: The Beloved Guardian / The Heartbroken Mountain Spirit
cb: The coloniser / the developer (who does not ask permission of the mountain, who takes without honouring) directly counterbalances Maria Makiling. Also the male lovers who abandon her (representing human inconstancy toward nature) counterbalance her enduring faithfulness to the mountain.

do: The divine serpent beings of water and the deep earth — the nāga are among the most universal sacred beings in all of Asia, governing rain, rivers, fertility, wisdom, the underworld and the threshold between the human and the divine
at: The Nāga (Sanskrit: serpent) are among the most widely distributed sacred beings in human civilisation, present in Hindu, Buddhist, Jain, Cambodian, Thai, Lao, Burmese, Vietnamese, Indonesian, Filipino and countless other traditions. They are cosmic serpents — sometimes with human heads and upper bodies and serpentine lower bodies, sometimes as pure massive snakes — who live in the underground water realm (Pātāla or Nāgaloka) and in rivers, lakes and oceans. They are simultaneously dangerous and divine: they govern rain (without their cooperation, no rain falls), river fertility, and the deep wisdom of the earth. In Buddhist tradition, the Nāga King Mucalinda protected the meditating Gautama Buddha from a storm by coiling around him and raising his hood as a shelter — one of the most iconic images in Buddhist art. In Hindu tradition, Shesha (Ananta) is the cosmic serpent upon whose body Vishnu rests between the cycles of creation. In Cambodia, Thailand and Laos, the Nāga are visible in every temple — the multi-headed serpent forms on staircases representing the bridge between the human and divine worlds.
of: Milk poured into anthills (Nāga homes), five-hooded serpent images venerated at temples, rivers honoured before crossing, the Nāga Panchami festival where milk is offered to snakes, specific trees (like the ashvattha/peepal) where Nāga are believed to live
an: The serpent/cobra (their own form), the elephant (who must ask the Nāga's permission before drinking from sacred pools), the eagle Garuda (their cosmic enemy — the tension between sky and water)
co: Deep blue and gold (their royal nature), green (the water vegetation they govern), white (the white cobra's holiness)
dp: Monday / Moon and Pluto/Saturn — the deep water, the subterranean wisdom, the keeper of accumulated rain
cr: Serpentine, green jade, blue-green fluorite, the naturally occurring serpentine stones found near rivers
el: Water and Earth — the underground water world that they govern
bp: The spine (the serpentine kundalini energy — nāga IS the root of the word for the sacred energy in the spine), the lymphatic system, the kidneys (water organs)
af: Below every river, below every lake, below every mountain spring — there is a palace. The Nāga receive every drop of water that falls from the sky before it rises again. I honour what I cannot see.
sh: The Nāga's anger — droughts, floods, and geological events are attributed to disturbed Nāga. Their shadow is the sacred ecology disrupted: the river poisoned, the well polluted, the forest stripped — the serpent turning its poison inward on the destroyers.
ar: The Cosmic Serpent Waters / The Divine Underground River Beings
cb: Garuda (the solar eagle who is the Nāga's eternal cosmic enemy — sky vs water, eagle vs serpent) is the supreme counterbalance to the Nāga. Also Indra (the rain god who must negotiate with the Nāga for the water to be released) counterbalances the Nāga (who hold all underground water until they choose to release it).

do: The amphibious river spirits of Japan — the kappa are tortoise-shelled, cucumber-loving, sumo-wrestling water children of enormous cunning and equal mischief, who can drown, heal or play depending entirely on whether they are respected
at: The Kappa (河童 — 'river child') are among the most iconic supernatural beings in Japanese folklore — small, humanoid water creatures with the shell of a tortoise, the beak of a bird, a plate of water balanced on the top of their head (which must remain full or they lose their power), webbed hands and feet, and greenish scaly skin. They are deeply complex: they can drown children and horses (dangerous), they love sumo wrestling and cucumber (benign and charming), they can be bested in a formal bow (they bow back and spill the water from their head plate, becoming helpless), they are meticulous about promises once made (honourable), and those who form a relationship with a kappa receive miraculous medical knowledge from them. They represent the ambivalent nature of all water: dangerous and life-giving simultaneously. The kappa are related to the Suijin (water gods) and are often considered their messengers or servants. In traditional Japanese river communities, kappa warnings governed children's behavior near water — a sophisticated system of water safety encoded in mythological form.
of: Cucumbers (their favourite food — Japanese families would carve their family name into cucumbers and throw them into rivers before swimming to gain safe passage), formal bowing at riverside, never polluting a river, sumo wrestling contests offered as tribute
an: The turtle (their shell), the frog (their webbed feet and river home), the otter (which some scholars see as the kappa's animal inspiration)
co: Blue-green (their scaly skin colour), brown-green (river water, their element)
dp: Monday / Moon — the river child, the tidal water's playful dangerous spirit
cr: Moss agate, green fluorite, blue-green tourmaline, river stones
el: Water — specifically fresh water rivers and ponds
bp: The top of the head (the water plate — the kappa's power source), the shoulder joint (they can remove and reattach limbs — and in some stories help heal humans with the same ability), the hands (sumo holds and cucumber-grasping)
af: The river demands courtesy. If I bow at the river's edge and it bows back — and we both acknowledge the spillage between us — we can be companions rather than predator and prey.
sh: The kappa who takes children — the warning encoded in every kappa story: unsupervised children near rivers are in danger, and the danger has a face, a personality and a preference for cucumbers
ar: The Amphibious River Child / The Cunning Water Trickster of Japan
cb: Tengu (the mountain air spirits) counterbalance the Kappa (the water river spirits) — the two great elemental mischief-makers of Japanese folklore. Also the Suijin (the proper river gods) counterbalance the Kappa (the playful river children who are not quite gods and not quite monsters).

do: The elemental water beings of European alchemy and occultism — the pure intelligence of water in its most refined form, the feminine elemental principle governing emotion, intuition, dreams and the hidden currents of feeling
at: Undines were defined by the Swiss-German alchemist Paracelsus (1493–1541) as the elemental beings of water — equivalent to the Gnomes (earth), Sylphs (air) and Salamanders (fire). They inhabit all bodies of water and govern the water element in all its expressions: rivers, oceans, rain, springs, snow and the water within the human body. They are depicted as beautiful feminine beings, sometimes fully human, sometimes mermaid-form. Unlike the folklore mermaids (who are often morally ambiguous or dangerous), the Undine in the Paracelsian tradition is a being of pure elemental intelligence — not quite human, not quite divine, but the personification of water's inherent properties: flowing, yielding, reflecting, penetrating, dissolving and cleansing. The Romantic-era novella 'Undine' by Friedrich de la Motte Fouqué (1811) gave the elemental a soul through love and marriage — she gains the immortal soul that all elementals lack through the capacity to love and be loved. In Theosophy and Anthroposophy, Undines were further developed as the beings who carry feeling and emotion through the water of the world.
of: Water left in moonlight (moon-charged water for Undine work), crystal bowls of water placed as altars, offerings poured into rivers, the cultivation of emotional honesty (their greatest gift to humans)
an: Fish, the dolphin, the swan, water birds
co: Sea blue, aquamarine, deep teal, the silver of moonlit water
dp: Monday / Moon and Friday / Venus — the water element's dual planetary governance
cr: Aquamarine, blue topaz, moonstone, blue lace agate, selenite
el: Water — the pure elemental water force in all its expressions
bp: The kidneys and bladder (water organs), the lymphatic system (the body's inner river), the emotional body (the Undine's domain of influence within the human being)
af: Water finds the lowest point. It does not fight gravity — it uses it. My deepest feeling is not my weakness. It is the intelligence that knows which way the world is actually flowing.
sh: The Undine without a soul — the being of pure feeling without the reflective capacity that love brings. Emotion that flows without direction, that dissolves without nourishing.
ar: The Water Elemental / The Intelligence of Feeling and Flow
cb: Salamander (the fire elemental) is the direct elemental counterbalance to the Undine (water). Also the Gnome (earth elemental) counterbalances the Undine — form and feeling, stability and flow.

do: The water nymph spirits of Slavic rivers and forests — the spirits of women who died young, by drowning, in childbirth or betrayed in love, who return as dangerously beautiful beings that govern water, fertility and the week of Rusalki
at: The Rusalka (plural: Rusalki or Rusalky) are among the most complex water spirits in European folklore. They are the spirits of young women who died before their time — drowned, killed, died in childbirth, or betrayed in love — who return to inhabit rivers, lakes and forests. They are simultaneously the source of life (the rain that feeds crops comes from their dances — during Rusalki week in early summer, the Rusalki leave the water and dance in the fields, and their dancing causes the grain to grow) and the source of death (they can tickle men to death, drag them into the water, cause madness). They are depicted as beautiful, pale young women with long flowing hair, often green-tinged or wet. During Rusalki week (the week before Pentecost in Orthodox tradition) they are particularly active and potentially dangerous. The Rusalki tradition represents the complex Slavic understanding of the female dead as continuing to participate in the life of the living world — not gone but transformed into something simultaneously more and less than human.
of: Offerings thrown into rivers during Rusalki week, the prohibition on swimming during certain periods (to not disturb them), songs sung at the river's edge during Rusalki week, linen and cloth offerings left on tree branches at the river bank
an: The willow tree (their home on the river bank), the cuckoo (whose call signals their arrival in spring), fish of the rivers
co: Green (their wet hair, the algae of the river), white (the pale death-colour), the deep blue-green of a river at dusk
dp: Monday / Moon and Friday / Venus — the feminine dead, the cycle of water and life
cr: Moonstone (the pale moon their skin resembles), green tourmaline (the river's colour), blue apatite, aquamarine
el: Water and Air — the river and the fields they dance through
bp: The skin (pale, cold, wet — the death-touched beauty), the hands (that tickle to death), the feet (that dance the grain to life)
af: The women who died before their time did not disappear. They became the rain. They became the dance that makes the grain grow. The unfinished life does not end — it transforms into something that feeds the living.
sh: The death-tickle — the Rusalka's shadow is the spirit who cannot distinguish between the living and the dead, who pulls the living down to join her because she is lonely in her unfinished state
ar: The Spirit of the Untimely Dead / The Water Dancer Who Feeds the Grain
cb: The Kikimora (the domestic spirit, the night weaver — a female spirit of a very different register: the house rather than the river) counterbalances the Rusalka (the wild water spirit). Also the spring grain goddess Yarila (or the masculine agricultural gods) counterbalances the Rusalki (who must be appeased so that the grain they dance over will grow).

do: The seal people of the North Atlantic — the seals who shed their skins to walk as humans, the most heartsick beings in all of world mythology, the eternal story of a love that crosses between worlds and the grief of a self that has been stolen
at: The Selkie (from Scottish Gaelic 'selch' or 'silkie' — seal) are among the most emotionally complex beings in world folklore. They are seals in the ocean and humans on land — their seal skin is their identity, their freedom, and their connection to the ocean world. When a human (almost always a man) steals a female Selkie's seal skin, she cannot return to the sea and must live as his wife. She is a perfect wife, a gentle mother — but she never stops looking for her skin. When her children (or she herself) finally find the hidden skin, she returns to the sea without hesitation, regardless of love for her human children. This is not cruelty — it is the Selkie's most essential truth: some beings belong to the sea, and no human love, however genuine, can change what a being fundamentally is. The Selkie myth appears across all North Atlantic cultures independently, and its emotional power is undiminished over centuries. The Selkie represents every being who has been taken from their natural world and asked to live as something they are not.
of: Fish left on the shoreline for the seals, never hunting seals (a taboo in many North Atlantic communities), songs sung at the tide line at dusk, the practice of looking for the seal-eyes in the ocean (believed to be the gaze of Selkie kin)
an: The grey seal (their most common form — the seal whose eyes are said to be most human), all seals of the North Atlantic
co: Grey and silver (the seal's colour), deep ocean blue, the green-black of North Atlantic water
dp: Monday / Moon — the tidal being, the creature of the eternal pull between land and sea
cr: Grey moonstone, labradorite (the sea's iridescence), aquamarine, grey pearl
el: Water — the cold North Atlantic Ocean
bp: The skin (the seal skin IS the Selkie's identity — its theft is the theft of self), the eyes (described as deeply human, full of sorrow and recognition), the hands (those who have Selkie ancestry are said to have webbed fingers)
af: I know what I am. My skin is not merely clothing. It is the body that belongs to the sea. No love, however deep and true, requires me to never again be what I truly am.
sh: The stolen skin — the Selkie kept from the sea by someone who loves her and believes that love justifies the theft. The shadow is the love that becomes imprisonment: I kept it because I loved you.
ar: The Seal Person / The Identity Stolen and Reclaimed
cb: The human husband (who believes his love justifies the theft of her freedom) counterbalances the Selkie (who cannot be possessed regardless of love). Also the land itself (the human world, with its warmth and children and domesticity) counterbalances the cold ocean (the Selkie's true home) — the most beautiful love story that cannot end well.
